name: accept-feedback-as-input-not-imprint
description: You received feedback that feels unfair, incomplete, or based on a fundamental misreading of you, and you find yourself investing energy in changing how the other person sees you, rehearsing arguments, or feeling that the verdict is final. The chapter prescribes accepting what you cannot control and framing others' views as input rather than imprint.

sections_html.when:
You received feedback that you believe is wrong, unfair, incomplete, or based on misattribution (they're projecting their own flaw onto you). You find yourself:

  • Rehearsing arguments you plan to make to change the person's mind
  • Feeling that their view of you now determines your identity
  • Spending significant emotional energy on a verdict you had no part in creating
  • Unable to move past the feedback because you cannot accept that the person continues to hold the view
sections_html.move:
Accept what you cannot control. Others' views of you are one thing you cannot change, no matter how wrong and unfair they may be. You can discuss, offer counterexamples, provide supporting evidence. But you cannot make someone think something different about you. The chapter: "Maybe she will and maybe she won't." The energy spent trying to force a change in how someone sees you is drawn from a finite supply. Spending it on an uncontrollable variable leaves less for what you can control: your own assessment of the feedback, your decisions about what to change, and your actual behavior.

Note: most people are not actually thinking about you. The chapter's calibration: "Most people are simply too obsessed with themselves to be obsessed with you." Your ex-spouse called you a pathetic excuse for a human being and may still hold that view. But she is not sitting at home dwelling on it. She is watching television. The amount of emotional space the other person's view of you takes up in their mind is usually far smaller than it takes up in yours.

Receive the view, do not wholesale adopt it. Others' views of you may be incomplete, outdated, unfair, or simply wrong. They may tell you something accurate about the person holding the view rather than about you. The chapter: "most annoying, they may be claiming something about you that is actually true only about them." At the same time, their view is not entirely dismissible. It may contain something worth examining. The chapter's formulation: "Don't dismiss others' views of you, but don't accept them wholesale either. Their views are input, not imprint."

Have compassion for them. This is not a technique for suppressing justified anger. It is a practical mechanism for loosening the grip of an unfair verdict. When someone levels an attack or withholds approval in a way that has a long history, remind yourself what their own history must look like: the wound they are operating from, the patterns they learned before you appeared. This does not excuse behavior. It de-personalizes it just enough that you stop treating their view as an authoritative judgment.
sections_html.signal: You have stopped rehearsing the argument you were going to make to change their mind. You can hold the view they expressed (whether fair or unfair) as one data point rather than as a verdict. You can examine what in their view is worth taking seriously without taking it wholesale. The statement "their view is input, not imprint" feels accurate rather than like a coping consolation.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "how much time in the last twenty-four hours have I spent thinking about how this person sees me, versus thinking about what I want to do with the feedback's content?" If the ratio is heavily weighted toward managing their view of you, this skill applies.

After: ask "am I still trying to make them see me differently, or have I moved to asking what I want to do with what they said?" If the latter, the skill has shifted your focus to the controllable.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, the receiver becomes obsessed with a verdict that is, in part, outside their authorship and entirely outside their control. The obsession consumes emotional energy without producing learning or change. The chapter's framing: you can offer notarized statements from your therapist or your dad or the pope, and you still cannot make her think something different. Every attempt to force the change keeps you anchored to their view as if it were the definitive truth about you. The "input not imprint" framing is what restores the distinction between a piece of evidence (which you can weigh, contextualize, and decide about) and a tattoo (which is permanent and defines you).
sections_html.example: The chapter uses the football case: you watch football for the chesslike strategy; your coworker insists it's your adolescent compensation for insecure masculinity. You believe you know your own reasons better than she does. You can offer evidence, but you cannot make her think something different. The chapter's prescription: let it go. You know why you watch football. Her belief about your reasons is her belief. The relevant question is whether there is anything in her view worth examining (is there anything in how you discuss football that a reasonable observer might read as compensatory?). If not, the view is input that has been examined and set aside. That is not the same as dismissing it unseen.
sections_html.source: Chapter 8, "Dismantle Distortions," lines 3907-3918 (accepting you can't control how others see you: the two-way-street explanation, the football case, the calibration about how little others think about you), 3924-3931 (have compassion for them: de-personalization as a practical tool, "their views are input, not imprint"). Stone and Heen call this the fifth and final distortion-dismantling strategy.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: adopt-growth-mindset-for-feedback
description: Feedback feels like a verdict on a fixed trait rather than information about where you are on a learning curve. The chapter draws on Dweck's research to show how holding traits as growable versus fixed changes how you hear feedback and what you do with it.

sources.chapter: 9. Cultivate a Growth Identity (Sort Toward Coaching)

sources.external: Carol S. Dweck, Mindset: The New Psychology of Success (2006)
sources.note: The canonical home of the fixed/growth distinction Stone and Heen draw on throughout the chapter. Dweck's book supplies what the chapter compresses: specific case studies across domains (sports, business, relationships, education), detailed strategies for recognizing and shifting out of fixed-identity patterns in the moment, the neuroplasticity evidence for growth, and the developmental origins of the two mindsets (how praise for intelligence vs. effort installs them). A reader of this skill who wants the full procedural depth of how to shift from fixed to growth mindset in practice will find it in Dweck's chapters on 'The Growth Mindset in Action' and 'Developing a Growth Mindset.' The book also develops implications Heen and Stone do not: how fixed mindsets affect leadership and relationships, and how the mindset can be recognized in others. Sharpens because the skill covers the feedback-specific application; Dweck's book covers the broader framework and its cultivation.

sections_html.when: You received feedback that touches on an ability, skill, or trait you have, and your immediate internal response is one of two things: pride that a fixed trait has been confirmed, or shame that a fixed trait has been revealed as insufficient. You are treating the feedback as a verdict on what you fundamentally are, rather than as information about where you currently are. You are living in the "Testing Room."
sections_html.move:
Ask whether you are currently treating the trait or ability the feedback addresses as fixed or as growable.

The fixed-mindset framing treats traits as inherent possessions: you have a certain amount of them the way a molecule has a certain number of atoms. Under this framing, feedback is always a rating of the fixed quantity. When the puzzles get harder, the feedback whispers: "Not enough puzzle smarts. You are not up to this task." Fixed-mindset people give up when struggling, because struggling with a fixed trait means you have been measured and found insufficient. They do fine when things are easy; they fall apart when challenged. The EEG data from Mangels and Good: fixed-mindset people pay close attention to whether they got the answer right or wrong, but stop listening when told what the correct answer was. They want the verdict; they have no appetite for the coaching.

The growth-mindset framing treats traits as flexible capacities that grow with attention, effort, and coaching. Under this framing, feedback is information about where you are on a learning curve. Struggling with a hard puzzle means you are in the exact challenge you need to improve. Growth-mindset people say after setbacks: "I'll study differently next time." Fixed-mindset people say: "I feel dumb; I'll study less; or I'll cheat." The chapter: "Not only weren't they discouraged by failure, they didn't even think they were failing. They thought they were learning."

Check which room you're in. Ask: "Am I treating this feedback as a rating of what I have, or as information about what I could develop?" The chapter calls these the "Testing Room" and the "Learning Room." Both rooms exist; you don't choose which feedback you receive. But you do choose, to a significant degree, which room you process it in.

Shift toward the Learning Room. The chapter's practical instruction: if you are uncertain whether a trait is capable of growth, that uncertainty is not a reason to assume it isn't. Run the experiment. Find a coach. Try things you are not good at and make a list of three ways you could do better after each failure. This is not blind optimism; it is empirically grounded. The chapter: "People do get better when they apply themselves, and people apply themselves when they believe they can get better." The traits that matter most in life (intelligence, leadership, compassion, creativity, self-awareness) grow with attention and coaching.

Note: growth mindset does not eliminate setbacks. A growth identity is not about getting good feedback. It is about how you hold whatever feedback you get. You may work hard and still receive a disappointing result. The chapter: "You'd hoped you were farther along this learning curve than you apparently are. Your payoff for effort is smaller than you'd hoped it might be. A growth identity is not about whether you get terrific or troubling feedback. It's about how you hold whatever you get."
sections_html.signal: When the next piece of difficult feedback arrives, your internal question changes from "does this prove I have enough [trait]?" to "where am I on the learning curve for [trait], and what does this tell me about how to develop further?" You are curious about what the right answer was, not just whether you got this one right.
sections_html.probe:
Before: after receiving feedback, ask "what did I most want to know: whether I was right/successful, or what I could do differently?" If the first, the fixed framing is running.

After: ask "when I reread this feedback, is my primary reaction rating (I passed or failed) or learning (this tells me something about where to focus next)?" If the latter, the growth framing is available.
sections_html.failure: Under a fixed mindset, feedback that reveals a shortfall has no constructive use. Because the trait is fixed, there is nothing to do with the information except grieve or deny. This produces two downstream failures: people with fixed mindsets become less accurate about their own capabilities (confused by data that varies around their fixed self-image), and they drop out earlier after setbacks because struggle is confirmation of fixed insufficiency rather than signal to redouble effort. The chapter notes that fixed-mindset people after failure are more likely to lie about their performance and consider cheating. These are the consequences of treating a correctable limitation as a permanent verdict.
sections_html.example: Dweck's puzzles study: children with fixed mindsets perform fine on easy puzzles and fall apart when the puzzles get hard, because hard puzzles reveal insufficient fixed ability. Children with growth mindsets say, "I was hoping this would be informative!" as the puzzles get harder. They are in the Learning Room; the hard puzzle is exactly the challenge they need. The EEG study at Columbia provides behavioral evidence: growth-mindset students listen closely to what the right answers were; fixed-mindset students lose interest once they know whether they got it right. The feedback structure differs: one group asks "how did I do?" and shuts down; the other asks "how can I do better?" and opens up.
sections_html.source: Chapter 9, "Cultivate a Growth Identity," lines 4195-4228 (the Testing Room/Learning Room distinction, Dweck's puzzles research), 4221-4245 (fixed vs. growth assumptions, the EEG Columbia study, the "aren't some traits fixed?" section), 4251-4321 (implications for self-perception accuracy, how we listen to feedback, self-fulfilling prophecies after struggle, the fifth-graders framing study, and the move toward the Learning Room). Stone and Heen frame this as the second of two shifts toward a robust identity, drawing on Dweck's work.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/embrace-identity-nuance — the companion shift; this skill addresses whether traits are fixed or growable; identity-nuance addresses whether the label is binary or complex
  • feedback/sort-toward-coaching — the behavioral practice that implements the growth mindset in a specific feedback conversation

name: align-on-conversation-purpose
description: A feedback conversation is about to begin (or has already started without any framing), and you are not certain what kind of feedback is being offered, who makes the final call, or whether the outcome is still open to influence. The chapter prescribes getting aligned on these three questions before engaging with content.

sections_html.when:
You are at the beginning of a feedback conversation, either scheduled or spontaneous, and you do not yet know: (a) whether what is about to happen is feedback and if so what kind, (b) who the decision-maker is, or (c) whether the feedback outcome is final or still open to influence. Jumping into the content without this alignment leads to arguments that resolve the wrong problem.

Also reach for this when the giver has launched into the middle of a conversation without any framing, and the conversation has already started to go sideways.
sections_html.move:
Ask three orienting questions before engaging with content:

  1. Is this feedback? If so, what kind? Some things that look like feedback are not. Not being put on the project team could be a resource allocation decision or it could be feedback; the birthday sweater that is a size too small could be a mistake or a message. If it is feedback, identify whether it is coaching, evaluation, or appreciation. Also ask: what kind of feedback would be most useful to you right now? If you need encouragement rather than critique, say so before the conversation unfolds. And ask yourself what the giver's purpose is: they may be offering coaching when you need evaluation, or offering evaluation when you need coaching.
  1. Who decides? Some feedback conversations require you to reach consensus; others have a clear decision-maker. Know which. The chicken logo for the Chicken Farmers' Convention: at the impasse between designer and client, the question of who gets the final call changes everything. Also: some conversations do not require agreement at all. If your ex-partner gives you feedback that you were a terrible person and you believe you acted thoughtfully, you do not need to reach consensus. You are each your own storyteller about what happened.
  1. Is this final or negotiable? If a performance rating is already final, spending your time trying to influence it wastes energy that would be better spent understanding it and discussing what to do next. If it is provisional, you may be able to affect the outcome. Determine which before deciding how to engage.

You can influence the frame and agenda regardless of how the giver starts. You do not have to simply react to whoever fires first. The chapter: "Regardless of how the other person starts, you can use your turn to frame the conversation constructively and to offer an agenda." If the giver launches into accusations, reframe: "I want to hear your perspective on this, and then I'll share my view, and we can figure out where and why our views are different." If they are in a "I'm right" frame: "Can we take a minute to step back so I'm clear on our purposes?" This is a process move about the conversation's structure, not about the substance being discussed. The chapter cites Gottman's finding that if the first three minutes of a fifteen-minute conversation are harsh and not corrected, the outcome is negative 96 percent of the time. Getting the opening right pays compound interest.
sections_html.signal: Before the content discussion begins, you and the giver both know what kind of feedback is being given, who is making decisions, and what can and cannot be changed. The chapter calls this getting aligned.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do I know what kind of feedback this is, whether I can influence the outcome, and who makes the final call?" If you cannot answer all three, the alignment has not happened.

After: ask "did I engage with the content before getting aligned on purpose and status?" If yes, you may have argued about the substance when you should have first clarified what kind of conversation you were in.
sections_html.failure: Without alignment at the opening, the conversation often treats as contentious what could have been settled in advance. You spend energy fighting over a decision that is already final (if you had known, you would have focused on understanding it, not changing it). Or you accept coaching passively when you needed evaluation first (because you never named what you needed). Or you argue about who is right when no one needs to agree at all. The chapter's Gottman finding makes the cost explicit: a harsh opening that is not corrected predicts a negative outcome 96 percent of the time.
sections_html.example: The performance rating conversation (the "version four" worked example in the chapter): the receiver opens by flagging what they know (they got a 4, they were told last year that customers would differentiate a 4 from a 5, they have specific data) and what they want to learn (the actual criteria, whether anything has changed). This is alignment framing done as assertion: naming the structure of the conversation you want to have before diving into the content. The receiver does not ask whether the rating can be changed until they have understood the process.
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5006-5017 (the open-body-close arc), 5023-5073 (the three alignment questions: is this feedback/what kind, who decides, is it final or negotiable), 5078-5087 (you can influence the frame and agenda; the repair-attempts research; process not substance). Stone and Heen frame these as the critical opening work before the body of the conversation.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/make-a-process-move — the general-purpose version of course-correcting a conversation's process; align-on-conversation-purpose is the specific application at the opening
  • feedback/close-with-commitment — the bookend; opening alignment and closing commitment bracket the body of the conversation

name: ask-for-one-thing-and-find-the-theme
description: You want feedback but the question "any feedback for me?" produces nothing useful, or you are overwhelmed by too many things at once. The chapter offers three techniques for soliciting focused, actionable feedback rather than a general assessment.

sources.chapter: 12. Get Going (Five Ways to Take Action)

sections_html.when: You want to improve but you are either getting no feedback, getting vague or label-heavy feedback (below the mean on "engagement"), or getting so much feedback that you cannot act on any of it. Willpower to change is a limited resource: too many changes drain it without producing any. You need one useful thing to start on.
sections_html.move:
Ask for one thing. Do not say "give me some feedback" or "how am I doing?" Instead, ask: "What's one thing I could work on?" For more precision, use the chapter's specific phrasing: "What's one thing you see me doing, or failing to do, that's getting in my own way?" This question gives the giver permission to be candid, helps them prioritize rather than dump everything at once, and focuses the conversation on your behavior rather than on general assessment. If you receive ten concerns, acknowledge and validate them all, then set priorities: "I'm serious about improving, and it's been my experience that the best way for me to do that is to focus on one thing at a time. Let's figure out a good place to start."

Go to several people and listen for the theme. Rather than relying on a single source, go to three to five people who observe you in different roles and ask each the one-thing question. Look for what recurs. Rodrigo went to five people (three close collaborators, his boss, a coworker he found irritating) and discovered that three of five independently mentioned the same thing: he hung back in conversations when he should have weighed in with direction. Without the theme-finding pass, any single response could have been written off as idiosyncratic. With the pattern visible, the evidence was hard to dismiss.

Ask what matters to them. A variant of the one-thing question: "What's one thing I could change that would make a difference to you?" This centers the giver's actual concern rather than your self-improvement agenda. Sharon asked this at dinner and received Colin's answer: not Skittles, not anything abstract, but bowling, because that was the only time all four of them did anything together. Sharon had not noticed. The question surfaces the specific concern hiding behind vague or absent feedback.
sections_html.signal: You have identified one specific improvement target, not a list. You have checked whether it appears across multiple sources (theme-confirmed) or only from one (still worth considering but less certain). If you used the "what would make a difference to you" variant, you know what the giver's real concern is.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do I have one specific thing I am actively working on right now?" If not, the one-thing question needs to be asked.

After: ask "did I hear the same theme from more than one person, or just from one?" If from one, treat it as a hypothesis. If from two or more, treat it as evidence.
sections_html.failure: Asking "any feedback?" produces either silence (too broad, no permission to be candid) or a comprehensive audit (too much to act on). Receiving a comprehensive audit without prioritizing leads to the chapter's cookie-and-radish finding: trying to attend to too many changes simultaneously exhausts willpower without producing improvement in any one area. And relying on one person's view risks acting on idiosyncratic data. The theme-finding pass distinguishes real patterns from one person's particular complaint.
sections_html.source: Chapter 12, "Get Going," lines 5666-5693 (name one thing: the cookie-and-radish research, the specific phrasing, and how to handle a ten-concern giver), 5698-5719 (listen for themes: Rodrigo's five-person pass and the three-of-five convergence on hanging back), 5725-5742 (ask what matters to them: Sharon and Colin's bowling conversation).
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/ask-how-am-i-getting-in-my-own-way — the chapter-6 version of this question in the context of soliciting blind-spot feedback; this skill's three-technique approach extends that to theme-finding and the "what would make a difference to you" variant

name: ask-how-am-i-getting-in-my-own-way
description: You want to learn how you come across, ahead of an event or in an ongoing relationship, and you are about to ask "how am I doing?" or "any feedback for me?" The chapter says these questions return either nothing useful or appreciation, because they do not name what you actually want and they do not narrow the topic to anything answerable.

sections_html.when: You are about to solicit feedback from a colleague, a manager, a partner, a direct report, or a friend. Your phrasing is forming as something general: "Got any feedback for me?" "How do you think it's going?" "Anything I should be doing differently?" The other person is about to give you a polite answer that costs them nothing and gives you nothing. Reach for this before that exchange happens.
sections_html.move:
1. Replace the noncommittal question with the chapter's specific one. Ask: "What do you see me doing, or failing to do, that is getting in my own way?" Or close variants ("...that's getting in our way as a team," "...that's holding back my development on this project"). The phrasing is the move.
  2. Notice why each piece of the wording does work. "What do you see me doing" names behavior, observable to them and not to you. "Or failing to do" opens omissions, which are otherwise easy to miss. "Getting in my own way" names the kind of honesty you want (the unflattering kind) and lowers the social cost of saying it (you are framing the request as for-your-own-good, not as inviting attack). The whole question is narrower than "any feedback?" and easier to answer because the topic and the kind are pre-specified.
  3. Receive the first answer with curiosity. The chapter notes that the answer often comes timidly: "Well, on occasion I suppose that you sometimes..." If you respond with appreciation and follow-up curiosity ("say more, what does that look like?"), the giver will fill in the picture. If you respond with self-defense or a smile that says you'd rather not hear more, the conversation closes and you taught them not to answer honestly next time.
  4. Use the answer as data about your impact, not as a verdict. The skill is about eliciting; what you do with what comes back uses the other blind-spot skills (look-for-pattern-not-counterexample, separate-impact-from-intent, name-which-mirror-you-need for second opinions).
  5. If you actually want appreciation, ask for that instead. The chapter is honest that "any feedback?" is sometimes a covert request for appreciation, and the giver may correctly intuit that and supply appreciation. Naming the kind you want (per negotiate-which-kind-you-need) is the cleaner move; this skill is for the case where what you want is blind-spot information.
sections_html.signal: You walk away with described behavior or omission, with described impact, in the giver's words. Not "you're doing great" and not "fine." A test: you could write down two specific things the giver said you do, with one example each, and the impact each is having. If you cannot, the question did not land or the receive-side did not invite the follow-up.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to predict the most useful answer this person could give me, can I picture what shape that answer takes?" If the answer in your head is "they'll tell me I'm doing fine, generally," your question has already collapsed it. The shape you should be picturing is "described behavior, with described impact, in their words."

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can replay the answer and identify behavior and impact in it. If the answer was "you're great" or "I can't really think of anything," the question failed; either you asked the wrong version, or you visibly preferred reassurance and the giver gave you what you preferred.
sections_html.failure: Without the specific question, the eliciting move runs into two failures the chapter names. In the first, you ask "how am I doing?" and your giver, sensing no real invitation to honesty, gives you appreciation. You walk away comforted and unenlightened. The blind spot is no closer to closing. In the second, your giver tries to be honest but does not know what you want feedback on (this project? our relationship? your leadership? your life?) and produces something so general that you can't act on it. The chapter's analogy: "how was your day?" returns "fine" from a nine-year-old. You taught them, by asking the empty question, that empty answers were what you wanted. By the time you decide you wanted something more, the conversation is over and the next one will start the same way unless you change the question.
sections_html.example: The chapter contrasts the noncommittal phrasing ("how am I doing?", "do you have any feedback for me?") with its narrower replacement ("what do you see me doing, or failing to do, that is getting in my own way?") at lines 2018-2023, and walks through the receive-side of the answer: timidly at first, then more clearly if the asker responds with curiosity rather than defense. The chapter places this skill among the practical responses to the prior "you can't see yourself by looking harder" point: since the receiver cannot self-illuminate, the eliciting question must do the work of inviting a specific kind of answer, and the giver needs the topic narrowed before the honesty becomes possible. Annabelle's situation, where two 360s three years apart surfaced the same theme, is the larger frame: she did not have a way to ask the question that would have surfaced the leak earlier in any one relationship.
sections_html.source: Chapter 4, "See Your Blind Spots," lines 2018-2023, the section "Ask: How Do I Get in My Own Way?" Stone and Heen prescribe the specific phrasing as a replacement for the noncommittal default and explain why each component of the wording does work (names behavior, includes omissions, names the kind of honesty desired, narrows the topic). The skill installs the chapter's eliciting question.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: ask-what-would-help-you-change
description: The giver of feedback will not or cannot acknowledge their contribution to the dynamic, and raising the systems view directly reads as deflection. The chapter offers a move that names what the giver could do differently while framing it as being in service of the receiver's own change.

sources.chapter: 6. Identify the Relationship System (Take Three Steps Back)

sections_html.when: You have taken responsibility for your part. You have seen the broader system contributing to the problem. The giver, however, is not ready or able to look at their contribution. They are still stuck in the frame that this feedback exchange is entirely about what you need to change. Asking them directly to acknowledge their role has not worked, and pressing harder will derail the conversation.
sections_html.move:
Rather than trying to force the giver to acknowledge and take responsibility for their part in the problem, describe how they could get a better reaction from you. You are asking them to change, but you are framing it, legitimately, as a request that serves your change.

The structure is:

  1. Acknowledge your own pattern or reaction, including that you are working on it.
  2. Describe specifically what the giver's behavior does to that reaction.
  3. Name what they could do differently that would help you do what they are asking.

The chapter's worked script from Gil to Sandy: "I have the strongest reaction to being surprised, because it makes me panic about where else you might be spending and not telling me. I know I'm overreacting sometimes and I'm working on that. It would help me if you would be willing to be upfront with me about the Grape-Nuts and the mochaccino 'mini-vacations,' and we can budget for them together."

That script contains all three components: Gil names his reaction and owns that it is out of proportion (step one); he names what triggers the reaction, the surprise, not the spending itself (step two); he describes a specific change that would help him manage his reaction (step three). The framing is "help me change" rather than "you are doing something wrong."

The legitimacy condition. The chapter notes the framing is legitimate: the request is genuinely in service of the receiver's change, not just a tactic. If it is not genuine, meaning if you do not actually believe the giver's behavior contributes to your reaction, this move becomes manipulation. Use it only when the systems observation is honest.
sections_html.signal: The giver has heard a request from you that focuses on something they can do, framed as serving your change. They are no longer on the defensive about their contribution; instead, they are considering a concrete behavioral adjustment. The conversation has moved from "who is to blame" to "what would help."
sections_html.probe:
Before: check that you have already taken responsibility for your own part. This move does not replace owning your contribution; it is what you do when, after owning your contribution, the giver still cannot see theirs.

After: ask whether your request was specific and framed as "this helps me change" rather than "this is what you are doing wrong." If it was general or framed as critique, it will have been heard as message two from take-responsibility-first-then-introduce-the-system rather than as a request.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, when a giver is stuck in the "it's all about you" frame, the receiver's options narrow to: accept the feedback and remain silent about the system (full absorber stance), or push the systems point directly and have it heard as deflection. Neither option produces useful change. The "here's what would help me change" framing creates a third path that moves the conversation forward without requiring the giver to abandon their frame. The chapter frames it: "Rather than trying to force them to admit to and take responsibility for their part in the problem, describe how they could get a better reaction from you."
sections_html.example: The chapter uses Gil and Sandy as the illustration. Sandy's feedback to Gil is implicit throughout the chapter: stop obsessing, stop confronting, stop making every small purchase a crisis. Gil cannot easily respond "you're right and also your hidden-spending behavior is part of what triggers me," because Sandy will hear that as "so actually it's your fault." What Gil can say is the script above: name his reaction, own it, and ask for something Sandy can give that would help him manage it. The framing shifts the conversation from mutual accusation to a negotiation about what each person can do.
sections_html.source: Chapter 6, "Identify the Relationship System," lines 3154-3162, the section titled "Here's What Would Help Me Change." Stone and Heen present this as the receiver's move when the giver is not ready to look at their contribution, and frame it as a legitimate request rather than a manipulation. The skill captures the move and the legitimacy condition.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/take-responsibility-first-then-introduce-the-system — the prior step; this skill applies when even the sequenced version does not open the giver to looking at the system

name: assert-as-left-out
description: You are in a feedback conversation and you have something to say, but every version you try either comes out as an attack or as passive acceptance. The chapter prescribes a reframe from "I'm right" to "here's what's left out" that lets you advocate strongly without making the conversation a truth-fight.

sections_html.when: You are in a feedback conversation and you disagree with what the giver is saying, or you have information, feelings, or a perspective that has not been introduced. You want to say something that matters. But every version you rehearse either starts a fight ("that's wrong"), signals passive hostility ("it's fine, I guess"), or stays bottled up entirely.
sections_html.move:
Reframe the purpose of assertion. You are not asserting to convince the giver that you are right. You are adding what is left out. The chapter: "You're adding what's most often left out: your data, your interpretations, and your feelings." The feedback conversation is a puzzle both of you are assembling. The giver has some pieces; you have some pieces. When you don't assert, you withhold your pieces. The puzzle stays incomplete. With both sets on the table, you can see where you agree and disagree, and why.

Replace truth-assertions with difference-assertions. The chapter's key move:

  • Pitfall: "That advice is wrong."
  • Better: "I disagree with that advice."

"That advice is wrong" invites the giver to defend its correctness. "I disagree" is a fact about your view that they cannot argue with. What follows is the productive conversation: why do you see it differently? You can then add the data and context that led you to your interpretation. The chapter: "You can be assertive about your point of view even as you are aware that it's your point of view and not necessarily the entire story."

Know the three trigger-specific assertion pitfalls. Each type of trigger produces a characteristic assertion failure:

  • Truth trigger pitfall: asserting as objective fact ("that advice is wrong"), which puts the giver on the defensive about correctness. Fix: own your view as yours ("I disagree with that advice, because at my last job we had a different approach and had fewer problems").
  • Relationship trigger pitfall: switchtracking or blaming ("you're a self-centered jerk"). Fix: name both topics and give each its own track ("I'm feeling underappreciated, so it's hard for me to focus on your feedback. I think we need to discuss how I'm feeling and the feedback itself"). A related pitfall: asserting "this is not my fault" rather than your contribution alongside your observation about the system. Fix: "I agree there are things I've contributed. I'd also like to look at the bigger picture together, because there are other inputs that matter here."
  • Identity trigger pitfall: overdramatic acceptance when overwhelmed ("it's true, I'm hopeless"). Fix: be honest that you are overwhelmed rather than performing collapse ("I'm surprised by all this and it's a lot to take in. I want time to think about it. Let's come back tomorrow"). Inverse pitfall: asserting that the feedback cannot be true because you are not that kind of person ("that's ridiculous, I'm not that kind of person"). Fix: "That's upsetting to hear, because it's not how I see myself or who I want to be." You can signal that the information doesn't fit your self-image without claiming the information is wrong.
sections_html.signal: You have said something specific, in your own words, that the giver did not know before. Not "fine, I suppose" and not "you're wrong," but a piece of your data, interpretation, or feeling that adds to the puzzle. The giver now has a piece they did not have before, and neither of you is simply defending your original position.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "what is the thing I most want the giver to understand about my experience or view?" If the answer is "that they're wrong," you are still in truth-assertion mode. The move is to translate that into: "what do I know that they don't? What piece is missing?"

After: ask "did I say something specific about my own data, feelings, or interpretation that the giver could not have known before I said it?" If yes, the assertion has added a piece.
sections_html.failure: Without the "left out" reframe, assertion in feedback conversations escalates into truth fights, passive hostility, or silence. The giver says something; you think it's wrong; you say it's wrong; they say it's right; you repeat more emphatically that it's wrong. No new information enters the room. The chapter's version two of the performance rating conversation is the failure mode made explicit: "I think 4 is a little low, but I suppose it's fine" — unclear and passive-aggressive. The chapter's version one: "this is just unfair" — a truth assertion that triggers the giver's identity. Both failures share the same root: the receiver is either trying to win an argument about who is right or not saying anything real at all. "Here's what's left out" is neither.
sections_html.example: The chapter's version four of the performance rating conversation: the receiver says "I was surprised I got a 4 instead of a 5. I was thinking that, but as I reflect on it, I realize that wasn't based on very much information." This asserts the surprise and the expectation while holding them with appropriate epistemic tentativeness (I realize I may not have had enough information). Later: "Based on what you've just explained about the criteria, and assuming there were no other factors, I do think I should have received a 5. Do you see it differently?" This asserts a view (5 was warranted under the stated criteria) while opening it to the giver's perspective rather than declaring it a fact.
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5302-5316 (the reframe: adding what's left out rather than asserting truth; puzzle building), 5322-5388 (common assertion mistakes for truth, relationship, and identity triggers). Stone and Heen describe asserting as "a mix of sharing, advocating, and expressing" and specify that effective assertion does not mean asserting certainty.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/negotiate-your-internal-voice — the prior step; when the internal voice is running a bodyguard defense, assertion gets distorted into hot inquiry or passive hostility; negotiate the voice first, then assert cleanly
  • feedback/make-a-process-move — when assertion alone doesn't move the conversation, a process move steps outside the content to name what's stuck

name: catch-the-wrong-spotting
description: Feedback has just arrived and you can feel yourself building a list of objections, exceptions, or factual errors as you listen. Your goal in the conversation has quietly become to win the dismissal, not to understand what is actually being said. You are about to nod, file the mental refutation, and move on.

sections_html.when: Someone is giving you feedback (or has just given it), and you notice your attention has drifted from "what is this person saying" to "what is wrong with what this person is saying." You are mentally cataloguing inaccuracies, exceptions, outdated assumptions, missing context, the wrong people consulted, the wrong frame applied. The feedback may indeed have something wrong with it. Reach for this before you let that something become your reason for closing the case.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice that the question driving your attention has become "what is wrong with this feedback" rather than "what is this person saying." This is the chapter's named habit, wrong spotting, and the chapter's claim is that we are very good at it because there is almost always something to find: it is 2+2=5 wrong, or different-planet wrong, or used-to-be-right, or right according to the wrong people, or right for you but wrong for me, or right but unhelpful.
  2. Do not deny that the something-wrong exists. The chapter is explicit that wrong spotting works because feedback usually does have parts that are off, vague, or based on incomplete information. The move is not to pretend otherwise.
  3. Pause the verdict anyway. Separate two questions that you are about to collapse: "do I understand what this person is actually saying" and "is what they are saying correct." The chapter's claim is that the second cannot be answered well until the first has been. Your mind wants to skip the first because the second feels more urgent.
  4. Reroute attention to the first question. Concretely: stop building objections, start asking yourself what the giver actually means under their words. The next moves in the chapter (spot the label, separate data from interpretation, clarify where the feedback is going, difference spot) are what you do once attention is rerouted. This skill is the upstream switch that makes them possible.
  5. Reserve the right to disagree later. Understanding is not the same as agreeing, and the chapter is clear that you may end up disagreeing more clearly after you understand. The deferral of the verdict is what protects the understanding, not what concedes it.
sections_html.signal: You can name, in one sentence, what the giver is actually saying — not your refutation of it, not the part that is wrong with it, but the claim itself in your own paraphrase. Your follow-up move is a clarifying question, not a counter-argument. If you still want to disagree at the end of the conversation, you can articulate what you understood that you are disagreeing with, rather than dismissing on the strength of the something-wrong you spotted in the first thirty seconds.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If the giver were listening to my internal monologue right now, what would they say I was doing — listening to understand them, or listening to find the wrong part?" If the honest answer is the second, wrong spotting is running.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if the internal monologue has switched from cataloguing what is wrong to constructing the giver's view in their terms, well enough that you could explain it back to a third party who had not heard them.
sections_html.failure: Without the catch, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you spot a real wrong, latch onto it as the verdict on the whole, dismiss the rest of the feedback by association, and lose the parts that were not wrong. Wrong spotting, the chapter notes, defeats wrong feedback successfully but defeats learning along with it. In the second, you spot a smaller wrong, decide it would be petty to dismiss the whole over it, and so you swallow the whole feedback uncritically — the same skipped-the-understanding step, just collapsed in the other direction. Both failures share a structure: the verdict was reached before the content was understood. The receiver gives up the chance to learn from feedback that was partially right and partially wrong (which is most feedback) because the binary of right-or-wrong was applied before the content of the message was clear.
sections_html.example: The chapter opens with Holly, a public defender hearing from her supervisor Irwin that she gets "too enmeshed" with clients. Holly hears the feedback, decides it is wrong (she grew up on these streets, she knows what a fighter in your corner looks like), and quietly resolves not to act on it. Later the chapter walks through what Irwin actually meant — not "care less" but "watch the specific pattern of giving small amounts of cash to clients who need much more, because it costs you their professional trust" — and Holly's wrong-spot dismissal turns out to have been a dismissal of feedback she might have used. The chapter's wider catalogue of "kinds of wrong" (2+2=5, different-planet, used-to-be-right, etc.) is the diagnostic for catching yourself running this pattern in real time. Paul's preparation before talking with Monisha at the end of the chapter is the disposition installed: "his purpose in the conversation is not to accept or reject, but first to understand."
sections_html.source: Chapter 3, "First Understand," lines 1118-1147 (the wrong-spotting catalogue), 1153-1158 ("Understanding Is Job One"), and 1420-1429 (the explicit pivot to "tell me more"). Stone and Heen frame wrong spotting as the receiver's habitual entry into feedback and name the costs: it succeeds at filtering bad feedback and fails at filtering for learning. The skill installs the upstream catch before any of the chapter's downstream understanding moves can run.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: change-your-vantage-point
description: You are in the grip of a distorted story about feedback and you cannot think your way out of it from your current vantage point. The chapter offers three moves that change the perspective you are seeing the situation from rather than trying to fix the story by arguing with it directly.

sections_html.when: You have seen the distortion (the story has stampeded past what the feedback said), but you cannot successfully contain it from the inside. You keep returning to the distorted interpretation even after you have identified it as distorted. The feedback is about you, and because it is about you, your attempts to assess it fairly keep getting hijacked by your emotional investment in the verdict.
sections_html.move:
Three techniques that shift the perspective you are using to look at the situation. None of them argues against the story directly; they change the viewing position.

Imagine you are an observer. Think about the advice you would give your sister (or a close friend) if she had received the exact same feedback in the exact same situation. You know that she is overreacting: "Mom said that to you? It means nothing. That's how she is these days. Why do you even care? You're an adult!" That advice is available to you about yourself if you deliberately step into the observer position. Try it as a thought experiment: how would a sensible, caring person outside the situation describe what happened and what it means? Then take your own advice. The chapter notes that the difference in perspective is dramatic even when you know it's just a thought experiment. You can also solicit an actual friend's perspective on a specific piece of feedback ("does this e-mail sound as damning to you as it does to me?").

Look back from the future. Place yourself mentally at a point ten, twenty, or forty years ahead and look back at today. Ask yourself how significant today's events are likely to seem in the grand scheme of things. The chapter's framing: "Today feels big right now, but from the perspective of many days hence, it will look pretty small." You may still find the feedback challenging from that future vantage point, but the forever bias (the assumption that you will always feel as bad as you feel right now) becomes visibly false from there. The future-you has lived through this and continued.

Cast the comedy. Humor about the situation is not denial; it is a sign that you have begun to gain perspective. Comedy requires stepping outside the situation enough to see it as a story with a set of hapless characters and interesting plot twists rather than as a tragedy you are trapped inside. The chapter: "If you can see humor in the situation, it means you're succeeding in gaining perspective." Humor also has a neurological effect: it activates the left prefrontal cortex, which is associated with positive emotion and which exerts a calming influence on the alarm signals that the amygdala is generating. Juliet, putting down her wineglass: "So... boy meets girl. Boy deceives, betrays, and dumps girl. Girl will never date another bad boy because she has finally learned her lesson. Wait, who's that super-hot drummer over there?" That re-narration does not deny the pain; it names it in a frame that includes a future.

The three techniques can be applied individually or in sequence; the observer perspective and the future vantage are primarily cognitive; the comedy technique adds the physiological benefit of activating positive neural circuits.
sections_html.signal: From the observer position: you can give the same advice you would give your friend, and you can recognize it as advice you could actually take. From the future vantage: the story no longer feels permanently dire; you can imagine having gotten through it. From the comedy: you can hear something faintly funny about the situation without feeling that laughing means dismissing the pain.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "if my closest friend told me she received this exact feedback, what would I tell her?" If the answer you'd give her is genuinely different from how you're treating yourself, the observer position has been found and the vantage-point shift is available.

After: ask "can I now describe this situation from the perspective of someone who was not inside it?" If yes, the vantage has shifted. The situation has not changed but the perspective has, and with a different perspective comes a different (and more accurate) story.
sections_html.failure: Without a vantage-point change, the receiver remains in the position of the object of the feedback: someone directly targeted, for whom every nuance of the feedback is evidence about their fundamental worth and future. From that position, the distorted story is almost impossible to correct by reasoning alone, because the reasoning itself is inside the distorted frame. The observer, the future-self, and the comedian are all outside the distorted frame, and any of them can see the feedback at its actual size. The chapter's key insight: the same feedback that would seem minor if directed at your sister seems catastrophic when directed at you. The difference is entirely perspective, not content.
sections_html.example: The chapter uses the mother-and-sibling scenario. You receive feedback from your mother (one of her typical comments) and find yourself spiraling: does she love me? Has she ever loved me? Your sister, hearing your fears, is incredulous: "What?! That one stupid comment is still bugging you? Why are you worrying about that? It meant nothing. That's how she is now, and anyway, you're an adult!" Your sister is the observer. You have access to the exact same perspective; it requires deliberately stepping into it.
sections_html.source: Chapter 8, "Dismantle Distortions," lines 3857-3875 (imagine you're an observer: the mother/sister scenario, the thought experiment, the option to solicit an actual friend's perspective), 3881-3884 (look back from the future), 3890-3901 (cast the comedy: the gallows humor framing, the neurological mechanism, Juliet's re-narration). Stone and Heen call this the fourth distortion-dismantling strategy, "Change Your Vantage Point."
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/contain-the-story — the complementary step; contains the scope where change-your-vantage-point shifts the perspective

name: clarify-where-its-going
description: Someone has just given you forward-looking feedback (advice, a rating, a verdict, a piece of news) and you are about to walk away with the headline (the label, the score) without having asked what to do with it. You will replay the conversation later and find a list of questions you wish you had asked.

sections_html.when: A feedback conversation is happening or has just happened, and the giver has handed you something forward-looking: a piece of advice you are supposed to apply, a rating that determines what comes next, a piece of news that changes your situation. You have the headline. You do not yet have the actionable forward content — what specifically to do, what to expect, what is now expected of you. You are about to leave (or to nod) anyway. Reach for this before you do.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice that the feedback has a forward-looking component and that you do not yet have it. The chapter splits feedback into a coming-from layer (data, interpretation) and a going-to layer (advice, consequences, expectations). Most feedback has both. The going-to layer is what you act on; without it, the feedback is just news.
  2. If the feedback is coaching, run the actionability test. The chapter's exact form: "If I did want to follow this advice, would I know how to do so?" If the honest answer is no, the advice is not yet usable. Ask for examples, contrasts, demonstrations. The chapter's Tony-speech example: "Sparkles? Describe what you mean. Show me some examples of speeches that sparkle. And show me some examples of speeches you think fell flat." The contrast is what makes the advice take shape.
  3. Stay in the inquiry until you can visualize doing it. The chapter's claim: when you discuss advice in this kind of detail, you can start to visualize it, and once visualized, advice that sounded useless ("you need to learn to say no") often turns out to be useful after all. The signal that the inquiry is done is that you can picture the next concrete action.
  4. If the feedback is evaluation, switch to the consequences-and-expectations question set. The chapter is clear that evaluation makes the inquiry harder: you are still vibrating from the impact, and you are not in a curious state of mind. Even so, the questions are the actionable part. The chapter's three illustrative cases give the shape of the questions: - On a piece of news (Max's hearing loss): What caused this? What can I do to slow or stop further loss? How does this compare to others my age? Will it get worse, how fast? - On a verdict (Margie not getting the department head role): What were people seeing as gaps? Are there suggestions for how to fill them? How does this affect my project mix, my compensation, the next opportunity? - On a relational evaluation (the live-in girlfriend who will not marry you): What are you assuming about the future? What would need to change? Are you unsure about marriage or about me? Is there a path or is there not?
  5. Carry a short list of forward-looking questions in your back pocket. The chapter calls this the rip-cord image: you already have the skills, the trick is remembering to use them when the impact has just hit. The list does not have to be long; it has to be ready.
  6. If you missed the moment, come back later. The chapter's note: unlike forgetting to pull a parachute rip cord, you can usually have the follow-up conversation. The asking is not closed when the meeting ends.
sections_html.signal: For coaching, you can describe the next concrete action you would take if you decided to follow the advice — not in the giver's label, in your own visualized behavior. For evaluation, you can answer "what does this mean for me, what will happen next, what is now expected of me, what should I do given where I stand" with sentences sourced from the conversation rather than from your own guesses.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I left right now, what would I actually do differently tomorrow because of this feedback?" If the answer is "I am not sure" or "I will figure it out," the going-to layer has not been clarified.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if the answer is a specific behavior (for coaching) or a specific set of consequences and expectations (for evaluation), and the specifics came from the conversation, not from your own filling-in.
sections_html.failure: Without the clarification, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you accept vague coaching, walk out with the label, and either do nothing different (because you do not know what to do) or do the wrong thing (because you filled in the meaning yourself). The chapter's case is Tom, whose friend Liz tells him to "learn to say no," which Tom dismisses as unhelpful — until he asks Liz how she actually does it, at which point her concrete practices (sharing the dilemma with the team, using sorting questions, deferring the answer) turn an annoying label into something he can try. In the second, you accept evaluation, walk out with the headline, and discover only later what it actually meant for you. The chapter's cases are Max, who hears that his hearing has dropped 80 percent and only later thinks of all the questions he wishes he had asked; Margie, who hears she did not get the role, says "that's disappointing — who got it," and only later thinks to ask what gaps she could fill; and the unmarried partner who says nothing in the moment and weeks later realizes there was a whole conversation to have had. In all three, the actionable forward content was reachable in the room and was lost.
sections_html.example: The chapter offers two parallel scenes, one per kind of feedback. For coaching, Tom and Liz on "saying no": the label is empty until Liz describes her own practice (sharing the dilemma with her team, using sorting questions, declining to commit on the spot), at which point Tom can visualize it and decide whether to try it. For evaluation, the chapter runs three vignettes — Max and the audiologist, Margie and the promotion, the live-in girlfriend's silence — each pairing what was said in the moment with the questions the receiver later wished they had asked. The pattern is the same across kinds: the receiver had access to the forward content in the room, the impact of the news displaced the inquiry, and the rip cord went unpulled.
sections_html.source: Chapter 3, "First Understand," lines 1347-1414 ("Ask Where the Feedback Is Going," with the subsections "When Receiving Coaching: Clarify Advice" and "When Receiving Evaluation: Clarify Consequences and Expectations"). Stone and Heen split the forward-looking layer by kind of feedback (coaching wants advice you can act on, evaluation wants consequences and expectations you can navigate), give the actionability test for coaching ("would I know how to do it?") and the rip-cord image for evaluation, and supply scripted question sets for each. The skill installs both flavors as one move: when the feedback has a future, ask for it, in the right form for the kind.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: close-with-commitment
description: A feedback conversation is winding down and you are about to part ways. The chapter prescribes making one final explicit move before the conversation ends to ensure both parties know what was agreed and what comes next.

sections_html.when: A feedback conversation is ending. The content has been discussed. You are about to go separate ways. Neither of you has explicitly named what was agreed, what the next steps are, or what, if anything, will be different going forward.
sections_html.move:
Before parting, spend one to three sentences naming what you have agreed on and what each party will do next. The chapter: "If we're not explicit, we often end up disappointed by the lack of progress, or confused about the other person's lofty expectations."

The closing commitment does not require you to agree with the feedback or promise to change. You can commit to: thinking about what was said and returning to it tomorrow; gathering more information; bringing others into the conversation; seeing how things go over the next two weeks; or describing exactly which parts of the feedback you have decided not to take and why. The goal is clarity.

Depending on the context, the chapter names four types of things to firm up:

  1. Action Plans. Who does what? What, if anything, is each party going to change or work on, and what do you each agree to do to make that happen?
  1. Benchmarks and Consequences. How will progress be measured, and when? What are the consequences if benchmarks are not met? Discuss this explicitly rather than leaving it implicit.
  1. Procedural Contracts. When do we talk again, and about what? Agreements about process: gathering additional input from others, not discussing the matter in front of certain people, giving each other the benefit of the doubt, a method for checking in.
  1. New Strategies. Where the friction reflects an ongoing difference that is not going to disappear, articulate the ideas generated during the conversation for how to accommodate each other more successfully going forward.

Not every conversation needs all four; a simple one-sentence summary of what each party now knows and will do next is sufficient for most conversations.
sections_html.signal: Both parties leave with a shared understanding of what was agreed. The chapter: "You should both know where things stand." If asked the next day what happened in the conversation and what each person committed to, both would give substantially the same answer.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do we both know what we each agreed to do next?" If uncertain, the close has not happened.

After: ask "if the other person describes this conversation to someone tomorrow, will they describe the same commitments I would?" If no, the commitment was not explicit enough.
sections_html.failure: Without an explicit close, feedback conversations end when someone gives up, walks out, shuts down, or time runs out. Neither party knows what the other took from the conversation. The giver assumes something will change; the receiver does not know what was expected. Or both parties felt they reached understanding, but with no committed action, nothing happens and both wonder what all that talking accomplished. The chapter: "Both giver and receiver wonder why they spent so much time on the matter to begin with, when nothing ever changes." A one-sentence close resolves this.
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5509-5527. Stone and Heen name this the "Close" stage of the open-body-close arc and present the four types of commitment. The one-sentence minimum ("I want to think about what you've said, and let's talk tomorrow") is their own formulation.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/align-on-conversation-purpose — the bookend at the opening; opening alignment and closing commitment bracket the conversation
  • feedback/dig-for-interests-then-generate-options — the prior step; once options have been generated, close-with-commitment nails down which ones apply

name: coach-your-coach
description: You are getting feedback from someone, and either the feedback isn't getting through or the way it is being delivered is creating interference. The chapter prescribes talking explicitly to the feedback giver about how they can work with you more effectively.

sources.chapter: 12. Get Going (Five Ways to Take Action)

sections_html.when:
The coaching or feedback you are getting is not helping. The feedback is unclear, delivered in a way that triggers you before you can hear it, focused on the wrong things, or simply absent. You keep absorbing feedback that isn't reaching you, or the person who has the most to offer isn't offering it.

Also reach for this in any formal or informal coaching relationship before problems develop: establishing a shared understanding of how you learn best prevents most of the interference.
sections_html.move:
Tell them about "feedback and you." Tell the person giving you feedback the things they need to know to get through. The chapter's examples:

  • Subtle doesn't work with me. Be really explicit and don't worry about hurting my feelings. You won't.
  • I tend to get defensive at first, and then I circle back later and figure out why the feedback is helpful. So if I seem defensive, don't be put off.
  • I react better when you present your advice as an idea that might help rather than as "the obviously right answer."
  • Here's what I've been working on lately in terms of self-improvement. That's where I need the most help right now.
  • I'm really sensitive to negative feedback. Don't give it in the middle of a presentation unless it's urgent and immediately actionable.

These are descriptions of your feedback footprint (from know-your-feedback-footprint) and your current growth focus. They are not demands; they are operational information that helps the coach get through rather than get blocked. The chapter: "We're explaining our particular defensive formation not to block givers out, but to help them get through."

For more formal coaching relationships, discuss preferences, roles, and mutual expectations. Three topics:

  1. Your feedback temperament and tendencies: your wiring profile, your footprint, the kinds of feedback that land and those that tend to trigger.
  2. Your current growth areas: what you are working on, what you want more help with.
  3. Their philosophy, strengths, and weaknesses: ask what they find challenging about coaching you. Ask whether there is anything in how you receive their input that makes their job harder.

Also establish: whether the coaching is confidential, how often you will meet, how progress will be measured, what your priorities are.

Raise it in hierarchy carefully. When the coach is your boss, you may hesitate because admitting learning edges feels risky. The chapter's guidance: frame your learning needs in terms of doing your current job more effectively, not in terms of ambition or past failures. Not "I want to improve my meeting skills because I see myself as VP in five years" but "I want feedback on running meetings because I want to use the team's time well during the upcoming merger." The chapter also notes that workers who seek out coaching on what they can improve tend to receive higher performance ratings.

Ask your coach what they find challenging. The conversation is mutual. Your coach has their own preferences and blind spots. Asking them what they find challenging about the coaching work invites a more honest collaboration.
sections_html.signal: The person giving you feedback now knows at least one thing about how you receive it that they did not know before, and that knowledge changes how they deliver it going forward. The interference has been reduced.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "does the person giving me feedback know anything specific about how I receive it?" If the answer is no (they just give it and hope), the coach-your-coach conversation has not happened.

After: ask "is the feedback I'm receiving now landing differently than before the conversation?" If yes, the coaching has improved. If no, the conversation may not have been specific enough.
sections_html.failure: Without coaching the coach, the feedback relationship continues to generate the same interference indefinitely, because neither party names what is blocking the feedback from getting through. Doug's football team lost all eight games because no one asked the coach to explain the "In and Out Zone." The players did not coach the coach about what they did not understand; the coach never learned that the defensive formation had not been communicated. The chapter: "Our colleagues and family aren't even aware that the advice isn't getting through. Or perhaps they are very aware it's not getting through, but they're not seeing that how they're handling it is part of the problem."
sections_html.source: Chapter 12, "Get Going," lines 5978-5993 (the football story, the coach-the-coach concept), 5999-6006 (what it does not mean: not erecting barriers, but minimizing interference), 6012-6029 (talk about "feedback and you": examples of what to disclose), 6035-6053 (preferences, roles, mutual expectations: three topics, clarifying questions), 6104-6115 (hierarchy: frame in terms of current job effectiveness, workers who seek coaching get higher ratings).
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/know-your-feedback-footprint — provides the self-knowledge that this skill uses to inform the coach
  • feedback/ask-how-am-i-getting-in-my-own-way — the complementary move for soliciting blind-spot feedback; this skill handles the coaching relationship setup

name: contain-the-story
description: The story about the feedback has spread beyond the domain of the feedback itself, and you can see the distortion. The chapter prescribes restoring the containment boundaries that strong feelings caused you to forget, and offers three tools for doing so.

sections_html.when: You have noticed (perhaps after using notice-the-distortion-type or separate-feeling-story-feedback) that your story about the feedback has expanded beyond what the feedback actually addressed. The feedback was about one thing, but your story is about all things. The feedback was from one person, but your story has it from everyone. The feedback was about right now, but your story has it covering your whole past and extending your whole future. You need to pull the story back to actual size.
sections_html.move:
The chapter names three "laws of physics" that strong feelings cause us to forget:

  • Time: The present does not change the past. The present influences but does not determine the future.
  • Specificity: Being lousy at one thing does not make us lousy at unrelated things. Being lousy at something now does not mean we will always be lousy at it.
  • People: If one person does not like us, it does not mean that everyone does not like us. Even a person who does not like us usually likes some things about us. People's views of us can change.

When the story has violated one of these laws, use one or more of the three tools to restore the containment.

Tool 1: Feedback Containment Chart. Draw two columns. Left column: what IS this feedback about? Right column: what is this feedback NOT about? Write answers in both columns. The left column is the domain where the feedback applies and where you can learn from it. The right column is the domain where the feedback does not apply, where your story has trespassed, and where you need to stop processing the feedback as evidence.

The chapter's example: receiving a rejection from a dream job. The left column is: whether you have the specific qualifications they were looking for, or whether you presented yourself in quite the right way. The right column is: whether you will never get a job you like, whether you will ever work in your chosen field, whether your future is determined by this outcome. Writing the right column plainly often reveals how large the story has grown. Writing the left column plainly reveals how small and tractable the actual feedback is.

Tool 2: Balancing Picture. When you know logically that you are over-weighting one negative comment in a set of generally positive feedback, but you cannot feel the proportion, make it visual. Draw a pie chart, a bar chart, a Post-it collage, a literal picture of the proportions. The chapter's finding: Alita, who was devastated by critical comments in a sea of positive patient ratings, was shocked at how different the balance of positives and negatives looked when she drew it. The visual representation does not produce a final truth about the feedback, but it makes the proportion legible in a way that emotional experience alone does not.

Tool 3: Right-size the future consequences. When consequences are real (not getting the raise, not getting the job, not being allowed to fly), do not dismiss them. But separate the consequences that will happen from the consequences that might happen. The chapter's warning: in the grip of upsetting feedback, we often process might-happen consequences as if they were will-happen. Your boss confirmed you are not getting a raise. That your spouse will leave you because of the missing raise is only something that might happen, and the chances are small. But in the moment, it feels inevitable.

Right-sizing means developing a realistic and healthy prediction of what is actually likely to follow from the feedback, rather than treating the catastrophic projection as a forecast. The chapter cites Daniel Gilbert's finding that people consistently overestimate how awful they will feel and how long they will feel awful in the wake of loss. And they underestimate their own resilience. What feels like permanent damage is, for most people, a recoverable setback that something new will eventually fill.
sections_html.signal: The left column of the containment chart has the feedback in it and it is smaller than your story was. The right column has the trespass material in it and you can see the boundary. The visual representation of the feedback proportion no longer surprises you; you can hold the proportion in your head. You can name one concrete consequence that will happen and distinguish it from the cascade of consequences your story had appended.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "in my story about this feedback, what domains does it cover?" If it covers all of your skills, all of your relationships, your past, and your future simultaneously, the story has violated all three containment laws.

After: ask "what is the smallest accurate statement of what this feedback says?" If you can write that statement in one sentence and it fits inside the left column of the containment chart, the story has been contained.
sections_html.failure: Without containment, learning is impossible because the feedback has become overwhelmingly large. A person who believes that one critical remark means they are fundamentally incompetent cannot learn from the specific thing the remark addressed; they are too busy defending or mourning their entire competence. A person who believes a single rejection predicts all future rejections cannot investigate what specifically did not work in this case. The containment step is what allows the feedback to be usable: it shrinks the story back to the actual size of the feedback, where the actual lesson lives.
sections_html.example: The chapter's "dream job rejection" case: Before containment, the story is "I'll never get a job I like." After containment: the left column is "whether I have the specific qualifications they were looking for, or whether I'm presenting myself in the right way." The right column is "whether I will ever work in my chosen field, whether my future is determined by this." The contained version is tractable: you can identify and address one or two discrete things. The uncontained version is paralyzing: you are contending with your entire career.
sections_html.source: Chapter 8, "Dismantle Distortions," lines 3747-3764 (the three laws of physics for stories and the general instruction to restore containment), 3770-3813 (the Feedback Containment Chart), 3819-3829 (the Balancing Picture), 3834-3852 (right-sizing future consequences). Stone and Heen call this the third distortion-dismantling strategy, "Contain the Story."
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/separate-feeling-story-feedback — the prior step; separates feeling, story, and actual feedback before this skill reapplies the containment boundaries
  • feedback/change-your-vantage-point — the complementary step for when containment is difficult; changes perspective rather than scope

name: diagnose-and-name-the-boundary
description: You are feeling agitated, overwhelmed, or like you can never do anything right in a relationship, and you wonder whether the feedback dynamic is unhealthy enough to warrant an explicit limit. The chapter offers six diagnostic questions for telling the difference between difficult-but-legitimate feedback and a genuinely problematic pattern, and a three-type taxonomy for naming the boundary that is needed.

sources.chapter: 10. How Good Do I Have to Be? (Draw Boundaries When Enough Is Enough)

sections_html.when: You feel agitated or depleted in a feedback relationship. The feedback may be coming from someone you care about (parent, partner, mentor, boss) who seems to genuinely want to help you. But the cumulative effect is that you feel like you can never do anything right, that your self-acceptance is being eroded, or that the feedback is making things worse rather than better. You are unsure whether to draw a limit or whether drawing one would be overreacting.
sections_html.move:
Step one: Run the diagnostic. Six questions identify when a boundary is genuinely needed. A single yes does not automatically mean a boundary is required, but a cluster of yes answers is a strong signal.

  1. Do they attack your character, not just your behavior? The feedback addresses not what you did but what is fundamentally wrong with you: you are not attractive, ambitious, or good enough, you are not worthy of love and respect the way you are now.
