  1. Is the feedback unrelenting? You have told them it is not helping, but they continue. They have asked you to stop or adjust and they have not. Unhelpful feedback is useless; relentless unhelpful feedback is destructive.
  1. When you do change, is there always one more demand? There is no threshold of improvement that ends the feedback loop. The next critique is always queued. The giving of feedback is an end in itself, not a means to improvement.
  1. Does the feedback giver take the relationship hostage? They link your acceptance of their feedback to their acceptance of you. "If you don't do what I say, it means you don't love me." This strips you of autonomy while appearing to leave you free.
  1. Are they issuing warnings or making threats? A warning is a good-faith communication of legitimate consequences ("if your people management doesn't improve, we can't keep you in this position"). A threat is a manufactured consequence designed to induce fear or dependence ("if you leave me, no one will ever love you"). The chapter: "A warning is when someone tells you the other shoe may drop; a threat is when they make sure it will squash you."
  1. Is it always you who has to change? In every conflict or problem between you, you are the only one who takes responsibility. You apologize, you give in, you absorb. The relationship has no mutual accountability.

An additional criterion: Are your views and feelings a legitimate part of the relationship? Regardless of anything else, does the giver listen to how the feedback is affecting you? Do they care? Are they willing to adjust how they give feedback based on its impact on you? If your feelings are not part of the equation, something is wrong. The chapter quotes Rollo: "It's fine to try to figure out whether the giver is too critical or you're too sensitive, but if the other person isn't listening to you and your feelings, the answer is beside the point."

Step two: Name the type of boundary. If the diagnostic confirms that a boundary is needed, identify which of the three types applies:

  1. I may not take your advice. The softest boundary. You are willing to listen and consider the input. But the choice is yours, and you may not act on it. Use this when the feedback is offered with good intentions and in the absence of any of the diagnostic warning signs, but the giver does not recognize that taking the advice is optional.
  1. I don't want feedback about that subject, not right now. You are not only asserting the right to decline; you are asserting the right to be free of the topic altogether. The subject is off-limits for now (and possibly permanently). Use this when the subject is one on which you have reached a saturation point, when you have already thought it through, or when you need emotional space to sort it out yourself before receiving anyone else's input.
  1. Stop, or I will leave the relationship. The starkest boundary. Simply being in the relationship while subject to the ongoing feedback is doing damage to your sense of self. Use this when the diagnostic shows multiple yes answers across the warning signs, when you have already tried softer boundaries and they have not been respected, or when the relationship has become genuinely harmful.
sections_html.signal: You can answer the diagnostic questions and name which ones apply. You can name which of the three boundary types fits the situation. You are no longer unsure whether you are overreacting; you have specific evidence (the yes answers) and a specific response (the type).
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do I feel like I can never do anything right in this relationship, or do I feel agitated but basically seen?" The first points toward a boundary; the second may not.

After: ask "can I name, in one sentence, which type of boundary I need and why?" If yes, the diagnostic has produced a clear answer and you are ready for the set-the-boundary-clearly skill.
sections_html.failure: Without the diagnostic, receivers either stay in genuinely damaging relationships by telling themselves the feedback is legitimate, or draw unnecessary boundaries that damage functional relationships by labeling ordinary difficulty as a problem. The chapter names three common patterns (constant critic, hate-love-hate cycle, renovation relationship) where the absence of a clear diagnostic keeps the receiver stuck: Hunyee absorbs her mother's criticism because she believes it must be legitimate; Henry is too preoccupied with pleasing Isabella to notice she is not listening to him; Brodie dreads work but has not named what is happening as something he can address. The diagnostic provides the vocabulary for naming these patterns and the three-type taxonomy provides the vocabulary for responding.
sections_html.example: Henry's case (renovation relationship): Isabella's feedback started with wardrobe suggestions and expanded to cover every aspect of his life. He is genuinely unhappy and has told her so, but she responded that he is too sensitive to feedback. Rollo's diagnosis: "Is the feedback giver not listening to you and your feelings?" For Henry, the answer is yes. That is the decisive signal. The diagnostic question about feelings legitimacy has answered the "is a boundary needed?" question. The type: at minimum, boundary type 2 (I don't want coaching about these subjects); realistically, type 3 (stop or I will leave or change the terms of the relationship).
sections_html.source: Chapter 10, "How Good Do I Have to Be?," lines 4530-4573 (the three types of boundary), 4574-4666 (the six diagnostic questions: character attack, unrelenting, always more demands, relationship hostage, warnings vs. threats, always you who changes; and the feelings-legitimacy criterion). Stone and Heen present these as the tools for determining whether and which kind of boundary is needed before attempting to set one.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/set-the-boundary-clearly — the procedural follow-on; once the type is named, this skill handles the conversation

name: diagnose-the-cross-transaction
description: A feedback exchange has just gone wrong despite everyone meaning well, and you are dismayed, deflated, or more anxious than before. The content is not obviously bad, yet it did not address what you came for. You are about to argue the content or suppress the dismay, when the actual diagnosis is that the kind of feedback you wanted and the kind you got were different.

sections_html.when: You came out of a feedback conversation worse than you went in, and the puzzle is that nothing the giver said was obviously wrong or unkind. The headline content sounded reasonable, the giver seemed to mean well, and yet you feel unseen, undirected, or more uncertain than before. Reach for this before you decide the giver is bad at feedback or that you are bad at receiving it.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause the content debate. Whether the feedback was correct on the merits is a separate question and you are not on it yet.
  2. Run the cross-transaction check. There are two patterns; either or both may be firing: - Pattern A — kind-mismatch on want vs. got: you wanted one kind of feedback, the giver delivered another. The chapter's three round-robin cases: someone wanted appreciation but got coaching, someone wanted coaching but got evaluation, someone wanted evaluation but got appreciation. In each, the content was thoughtful; the kind was wrong. - Pattern B — kind-mismatch on intent vs. hearing: the giver intended one kind, you funneled it into another slot in your head. The most common version is the structural one: every coaching message carries an implicit "so far you are not doing this as well as you might," and the receiver hears the evaluative echo as the loudest part. The other common version: a casual remark intended as coaching ("call your mother") lands as evaluation ("you are a bad child").
  3. Name which pattern is firing, in one sentence each, in your own words. "I came in wanting evaluation; I got appreciation, which is why I am more anxious, not less." "Dad intended coaching; I heard 'you are uncoordinated,' which is evaluation."
  4. Decide what the diagnosis changes. If pattern A: the next move is to ask explicitly for the kind you wanted (see negotiate-which-kind-you-need), not to dismiss the feedback you got. If pattern B: the next move is to surface the funneling ("I notice I am hearing this as evaluation; was that your intent?") rather than treat the evaluative reading as fact.
sections_html.signal: You can complete the sentence: "I am dismayed because I wanted X but got Y" or "I am dismayed because I am hearing Y in what they meant as X." The dismay no longer feels like a generic bad feedback experience; it has a specific shape, and the shape suggests its own next move.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to say in one sentence why this conversation went badly, would I name the content, the giver's manner, or a kind-mismatch?" If you would name only the content or only the giver, you have not yet checked for the cross-transaction.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can name a kind-mismatch (pattern A or B, or both) and the conversation's failure now reads as a structural problem rather than a personal one.
sections_html.failure: Without the diagnosis, you misread a kind-mismatch as a content problem and either argue the content endlessly (the coaching is bad, the evaluation is unfair, the appreciation is hollow) or absorb the conclusion that you cannot handle feedback. The giver, who was acting in good faith with a different kind in mind, reads your reaction as ungrateful or fragile and stops offering anything. The relationship loses both the kind that was actually exchanged and the kind that was actually wanted, and the underlying need (for appreciation, coaching, or evaluation) does not get met because no one names that it was the need at stake.
sections_html.example: The chapter's law-firm scene runs the round-robin: April wants appreciation but Donald gives her coaching on time management, and she leaves feeling more invisible than ever; Cody wants coaching on his research memo and gets a reassuring "you are right on track for a first-year," which leaves him feeling more lost; Evelyn wants evaluation about partnership prospects and gets warm thanks, which leaves her parsing the appreciation for hidden bad news. None of Donald's individual responses are wrong on the content; all three conversations fail because the kind is mismatched. The chapter also names the structural version through Elsie at the batting cage: Dad is delivering coaching, but the implicit evaluative echo ("so far you are not doing this as well as you might") is the loudest part for her.
sections_html.source: Chapter 2, "Separate Appreciation, Coaching, and Evaluation," lines 970-1016. Stone and Heen identify two patterns of crossed wires in the "Beware Cross-Transactions" and "A Complication: There Is Always Evaluation in Coaching" sections: want-vs.-got mismatch (April, Cody, Evelyn) and intent-vs.-hearing mismatch with the structural evaluative echo inside coaching (Elsie, the steering-wheel comment, the Motor Vehicles employee). The skill installs the diagnosis that distinguishes these mismatches from content problems.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: difference-spot-not-wrong-spot
description: You have understood the feedback well enough (you spotted the label, separated data from interpretation, asked where it is going), and you and the giver still see the situation differently. You are about to conclude they are wrong, or to swallow the difference and pretend you agree, when the actual move is to surface what is different about your data and your interpretations.

sections_html.when: You have done the work to understand what the giver is actually saying, and your view still does not match theirs. You are about to settle the disagreement by deciding one of you must be wrong, or by absorbing their view to keep the peace, or by ending the conversation politely with nothing changed. Reach for this before you do, because the chapter's claim is that the disagreement is rarely a right-versus-wrong problem. It is almost always a we-have-different-data and/or we-interpret-the-data-differently problem, and naming which is the move that opens it.
sections_html.move:
1. Reframe the disagreement. The default reading is "one of us must be wrong, almost certainly them." The chapter offers a structurally different reading: we are looking at different data, and/or we are interpreting the data differently. Almost any feedback disagreement decomposes into one or both.
  2. Ask about their data that you do not have. Where do they sit, what do they see, who do they talk to, what reports cross their desk that do not cross yours? The chapter's framing: different roles, different physical locations, different bodies, different sensitivities, different cares all produce different data even when people are looking at the same situation. Your boss knows what your peers are paid; you do not. The receptionist sees who comes through the door; the CEO does not.
  3. Share your data that they do not have. The chapter is explicit that this is not the time to discard your own observations; you are not trying to get their ideas into an empty head, you are trying to get their ideas into your already-full head. Make your data visible to them as you ask about theirs.
  4. Surface differences in interpretation. Same data, different stories. The chapter names two specific factors most often embedded in interpretive differences: - Implicit rules: the unspoken rules in your head about how things should be ("conflict is bad" vs. "conflict is healthy"; "be direct" vs. "be nice"; on time means seated and ready vs. on time means walking in by then). You have rules; the giver has rules; you each treat them as the rules. Naming them as your rules and theirs is the move. - Heroes and villains: each of you is the sympathetic protagonist of your own version of the story, and may be reading the other as the obstacle. The chapter's surgeon-and-son scene: each is sure they are protecting the father, each sees the other as misguided. Once both heroes are visible, the moral framing softens enough to discuss the underlying disagreement.
  5. After the differences are on the table, do one more pass: ask what is right about their feedback. The chapter places this as a deliberate coda. The mindset is not "let me grade their feedback for objective truth" (that slides back into wrong spotting) but "what makes sense about what they are saying, what seems worth trying, where do they have a point." The chapter's image: walking through a forest identifying birds instead of trees. Noticing the birds does not make the trees wrong.
  6. If you still disagree at the end of all this, the chapter is clear that you have succeeded at understanding even if you have not converged. You can be transparent about your reaction ("that's upsetting to hear, I'm almost speechless") and still keep the conversation open.
sections_html.signal: You can name, in two parts, what is different about your data and what is different about your interpretation. You can articulate at least one piece of data the giver has that you do not (and ideally one piece you have that they do not). You can name at least one implicit rule each of you is applying to read the same situation. You can list two or three things the feedback gets right, distinct from agreeing or disagreeing with the verdict overall.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "When I think about why we see this differently, do I think 'they're wrong' or do I think 'we have different X'?" If the answer is "they're wrong" (and the X has not been named), wrong spotting is still running underneath your understanding work.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can complete the sentence "we see this differently because we have different ___ and we are interpreting the data through different ___." If you can do that, the disagreement has shape and the conversation can move.
sections_html.failure: Without difference spotting, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you understand the feedback (you did the work) and conclude that the giver is, on balance, wrong. The "on balance, wrong" verdict feels like a careful judgment but is in fact the wrong-spotting habit re-entering through the back door, now armored with the gloss of "I really tried to understand." The receiver loses the chance to learn from the parts of the difference that were genuinely informational about the world. In the second failure mode, you absorb the giver's view because you respect them or want to avoid conflict, and you give up your own observations and rules without testing them. The chapter is explicit that neither swallowing nor dismissing is the move; the goal is each of you understanding both views, with the differences named at the level of data and interpretation, not at the level of right-versus-wrong. Without that named structure, joint solutions are unreachable, because the parties cannot tell which differences are about facts they could share and which are about rules they may need to negotiate.
sections_html.example: The chapter's case is Mavis and Davis. Mavis is a lawyer on a cross-functional team; Davis, the account manager, gives her blunt feedback at her annual review: "You don't understand the business side of the business. Your laborious legal reviews slow the sales process and we lose out to fleeter competition." Mavis's first read: Davis is wrong, he doesn't see what I see. The chapter walks through the data each has (Mavis: the litigation reports, the general counsel's mandate, the cost of settlements; Davis: the customer conversations, the pitch-to-close ratio, what other firms are willing to offer) and the implicit rules each is applying ("legal's job is to rein in sales" vs. "no deal, no firm"). Neither is wrong on the data they have or the rules they hold; the puzzle is that each has only part of the picture, and difference spotting is what gets the parts on the table together. The chapter's coda case (the surgeon and the son in the postoperative corridor) makes the heroes-and-villains version: each is protecting the father from suffering, each sees the other as misguided, and the morality play has to soften before the medication question can be discussed.
sections_html.source: Chapter 3, "First Understand," lines 1420-1545 ("Shift From Wrong Spotting to Difference Spotting" through "Ask: What's Right?"). Stone and Heen offer the explicit decomposition (different data and/or different interpretations), name the two most common interpretive factors (implicit rules, heroes-and-villains), and place the right-spotting coda as a deliberate further step rather than the conclusion. The skill installs the chapter's second-stage move: once you understand the feedback, surface the differences at the levels of data and interpretation rather than at the level of right-versus-wrong.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: dig-for-interests-then-generate-options
description: A feedback conversation has reached an impasse over what should change. The chapter prescribes moving from position-fighting to interests-and-options, a problem-solving move drawn from Fisher and Ury's Getting to Yes applied specifically to feedback conversations.

sources.external: Roger Fisher, William Ury, and Bruce Patton, Getting to Yes: Negotiating Agreement Without Giving In (1981, 2nd ed. 1991)
sources.note: The canonical home of the positions/interests distinction that Stone and Heen explicitly cite. Fisher and Ury's book supplies what the chapter compresses: the full four-principle framework (separate people from problem, focus on interests not positions, invent options for mutual gain, insist on objective criteria), the BATNA concept (best alternative to a negotiated agreement), and detailed case studies of the distinction in complex negotiations. A reader of this skill who wants the deeper treatment of how to identify interests, how to handle resistant parties, and how to use objective criteria when interests genuinely conflict will find it there. Sharpens because Getting to Yes develops the interests-based framework as its entire subject; the skill extracts one specific application (feedback conversations reaching solution impasses) that the book subsumes in a larger negotiation framework.

sections_html.when: A feedback conversation has reached agreement on what the problem is (or at least that there is a problem), but is now stuck on what to do about it. The giver has a position (their prescription for what you should change), you have a position (usually a different or less drastic prescription), and neither position is acceptable to the other. You are arguing about solutions rather than exploring possibilities.
sections_html.move:
Distinguish positions from interests. Positions are what people say they want: "Be on time," "Cut your hair," "Stop micromanaging." Interests are the underlying needs, desires, fears, and concerns the stated position is meant to address. The chapter, drawing on Fisher and Ury: "Often interests can be met by a variety of options, some different from what anyone sees at the outset."

Listen for the interests behind the position. Advice typically arrives as a position that represents the giver's best current idea for what to do. The interest behind it is usually broader. Earl's supervisor said "clean up your appearance." The interest behind it: "We want families to feel comfortable with you more quickly." Once Earl had heard the interest rather than just the position, he could generate an option the supervisor had not considered: have the supervisor introduce him as a semiprofessional bluegrass banjo player. This put his appearance in a context that turned surprise into intrigue. The interest was met; the position was bypassed.

Identify which bucket the interest falls into. The chapter names three types of underlying interest:

  1. Helping you. The giver sees ways you could improve, or wants to protect you from problems they see that you don't. If this is the bucket, options that accelerate your growth or protect you from the named risk address the interest.
  1. Helping themselves or the relationship. The giver is telling you that your behavior is affecting them. "You travel too much" may mean "I feel neglected." Options that address the relational impact, not just the stated behavior, meet the real interest.
  1. Helping the organization, team, or someone else. The feedback is motivated by concern for a third party or system. Your boss cannot give you a higher rating because it would be unfair to others; your friend is raising an issue on behalf of a mutual friend who is uncomfortable raising it themselves. Options that address the third party or systemic concern address the real interest.

Generate options. Once you have identified the interests involved (including your own), generate options that address those interests rather than fighting over positions. The chapter: "The number one reason we don't come up with good options is that we simply don't think to try." Explicitly invite joint option-generation: name the different interests out loud and invite the other person to think with you. Some options will fully resolve the issue; some will be process options (we try this, then reassess; we trade off; we each try our approach and show both to the relevant audience). A process option does not require final agreement; it requires a next step that both parties believe is fair.
sections_html.signal: The conversation has moved from "your position vs. my position" to "what are you actually trying to accomplish here, and what are some different ways we could accomplish it?" At least one option has been generated that neither of you had before the conversation shifted to interests.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do I know what the giver is actually trying to accomplish, or do I only know what they say should change?" If only the latter, the interests have not been heard.

After: ask "is there an option on the table now that addresses what the giver is trying to accomplish while being acceptable to me?" If yes, the move has generated new possibilities.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, feedback conversations about solutions produce position fights. Each party argues for their version of what should change, and neither version is adopted because neither party understands why the other is proposing it. The chapter's example: Alita's patients complain that she runs late (position: run on time). Their underlying interest is not merely punctuality; it is to understand what is happening and to feel acknowledged. A sign explaining why appointments run late meets part of this interest without Alita being able to solve the structural problem that causes the delays. The position fight (run on time vs. I cannot change the structure) could not be resolved because neither party had articulated the actual interest.
sections_html.example: Earl's case: supervisor's position = "cut your hair and trim your beard." Underlying interest = "families should feel comfortable with you more quickly." Earl's counter-position = "I won't change my appearance; people who judge by appearance are no different from those who judge by disability." Impasse on positions. Earl identified the underlying interest (comfort, quickly) and generated a new option (preface his introduction with his banjo playing). The interest was met; the position fight was bypassed. Earl and his supervisor were then working together on the shared interest rather than against each other's positions.
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5432-5503. Stone and Heen draw explicitly on Fisher, Ury, and Patton's "Getting to Yes" distinction between positions and interests (line 5461). The chapter applies this framework specifically to feedback conversations and identifies three buckets for interests behind feedback.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/make-a-process-move — the prior step when the conversation is stuck on process; dig-for-interests applies when the conversation has moved past the stuck-process stage and reached a substantive impasse
  • feedback/close-with-commitment — the follow-on step; once options have been generated, nail down which ones will be acted on

name: embrace-identity-nuance
description: Feedback has contradicted a core identity label and you find yourself either exaggerating the feedback (it means I am fundamentally bad) or deflecting it (it cannot be true because I am good). The chapter locates the problem in the all-or-nothing structure of the label and prescribes replacing it with a complex, realistic self-description.

sources.chapter: 9. Cultivate a Growth Identity (Sort Toward Coaching)

sections_html.when: Feedback has arrived that challenges a core identity label you hold (I'm a good parent, I'm a fair person, I'm a strong leader, I'm someone who would do anything for those I love). You find yourself cycling between two unacceptable options: accept the feedback and lose the label entirely, or reject the feedback to preserve the label. Neither feels right. You are "flipping like a fish on the deck of a boat."
sections_html.move:
Recognize that the binary choice is produced by the structure of the label, not by the feedback itself. An all-or-nothing label works like a light switch: either you are ablaze in glory or you are in the dark. Even minor feedback that says "you're not all" gets heard as "you're nothing." The way out is to replace the all-or-nothing label with a realistic, complex description.

Rewrite the label as a complex self-description. You are not "a good son" or "a bad son." You are someone who cares deeply about being a good son. There are examples of your living up to that, and examples of your falling short. That is reality for every person who is trying to be anything. The chapter's formulation: "You've been complicated all along."

Accept three specific things about yourself. The chapter offers three acceptance items that underlie identity nuance and that are worth naming explicitly:

  1. You will make mistakes. The chapter: "Accepting the fact that you will make mistakes takes some of the pressure off." This does not make each mistake less worth examining. It removes the identity catastrophe from the equation.
  1. You have complex intentions. Your motives include the good ones you claim and also less noble ones you may not fully acknowledge. Self-interest is not evidence of moral failure; it is the nature of human motivation. The chapter: "pursuing a certain amount of self-interest is a requirement of being alive."
  1. You have contributed to the problem. In most feedback situations you have done or failed to do things that got you there. Accepting this closes the "I don't need to take feedback from you because you did something wrong too" loop.

Apply the nuanced description to the specific feedback. The chapter's worked case: when the narrator accepts that their decision to place their mother in a care facility involved both good reasons and genuine self-interest, they can hear Rita's feedback without either denying it (I had only pure motives) or accepting its maximum interpretation (I am heartless and selfish). A nuanced description holds both. The feedback then becomes something to examine rather than something that defines you.
sections_html.signal: You can write a sentence that acknowledges the identity label you care about, names instances of your living up to it, and names instances of your falling short, without the falling-short instances destroying the first part. Rita's feedback no longer requires you to choose between "good son" and "bad son." You can hold "complicated son" with more honesty and less pain.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "am I choosing between two options: accept this feedback and see myself as bad, or reject it and see myself as good?" If yes, the all-or-nothing label is running.

After: ask "can I describe what happened without using only 'good' or only 'bad'?" If yes, the nuanced description has replaced the binary label, and the feedback can now be examined for what it actually contains.
sections_html.failure: Without identity nuance, every piece of feedback that touches a core identity label forces a binary crisis. The receiver spends their energy toggling between exaggeration and denial, neither of which is learning. The chapter notes that even when both options feel untenable, people continue to toggle rather than finding a third option, because the third option (you are complex, not all or nothing) has not been introduced. The feedback that would have been useful gets lost in the identity crisis it triggered.
sections_html.example: The narrator helped move their mother into a full-time care facility. Rita's feedback was that this was selfish. While holding the all-or-nothing label "good son who would do anything for those I love," the narrator cannot hear the word "selfish" without having the label destroyed. After applying identity nuance: "I had good reasons for the decision. I also had self-interest in the decision. Both are true. I saw my mother nearly every day. I also had moments of impatience. None of this makes me only good or only bad." From that description, Rita's feedback becomes something worth examining: was the self-interest larger than the narrator had acknowledged? This is now a question about a specific decision, not a verdict on identity.
sections_html.source: Chapter 9, "Cultivate a Growth Identity," lines 4064-4105 (is your identity brittle or robust; the two shifts; the all-or-nothing label problem; the toggle between exaggeration and denial), 4111-4131 (embrace identity nuance: the narrator's worked case with the mother, the care facility, and Rita), 4136-4181 (the three things to accept about yourself: mistakes, complex intentions, contribution to the problem). Stone and Heen frame this as the first of two shifts toward a robust identity.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/adopt-growth-mindset-for-feedback — the complementary identity shift; nuance addresses the structure of the label (binary vs. complex); growth mindset addresses whether the traits involved are seen as fixed or growable

name: engaging-is-not-accepting
description: You are receiving feedback you suspect is wrong or unfair, and you feel forced to choose between taking it (to avoid being defensive) or rejecting it (to protect your judgment). The binary is making you either swallow feedback you do not believe or shut out a conversation you have not actually had.

sections_html.when: You are about to do one of two things and feel they are your only options. Option A: take the feedback because rejecting it would prove you cannot handle criticism. Option B: dismiss the feedback because if you engage with it you will end up agreeing to something you do not actually agree with. The pressure of the binary is what to notice. Reach for this before you commit to either.
sections_html.move:
1. Separate two questions that have collapsed into one. Question 1: am I willing to engage this conversation skillfully (listen, understand, ask, consider)? Question 2: do I intend to use this feedback to change?
  2. Commit to question 1 independent of your answer to question 2. Engaging fully does not commit you to taking the advice; it commits you only to understanding it.
  3. Inside the engagement, do the work: hear out what is being said, surface what you do not understand, look for what might be true even if the headline is off, name what you see differently.
  4. Only after the engagement decide question 2: take the feedback in full, take part of it, set it aside, or decline it. "I have understood, and I am choosing not to use this" is a legitimate landing.
  5. If the answer to question 2 is no, you may still owe a response (acknowledgment, your view, a boundary). Engagement-with-decline is not silent dismissal.
sections_html.signal: You can describe the feedback fairly enough that the giver would say "yes, that's what I meant" before you say what you intend to do with it. Your decision to use or not use the feedback is made on the merits, not driven by the felt need to prove you are open or to prove you have backbone.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "Am I treating disagreeing with this feedback and refusing to hear it as the same thing? Am I treating hearing it out and accepting it as the same thing?" Note where the collapse is happening.

After applying, ask the same questions. The skill landed if the two questions are now visibly separate and you can name what you would do with each independently.
sections_html.failure: Without the separation, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you treat engagement as commitment, so to protect your judgment you refuse the conversation, dismiss the giver, or argue everything down. You skip the part where some of it might have been useful, and you leave the relationship strained. In the second, you treat refusal as defensiveness, so you accept feedback you do not believe, change behavior on inputs you have not actually weighed, and store resentment that surfaces later as withdrawal or counter-attack. The binary makes both failure modes feel like virtue.
sections_html.example: The book opens the "Benefits of Receiving Well" section by spelling out the disambiguation directly. Receiving feedback well does not mean you have to take it. It means engaging skillfully and making thoughtful choices about whether and how to use what you are hearing, including, where appropriate, setting a boundary and saying no (the boundaries chapter, chapter 10, is where the no gets its own treatment). The intro plants the disambiguation so the rest of the book can teach engagement skills without the reader hearing them as instructions to capitulate.
sections_html.source: Introduction, "The Benefits of Receiving Well," lines 423-428. Stone and Heen state explicitly that receiving well is about engaging the conversation skillfully and making thoughtful choices about use, and signpost the boundaries chapter as the place where declining is treated as a full option. The skill installs that disambiguation as a moment-of-decision move.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: give-yourself-a-second-score
description: You received a negative evaluation. The first score is in and it is not what you wanted. The chapter's third growth-identity practice is to earn a second score, for how you handle the first, and to make the second score part of your identity.

sources.chapter: 9. Cultivate a Growth Identity (Sort Toward Coaching)

sections_html.when: You received a negative evaluation: a rejection, a poor review, a thumbs-down, a loss. The first score is in, and it is unflattering. Your initial impulse is to either find someone to blame or to collapse entirely. There is nothing you can do to change the first score. What you do next is entirely within your control.
sections_html.move:
Imagine an invisible second evaluation. After every first score, there is a second score that rates how you handle the first. The first score is for the situation itself; the second score is for what you do with it. You can receive an F for the situation and an A+ for the handling. Both scores are real; in the long run the second is often more important.

Give yourself the second score. This is not a consolation prize for failure. It is a genuine separate assessment. Melinda and Mel both received a thumbs-down on their YouTube video. Mel's second score: angry, blamed the audience for being too stupid to understand, withdrew. Melinda's second score: studied the craft, took a class, iterated, eventually reworked the video to a mostly positive reception. Both received the same first score; only Melinda received a second score worth keeping.

Make the second score part of your identity. The chapter's prescription: do not just occasionally aim for a good second score. Make it a component of your self-story. "I don't always succeed, but I take an honest shot at figuring out what there is to learn from the failure. I'm actually pretty good at that." Set up a Second Score Scorecard in your mind. This identity investment means the initial evaluation is no longer the end of the story; it is the start of the second story about what you make of the experience.

Handling it well is not performing wellness. The chapter is explicit: handling something well does not mean denying pain or emerging unscathed. Heather, whose longtime girlfriend left her, handled it well. Her handling: she got up every morning, went to work, treated everyone respectfully, and when she could not manage on her own, asked for help. That is handling it well. It is not claiming the situation is fine. It is facing whatever you are dealing with head-on. The chapter: "Even as Heather's identity as someone who is worthy of love took a hard hit, she found growth: 'I learned that I could deal with a tough loss with grace and resilience.'"
sections_html.signal: You can name what you are going to do in response to the first score that is within your control. You are not asking "how do I make the first score different" (unanswerable) but "what is the best next thing to do given that the first score is what it is" (answerable). The second score is available to you regardless of what the first score was.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "when I think about this negative evaluation, am I primarily thinking about undoing it, or about what to do with it?" If undoing, the second score has not been identified yet.

After: ask "what is the one concrete thing I am going to do in response to this evaluation?" If you can name it and do it, the second score has been claimed.
sections_html.failure: Without the second score concept, a negative first score has nowhere to go. Mel's response (blame the audience) protects his first score self-image at the cost of any second score. His identity is entirely invested in whether the work was good; since the audience called it not good, there is nothing to do except dispute the audience. Melinda's identity includes how she handles setbacks, which is partly independent of whether any given work is received well. That identity investment gives her something to do after the rejection. It also produces better work: she learned the craft and eventually posted a better video. The first score was a data point; the second score is a choice.
sections_html.example: Mel and Melinda post their first YouTube video. It gets savaged. Mel blames the world; Melinda wonders what they can learn. A few weeks later Melinda sees flaws in the execution that she had not noticed before. She studies, takes classes, makes more videos, and reworks the original to a positive reception. The chapter's summary: "Both Mel and Melinda got a thumbs-down first score; only Melinda got a thumbs-up second score. In this example, as is so often the case, a good second score is what really matters."
sections_html.source: Chapter 9, "Cultivate a Growth Identity," lines 4424-4453. Stone and Heen name this "Practice #3," "Give Yourself a Second Score." The Mel/Melinda case and Heather's account are the worked illustrations.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/unpack-judgment-from-evaluation — the prior step; once the first score has been unpacked into assessment, consequences, and judgment, the second score is about how you respond to the whole
  • feedback/adopt-growth-mindset-for-feedback — the conceptual foundation; the second score is a behavioral implementation of the growth mindset applied to the moment after a negative evaluation

name: identify-which-trigger-is-firing
description: You are reacting strongly to feedback (defending, attacking, withdrawing, or quietly going numb) and you are about to either suppress the reaction and "stay professional" or act on it without diagnosis. The reaction itself contains information you have not yet read.

sections_html.when: You are mid-feedback or in the immediate aftermath, and you can feel the reaction (heart pounding, stomach clenched, mind composing rebuttals, or a flat refusal to engage). You are about to do one of two things: push the reaction aside as a distraction so you can keep functioning, or let the reaction drive your next move without naming what set it off. Reach for this before either move.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause before responding or suppressing. Treat the reaction as a smoke alarm: it is telling you something is on fire somewhere, and the right first step is to locate the fire, not silence the alarm.
  2. Ask the categorizing question: which of the three is firing right now? - Truth trigger: the reaction is to the substance of the feedback. The internal sentence is "this is wrong, unfair, or unhelpful." The feeling is indignation or exasperation. - Relationship trigger: the reaction is to the giver, their credibility or motives, or to how you feel treated by them. The internal sentence is "I cannot hear this from you." The feeling is hurt, suspicion, or anger about the person, not the message. - Identity trigger: the reaction is to what the feedback implies about who you are. The internal sentence is "if this is true, what does that make me?" The feeling is destabilization, shame, threat, or being off balance.
  3. Name a mix where one exists. Triggers stack: feedback can hit all three at once (the chapter's example, Miriam at the bar mitzvah, gets truth-triggered AND relationship-triggered by the same comment). Naming the mix prevents you from solving for one and being run by the others.
  4. Pick the matching response track. Each trigger has a different remedy, developed in its own chapters of the book. Truth triggers want sorting and understanding. Relationship triggers want disentangling what from who. Identity triggers want stabilizing your story before you can engage. Mismatched remedies (arguing the content when the wound is identity, defending yourself when the issue is the relationship system) deepen the trouble.
  5. Only then engage the conversation, having decided which problem you are actually solving.
sections_html.signal: You can name the trigger (or triggers) firing in a single sentence per type, in your own words, before you take your next action in the conversation. The reaction is no longer just felt; it is read. Your next move is selected for the trigger that is actually firing, not for the loudest reaction or the easiest deflection.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask yourself: "If I had to write down right now what is upsetting me about this feedback, would I name the content, the giver, or what it implies about me?" If you cannot answer, or if you would write "everything" or "I don't know, I just feel awful," you have not yet read the alarm.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if you can answer with one or more of the three categories named explicitly, with a sentence locating each one in this specific feedback.
sections_html.failure: Without the diagnosis, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you treat the reaction as an obstacle to push past, so you suppress it, perform composure, and lose the information the alarm was carrying. The fire keeps burning; you act calm; the trigger leaks out hours or weeks later as withdrawal, resentment, or a reaction to the next small thing. In the second, you act on the reaction without reading it, applying the wrong remedy: arguing the content of feedback whose real injury was relational, attacking the giver when what you actually need is to steady your identity, or stabilizing your identity when the feedback was just plain wrong on the merits. The mismatch deepens the original trouble and confirms the giver's view that you cannot receive feedback. The receiver loses both the information the trigger was carrying and the encounter the trigger was inside.
sections_html.example: The book opens chapter 1 with the metaphor directly: trying to ignore a triggered reaction without first identifying its cause is like dealing with a fire by disconnecting the smoke alarm. Then it introduces Miriam, whose husband Sam tells her she was "unfriendly and aloof" at his nephew's bar mitzvah. Miriam's reaction reads as one big upset, but the chapter shows it is at least two: a truth trigger (the feedback is wrong, was she supposed to tap dance on the table) and a relationship trigger (she rearranged her mother's dialysis to be there and Sam can't even remember his nephew's name). When she switchtracks to the second without naming the first, both topics get tangled. The chapter's whole framework rests on the move of separating the triggers before responding.
sections_html.source: Chapter 1, "Three Triggers That Block Feedback," lines 493-527. Stone and Heen frame triggers as both obstacles and information ("a kind of map that can help us locate the source of the trouble"), and lay out the three categories as the book's organizing spine: truth triggers (challenge to see), relationship triggers (challenge of the we), identity triggers (challenge of being me). The skill installs the chapter's central diagnostic move: before applying any remedy, name which alarm is sounding.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: know-your-feedback-footprint
description: You are about to receive difficult feedback, or you have just received it and found yourself reacting in a way that felt automatic and hard to stop. The chapter names your predictable reactive pattern as your feedback footprint and prescribes three moves for working with it before, during, and after the conversation.

sections_html.when: You know you are about to receive feedback that will be difficult to hear, or you have realized, after a feedback conversation, that you reacted in your habitual way again (defended, went silent, agreed immediately and quietly resolved never to change, became awkwardly friendly, etc.) and did not actually receive what was said. You want to interrupt the pattern next time.
sections_html.move:
Before the conversation: name your footprint. Ask yourself, plainly: "How do I typically react when I receive feedback that upsets me?" The chapter offers examples: blaming others, switchtacking, crying, apologizing, chattering, going silent, agreeing while quietly resolving never to change, lawyering up emotionally, becoming awkwardly friendly, panicking. Note that your mind will present each example as an exception to how you actually are. The chapter is direct: those exceptions are not exceptions. They are how you are. If you have trouble discerning your footprint, ask those around you and notice your defensiveness as they describe it.

Before the conversation: inoculate yourself against the worst. If you have advance notice that difficult feedback is coming, think through what the worst plausible outcome is. Try it on emotionally, in a balanced and unhurried state, before you are in the conversation. Reason through what you would do if that outcome arrived. The chapter's mechanism: when you've already encountered the scenario once in imagination, the actual arrival is less shocking. Your nervous system has seen this threat before and knows it can handle it. You'll think, "Yes, this is what I feared might happen. I've seen this before. I'll be okay." You also have already thought through what you would do, rather than improvising while distressed.

During the conversation: notice what's happening. When feedback arrives and you feel your footprint activating, name it to yourself: "Okay, this is that thing I do. That triggered thought pattern I get into. That sick feeling I get." The chapter's finding: naming your reaction to yourself activates the left prefrontal cortex, which exerts a calming influence on the amygdala's alarm. Seth's account: "And that one thought really helps. I'm not fighting or resisting my thoughts and reactions; I'm just noticing them. Once I think, 'Yep, this is the part where I have my overreactions,' I actually start to calm down."

You are not trying to suppress the reaction. You are observing it, which creates enough distance for the reaction to begin to pass rather than to escalate.
sections_html.signal:
Before: you can name your footprint in one sentence (e.g., "I tend to agree immediately and then quietly resolve to change nothing" or "I get panicky and start snowballing"). After inoculation: you can describe what you would do if the worst-case arrived; it no longer feels abstract and terrifying.

During: you can name your reaction to yourself while it is still happening. The naming produces a sense of slight distance from the reaction; you are watching it rather than being entirely inside it.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "if I imagine getting very difficult feedback tomorrow, what is my first instinct?" If the answer is a behavioral reaction (flee, fight, deny, exaggerate) rather than a deliberate process, you have found your footprint.

After: ask "when the reaction arrived during the conversation, did I notice it, or did I only recognize it later in retrospect?" If only retrospect, the during-conversation noticing move needs more practice. If during, it has started to land.
sections_html.failure: Without knowing the footprint, each difficult feedback conversation triggers the same automatic reaction, and the reaction blocks learning. The person who always agrees and quietly resolves not to change never gets to decide consciously whether the feedback is worth acting on. The person who always panics cannot think about what the feedback actually says while the panic is running. The person who always deflects into critique of the giver's delivery never processes the content. Naming the footprint in advance creates a small, reliable gap between trigger and reaction. The chapter: "If you name it, you have some power over it."
sections_html.example: Seth, a counselor, receives a single offhand comment from his boss in a meeting and is instantly unable to think. He later recognizes this is his pattern: he is easily triggered, his reaction strong and fast. In retrospect the comment was benign. Seth has since learned to use the during-conversation noticing move and reports that naming the reaction helps him calm down. His footprint is panic plus snowballing; by naming it he gains enough distance to begin recovering.
sections_html.source: Chapter 8, "Dismantle Distortions," lines 3645-3665 (know your footprint: examples of reactive behaviors and stages, the instruction to ask yourself and notice your defensiveness as others describe you), 3671-3679 (inoculation technique: pre-experiencing the worst, reasoning through consequences, the immune-system analogy), 3684-3693 (notice what's happening during the conversation: Seth's account of self-observation and calming). Stone and Heen present these as three components of the first distortion-dismantling strategy, "Be Prepared, Be Mindful."
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/understand-your-wiring-profile — the biological explanation of why the footprint exists; wiring profile and feedback footprint are complementary, with one describing physiology and the other describing learned behavioral patterns
  • feedback/notice-the-distortion-type — the diagnostic of which distortion is running once the footprint has activated

name: listen-for-the-issue-beneath-the-coaching
description: Someone close has just given you advice ("you should be more responsive," "you'd be happier if you didn't think about work so much," "you're drinking too much, it's not good for you"). The advice has emotional weight on their side that does not match the surface request. The chapter says the surface coaching may be a relationship concern in disguise, and you are about to address the wrong topic.

sections_html.when:
Someone close has framed something as advice or coaching for your benefit. The advice has heat or weight on their side that the surface message does not justify. They have raised it before, or insisted on it, or returned to it. You can feel that disagreeing with the advice will not actually settle the matter. You are about to argue with the advice on its terms, or accept it on its terms, or dismiss it as their hangup, and walk away thinking the conversation was about what they said it was about.

This is the inverse of switchtracking. In switchtracking, the receiver substitutes a second topic for the giver's. Here, the giver has already done the substitution: they have packaged a relationship concern as coaching, and the receiver who stays on the giver's surface topic has stayed on a topic that was not the real one.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice the mismatch between surface kind and emotional weight. If the apparent kind is coaching ("here is something you could do better") and the giver's affect is closer to hurt, frustration, anxiety, loneliness, or sustained insistence, the surface coaching is likely not the real exchange. The chapter is explicit that the giver may not realize they are doing this; the coaching frame is often where their actual concern goes when feelings are uncomfortable to raise directly.
  2. Ask yourself the chapter's check question before answering the advice: "Are we on the same track? What is the real topic here?" Resist the impulse to answer the advice on its merits before you have answered this.
  3. Translate the surface coaching into its possible underlying concerns, using the chapter's "might mean" pattern. The chapter's worked pairs: - "You might want to be more responsive" might mean "I'm frustrated that you don't return my calls." - "I think you'd be happier if you didn't think about work night and day" might mean "you're so preoccupied with work that it's lonely for me." - "If you delegate some of your workload to me, you'll have more time for the important things" might mean "I want you to trust me with more responsibility." - "You're drinking too much, it's not good for you" might mean "I'm worried about your drinking, and it's getting in the way of our relationship."
  4. Open the underlying topic by asking the giver directly. The chapter's specific move with parents (and which generalises): "What are you worried about?" The variant for a partner or colleague: "What is going on for you?" or "Is there something else you want me to hear?" The point is to invite the giver to tell you what is actually on their side of the table, instead of trying to guess from the surface advice.
  5. Engage the underlying topic when it surfaces, on its own terms. If your mother's "why aren't you married yet?" turns out to be a tangle of worry about your future, regret about her own choices, fear that you don't take her advice, and an inability to relax until you are settled, the conversation worth having is about those, not about whether you are making enough effort to date. Once the real topic is on the table, you can decide whether the surface coaching still matters; sometimes it does, often it does not in the form first stated.
sections_html.signal: You can name two things: what the giver said on the surface, and what the underlying concern turned out to be (or is most likely). The conversation is now about the second. The giver feels heard on something they were not previously heard on, and you are no longer arguing about a topic that was never the real one.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I addressed the advice exactly as stated and resolved it to my satisfaction, would the giver feel the conversation was over?" If the answer is no or maybe, the advice is not the real topic and you are about to address the wrong one.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if the giver's answer to "what are you worried about" or "is there something else going on" surfaces a concern you were not addressing, and the conversation has moved onto that concern as the actual topic.
sections_html.failure: Without listening underneath, the receiver does what the chapter calls "case closed, let's move on": you address the surface advice, agree or disagree on its merits, and walk away. Sometimes that works. Often the giver feels not just unhelped but unheard, because the actual concern has gone unspoken and unaddressed. You then become baffled when the same advice keeps coming back, in slightly different forms, with the same heat, and conclude the giver is being unreasonable. The chapter's claim is that the giver is not unreasonable; the giver is using the coaching frame because feelings are harder to raise directly, and you have politely treated the coaching as the real conversation. The cost is the same in both directions: the giver does not get the conversation they actually need, and you do not get to know what the conversation was actually about. The chapter also names the symmetric trap from the receiver side: switchtracking around your own autonomy or acceptance triggers ("Mom, I'm thirty-eight years old, I can run my own life"), which preserves the surface fight and misses the underlying concern just as completely.
sections_html.example: The chapter's worked sequence is the mother-and-marriage scene. Mother asks: "Why aren't you married yet? I don't think you're really making an effort to meet people." The receiver's three default moves are all addressed and rejected: argue the assessment ("that's not true, I am making an effort"), switchtrack on acceptance ("I'm perfectly happy single, why are you always trying to change me?"), or switchtrack on autonomy ("Mom, I'm thirty-eight, I can run my own life"). Each fights the surface advice and stays on the surface advice. The chapter's alternative is to ask "what are you worried about?" and listen for what actually emerges. The chapter then lists possible answers: I'm worried it will get harder as you get older. I'm worried you'll end up with someone you don't like, like I did. I'm worried I did something wrong. I can't relax until you're married. None of these are about dating strategies. The chapter notes that understanding the real concern also eases the receiver's autonomy and acceptance triggers, because what looked like a verdict on you turns out to be largely about her, and once that is on the table you can make a calmer decision about whether your original triggers still feel important to discuss.
sections_html.source: Chapter 5, "Don't Switchtrack: Disentangle What from Who," lines 2654-2711 ("Listen for the Relationship Issues Lurking Beneath Their 'Advice'"). Stone and Heen treat this as a separate move from switchtrack-spotting: even the receiver who is alert enough not to substitute their own topic for the giver's can still misunderstand what the giver's topic actually is, because givers often package relationship concerns as coaching. The chapter's check question ("Are we on the same track? What is the real topic here?"), the four "might mean" diagnostic pairs, and the "what are you worried about?" probe constitute the move. The skill installs the chapter's third move in its three-step relationship-trigger remedy (after spotting and signposting): listening for the topic the giver is actually on, not the topic they have stated.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: look-for-pattern-not-counterexample
description: Feedback has just stung, and you can feel yourself reaching for contradictory evidence to file it as an outlier ("but X said the opposite," "but I won that award," "but I'm not like that with Z"). Reach for this before the contradiction lands. The chapter inverts the reflex, asking you to look for consistent feedback rather than contradictory.

sections_html.when: A piece of feedback you do not like has just landed. Within seconds, your mind has a roster of counterexamples ready: people who said the opposite, awards you have won, contexts where you behaved nothing like that. You are about to use the roster to file the feedback as an outlier and move on. Reach for this before you do, because the chapter says counterexample-hunting is exactly how blind spots stay hidden.
sections_html.move:
1. Catch the contradictory-evidence reflex. The chapter's image: "you say I'm self-absorbed? Then how come I won the community service award last year? You think I interrupt? Let me stop you right there..." The reflex is fast and feels like fairness ("look at all this contrary data"), but it is structurally an avoidance move. The skill begins with naming the reflex.
  2. Look for consistency, way one: same behavior, different interpretation. The chapter's table pairs the receiver's self-description against others' read of the same behavior: shy / aloof, upbeat / phony, spontaneous / flaky, truth-teller / nasty, passionate / emotional, smart / arrogant, high standards / hypercritical, outgoing / overbearing, quirky / annoying. Run the feedback through this filter: is the giver and you describing the same observable behavior, with one of you using a flattering label and the other using an unflattering one? If yes, the disagreement is not about what happened; it is about what to call it. The chapter's framing: others may be misunderstanding you (shy read as aloof) or you may be unaware of your impact (outgoing landing as overbearing). Both produce the same paired pattern.
  3. Look for consistency, way two: where have I heard this before? Run the feedback against your history. Has another person, in another context, at another time, said something that lands in the same general region? The chapter's punchline is brutal: if your first-grade teacher and your first wife both complained about your hygiene, it might be time to listen. The point is not that any single piece of feedback is decisive; it is that consistency across time, source, and context is the strongest evidence you have access to as a receiver, and counterexamples cannot dissolve it.
  4. Hold the consistencies separately from the verdict. The skill is not "I must be guilty"; it is "the pattern is real, regardless of whether I accept the giver's interpretation of it." The data is the recurrence. The interpretation is still up for negotiation (per separate-data-from-interpretation and difference-spot-not-wrong-spot, applied across multiple feedback events instead of within one).
  5. Notice the silence that has been hiding the pattern. The chapter's "we collude to keep each other in the dark" point: people often don't tell us, and the receiver mistakenly reads silence as confirmation. The pattern has been visible to others for years; the question is what they were willing to say and when.
sections_html.signal: You can name, in two parts, the consistency the feedback is part of. Part one: which behavior on the chapter's table (or a similar pair) is being described, with the flattering label you use for it and the unflattering label others use for it. Part two: at least one prior instance, ideally from a different relationship and a different period of your life, where the same theme surfaced. If you cannot name any prior instance, you have either checked carefully and this really is novel (rare), or you have not yet looked.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to write down right now whether this feedback connects to anything I've heard before, would my first instinct be to list reasons it does not?" If yes, the counterexample reflex is running.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can complete two sentences. "The flattering label I have for this behavior is ___; the unflattering label they have for it is ___." "Where I've heard this before, from a different source, is ___." If both blanks fill in, the pattern has shape, even if you and the giver still disagree on what to do about it.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, the counterexample reflex produces a stable but misleading verdict: each piece of feedback is a one-off, no piece coheres with any other, and the receiver concludes there is no pattern. The chapter is explicit about why this is wrong. First, the receiver is selectively recalling counterexamples and not pattern-confirmations, by virtue of being the receiver. Second, the people who saw earlier instances of the pattern often said nothing (the colluding-silence point), so the absence of prior corroboration is not evidence; it is censorship in the politest possible form. Third, the same observable behavior is often paired with both flattering and unflattering labels, and counterexamples in the flattering vocabulary do not contradict feedback in the unflattering vocabulary; they confirm it. Annabelle's case is the worked instance: two 360s three years apart, same theme, no recognition of the pattern; she instead processes each 360 as a separate puzzling event explainable by ad-hoc theories about her subordinates' motives.
sections_html.example: The chapter at lines 2029-2098 lays out both consistency-checks and gives the table directly. The shy/aloof, high-standards/hypercritical, outgoing/overbearing pairings are the chapter's own examples of the same-behavior-different-interpretation pattern. The "where have I heard this before" probe is sharpened with the first-grade-teacher / first-wife / hygiene example as the chapter's deliberately uncomfortable case. Annabelle's two-360s-three-years-apart situation, given as the chapter's framing case (lines 1751, 1806), is the larger demonstration: same feedback, separated in time, processed each time as though novel, when the pattern across time was the strongest data available.
sections_html.source: Chapter 4, "See Your Blind Spots," lines 2029-2098 (the section "Look for Patterns" with the same-behavior-different-interpretation table). Stone and Heen prescribe consistency-search as the diagnostic move that contradictory-evidence-search forecloses, give the table of paired labels as a concrete tool, and add the "where have I heard this before" probe as the second consistency check. The skill installs the inversion of the receiver's default reflex: instead of looking for contradictions, look for recurrences.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: make-a-process-move
description: A feedback conversation has gotten stuck, is escalating, or is cycling without progress. The chapter prescribes stepping outside the content of the conversation to diagnose what is stuck, describe it explicitly, and propose a correction.

sections_html.when: You are in a feedback conversation and something has gone wrong with the conversation itself rather than with the content. Both of you are repeating yourselves, the heat is rising without resolution, you cannot agree on what you are even arguing about, one of you has shut down, or you are deadlocked and need a fair way to break the impasse. You are not stuck on the substance; you are stuck on the process.
sections_html.move:
Step outside the conversation and function as your own referee. The chapter: "Supercommunicators had an exceptional ability to observe the discussion, diagnose where it was going wrong, and make explicit process interventions to correct it. It was as though they were functioning in two roles at once: They weren't just players in the game, they were also referees."

Three steps:

  1. Diagnose. From outside the conversation, name specifically what is stuck. Common patterns: - Both are arguing and neither is listening. - The conversation has two intertwined topics that need to be separated. - Both parties are treating a difference of perspective as a question of truth. - The emotional intensity is too high to continue productively. - You are deadlocked and need a process to break the tie.
  1. Describe. Say out loud what you have diagnosed. The chapter's worked examples: - "We're both making arguments, trying to persuade the other, but I don't think either of us is listening to, or fully understanding, the other." - "I see two issues here, and we're jumping back and forth between them." - "I'm not saying my view is right and yours is wrong. I'm saying we see it differently." - "I'm shocked by this. My internal voice is saying this simply did not happen that way. I'd like to take a break and come back in a couple of hours." - "We're deadlocked. We both need to agree, and we don't."
  1. Propose. Offer a specific next step or invite the other person to problem-solve about how to go forward. Do not demand; propose or invite. The worked examples continued: - "Tell me more about why this is so important to you." - "Let's focus on one issue at a time. Which do you want to talk about first?" - "I wonder if there are aspects of how you see this that I'm not fully understanding, as opposed to simply disagreeing with? What would you add?" - "I'd like to take a break and come back to this in a couple of hours after we've both had time to calm down." - "What's a fair and efficient way to decide when we don't agree?"

Expect it to sound slightly awkward. The chapter: "All of these examples sound slightly awkward—not how regular people talk. And paradoxically, that's one of the reasons these kinds of interventions can be so powerful." The slight formality is the signal that you have stepped outside the flow of the conversation to name its process. That pause is the point.

The process move is not about the substance: you are not telling the giver what they can or cannot say. You are describing the state of the conversation and suggesting a direction.
sections_html.signal: You have said something out loud about the conversation itself (not its content), and both parties have a shared observation of what was stuck. The conversation has moved off the escalation track and onto a different track. The giver is engaging with your process description rather than continuing to drive the previous direction.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "what is happening to the conversation, not what it is about?" If the answer is something like "we keep repeating ourselves" or "it's getting louder without getting anywhere," a process move is called for.

After: ask "did anything shift in how we were engaging with each other?" If yes, the process move has altered the conversation's trajectory.
sections_html.failure: Without process moves, a stuck conversation stays stuck. Both parties continue doing more of whatever they were doing that was not working: arguing more loudly, listening less, withdrawing further, or repeating the same point more emphatically. The chapter notes that skilled communicators are distinguished precisely by this referee capacity; it is a learnable skill, not a personality trait. The failure mode is treating the stuck conversation as a content problem (trying to say the content differently or more forcefully) when it is actually a process problem (the structure of the exchange is preventing anything new from entering).
sections_html.example: The chapter provides five distinct worked examples of process moves, covering five different stuck patterns. The most structurally complete is: "Okay, we're deadlocked. We both need to agree on this, and we don't. Your solution is that I should give in. As a process, that doesn't feel fair to me. On the other hand, I don't know how to break this deadlock, so we've got to figure it out. What's a fair and efficient way to decide when we don't agree?" This contains all three steps: diagnose (we're deadlocked, your proposal is that I give in), describe (that doesn't feel fair as a process), propose (let's figure out a fair and efficient way to break this kind of tie).
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5394-5427. Stone and Heen call this the third skill in the body of the conversation (after listening and asserting), name it "process moves," and describe it using the "referee" metaphor. The five worked examples span the common stuck patterns.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/align-on-conversation-purpose — the opening version of a process move; align-on-conversation-purpose sets the process frame at the start, make-a-process-move corrects it mid-flight

name: mitigate-when-not-changing
description: You have decided not to change in response to feedback. The chapter argues that you still have a duty to reduce the cost of your decision on others, and offers three moves for doing so.

sources.chapter: 10. How Good Do I Have to Be? (Draw Boundaries When Enough Is Enough)

sections_html.when: You have considered feedback carefully and decided not to take it. Your children want you to move out of your home; your staff wants you to abandon the reorganization; your neighbor wants you to get rid of your cat. You have heard the concerns and are going ahead anyway. The relationship continues. You cannot simply go your way and declare the matter closed.
sections_html.move:
Inquire about and acknowledge the impact on them. Ask directly how your decision affects those who live and work with you. Larry decided not to take medication for his ADHD. This affects his family (who have to prompt, prod, and remediate) and his construction crew (who have efficiency and safety concerns). Larry needs to have that conversation: what are the actual impacts, and what can be put in place together to address them? The decision is his; the consequences are shared.

Coach them to deal with the unchanged you. Tell them specifically what they can do to manage the situation given that you are not changing. Jackie knows she dominates discussions and has decided, after a year of trying, to stop working on it. Her message to her teammates: "I know I can be overbearing. I tried to change. It was a lot of effort for almost no benefit. So I give you all permission to cut me off. Red-card me. I promise I won't think you're being rude; I need the help." She is not pretending she will change. She is giving others the tools to work with the person she actually is.

Problem-solve together. This is not about shutting down the conversation but about jointly inventing ways to minimize the cost of your decision. Mark has offered Steve football tickets for three decades and Steve reliably shows up late or not at all. Steve is not going to change. Rather than Mark continuing to offer and be disappointed, or stopping the invitations entirely, they work out a modified protocol: when Mark extends an invitation, they discuss Steve's specific conflicts, Steve's level of commitment, and what happens to Mark if Steve bails. Sometimes Steve suggests they do something else that better fits what they actually want: time together. The chapter's framing: "The clarity of Steve's boundary made it easier for the brothers to spend time together."
sections_html.signal: You have had a conversation (not just thought about having one) with the affected parties. You know specifically what difficulties your decision creates for them. You have either coached them on how to work with the unchanged you, or you have jointly designed a modified arrangement that reduces the friction. The people affected are no longer simply buffeted by your decision without input.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "have I actually talked to the people affected by my decision, or have I only informed them?" If only informed, the mitigation conversation has not happened.

After: ask "do the affected parties have something they can do to manage this situation, other than wait for me to change?" If yes, the mitigation has given them agency. If no, the duty to mitigate has not yet been fulfilled.
sections_html.failure: Without mitigation, the decision not to change is experienced by others as indifference to its impact on them. The relationship is left with a source of ongoing friction that is known, named, and deliberately unaddressed. The people affected either absorb the consequences silently (which builds resentment), continue to push for change (which generates repeated futile conversations), or leave the relationship. The chapter notes that mitigation paradoxically can make it easier to be together: when the unchanged reality is acknowledged and planned for, rather than denied or resisted, both parties can engage honestly with who each person actually is.
sections_html.example: Mark and Steve's thirty-year football ticket conflict. Steve is not reliable. Steve knows this. Mark knows this. Instead of the choices of continued resentment (Mark keeps inviting and is repeatedly disappointed) or withdrawal (Mark stops inviting), they problem-solve: specific check-ins about Steve's conflicts on the day in question, explicit discussion of the cost to Mark if Steve bails, and Steve's offer to sometimes suggest an alternative that serves their real goal (spending time together). Both parties are working with reality rather than the fiction of a changed Steve.
sections_html.source: Chapter 10, "How Good Do I Have to Be?," lines 4886-4893 (duty to mitigate: the framing), 4899-4903 (inquire about and acknowledge impact: Larry's ADHD medication decision), 4908-4912 (coach them to deal with the unchanged you: Jackie's dominates-discussions case), 4917-4930 (problem solve together: Mark and Steve's Steelers tickets). Stone and Heen frame this as the counterpart to boundary-setting: if you're keeping the feedback out, you still owe those affected the work of making it easier to live or work with the unchanged you.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/set-the-boundary-clearly — the companion skill for the inbound direction; this skill handles the outbound direction (your impact on others when you don't change)

name: name-both-needs
description: You are reacting more strongly to a piece of feedback than the content seems to warrant, or you are suppressing a reaction that you suspect will surface later.
