The disproportion is a sign that two different needs are colliding under one conversation, and the conversation cannot resolve until both are named.

sections_html.when: You are receiving feedback and the size of your reaction does not match the size of the input. Either you are flaring at a small note ("why can't you just accept me?") or you are nodding along now and noticing a stored charge that you expect to surface in a day or a week. Reach for this when the reaction feels too big, too small, or too delayed, and you suspect more than the headline content is at stake.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause the content debate. Whether the feedback is true or fair is a separate track and you are not on it yet.
  2. Name the learning need out loud (to yourself or in the conversation): "Part of me does want to get better at this and is curious what is here to learn."
  3. Name the acceptance need out loud: "Part of me wants to be received as I am, and this feedback is signaling that how I am is not quite okay, which is what is stinging."
  4. Hold both as real and simultaneous. Refuse to collapse either one. The acceptance need is not weakness to be argued down; the learning need is not betrayal of your selfhood.
  5. Decide what each need is asking for separately. The learning need may want a clarifying question, an experiment, a piece of coaching. The acceptance need may want a reassurance from the relationship, a boundary, a gesture, or simply your own acknowledgment that the sting is legitimate.
  6. Then, with both surfaced, return to the content of the feedback.
sections_html.signal: You can state, in a sentence each, what you want to learn and what you want to be accepted for, and the two sentences sit alongside each other without one canceling the other. The reaction-size returns to proportion: either the flare subsides because the acceptance need has been heard, or the suppression releases because the learning need is no longer being smuggled through the back door.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "Is the size of my reaction explained by the content of this feedback?" If no, ask "what else is being touched?"

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can answer the second question with a named need ("I want them to know I am already trying," "I want this person specifically to think well of me," "I want the work itself to be seen") rather than a vague "I just felt weird about it."
sections_html.failure: Without naming both needs, one of them goes underground and runs the conversation from there. If the acceptance need is hidden, you argue the content endlessly because winning the content argument is doing the work the acceptance need is silently asking for. If the learning need is hidden, you defend yourself and miss the coaching the relationship is offering, then notice months later that you are stuck. Either way, the conversation that is happening is not the conversation that is needed, and the unspoken need does not go away; it leaks into withdrawal, resentment, or the next argument.
sections_html.example: The book frames the entire intro around this collision. Humans are wired to learn, and learning about ourselves is also where we collide with our longing to be loved, accepted, and respected as we are. The "Tension Between Learning and Being Accepted" section names the bristling out loud: "Why can't you accept me for who I am and how I am? Why are there always more adjustments, more upgrades?" The book's stance is that the tension is not going away and the leverage is in managing it, not resolving it, which is what naming both needs does in the moment.
sections_html.source: Introduction, "The Tension Between Learning and Being Accepted," lines 392-417. Stone and Heen identify receiving feedback as sitting at the intersection of two fundamental drives, and frame the receiver's task as managing the tension rather than picking a side. The skill operationalizes the framing as a moment-of-trigger diagnostic.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: name-which-mirror-you-need
description: You have received unwelcome feedback and you are about to take it to a friend, partner, or trusted colleague for a second opinion. Your phrasing is forming as "this can't be true, can it?" or "he's overreacting, right?" The chapter says this is the implicit ask of a supportive mirror, and you may not actually want what you are about to ask for.

sections_html.when: You have a piece of feedback you want help thinking about. The person you are about to take it to is close to you, knows you well, and would normally line up on your side. You are about to lay out the feedback and the giver in a way that makes it easy for your friend to dismiss it ("the guy in Purchasing was overreacting"). Or, the inverse situation: a friend has come to you with their own such feedback and you are about to give an honest read where they were hoping for support. Reach for this before either of those exchanges starts.
sections_html.move:
1. Recognize the two kinds of mirror. The chapter's distinction. A supportive mirror shows your best self under flattering light. Its function is reassurance, restoration of the relationship, reminding you that you are a good person and that the bad picture is not the real you. A honest mirror shows you what others actually saw on the day in question, including the leaky face and the bedhead. Its function is to surface a blind spot. Both are legitimate. Both are needed at different times. Confusing them is what produces the trouble.
  2. Notice your default. Your default ask, especially with a long-standing close mirror, is supportive. Your default phrasing telegraphs it ("right?", "this can't be true?", "the giver was being unreasonable, wasn't she?"). The chapter's image is the wicked queen in Snow White, who is not actually asking the mirror for an honest assessment. Catch the default before you ask.
  3. Name the kind you want. The chapter gives explicit scripts. For honest: "Here's feedback I just got. It seems wrong. My first reaction is to reject it. But I wonder if this is feedback in a blind spot? Do you see me doing this sometimes, and if so, when? What impact do you see it having?" For supportive (the chapter validates this is also legitimate): "I just need to vent for a minute, I'm not asking you to litigate it." Either is fine; both being unspoken is what causes crossed wires.
  4. Name what you are not asking for, when useful. The chapter notes that long-standing supportive mirrors can feel betrayed when honest feedback suddenly arrives. The reverse is also true. If you are switching modes, say so. If you are staying in a mode but the situation looks borderline, say that too.
  5. Apply in reverse when you are the mirror. Before answering a friend's "tell me this isn't true," ask which kind of mirror they need today. Sometimes ask out loud. Sometimes infer and check ("do you want me to tell you what I actually see, or do you want me to be on your side for an hour?"). The same script works in both directions.
sections_html.signal: Both you and the friend can name, in one sentence each, which kind of mirror this conversation is. You asked for honest, they are giving honest, and you are receiving with the curiosity the request implied; or you asked for supportive, they are giving supportive, and you are not later disappointed that they did not push back. The asymmetry-trap (you wanted honest, got reassurance; or you wanted reassurance, got an unprompted honest read) does not occur.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If my friend gives me an answer that aligns with the original feedback rather than against it, do I actually want to hear that, or am I about to be mad?" If the answer is "I'd be mad," you are asking for a supportive mirror. Ask for it on purpose, or change what you are asking for.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if the question your friend just heard, and the answer your friend is preparing to give, are the same kind of thing. If you can predict roughly the shape of the answer (honest read, with examples; or warm reassurance, possibly without specifics) and you welcome that shape, the mirror is matched.
sections_html.failure: Without naming, two failures recur. In the first, you ask the implicit supportive ask, your friend correctly delivers reassurance, and you walk away with confirmation that the original feedback was wrong. The blind spot stays in the dark. Worse, you have now triangulated against the giver of the original feedback, and the mutual friend or colleague who gave it next time will be processed as having an agenda (per the use-your-reaction-as-blind-spot-alert skill, this is the same failure looped). In the second, you ask for one mirror and the friend gives you the other. You wanted reassurance after a hard day; your spouse delivered an honest read of where they think the original feedback was right; the conversation about the feedback merges with a fresh injury about the relationship, and now there are two upset things to deal with instead of one. The chapter notes the betrayal-by-mirror-shift directly: when someone has been a supportive mirror, an unexpected shift to honest can feel like an ambush even when the honest read is true and offered with care.
sections_html.example: The chapter walks through the move at lines 2100-2124. The script for inviting an honest mirror is given verbatim ("Here's feedback I just got. It seems wrong. My first reaction is to reject it. But I wonder if this is feedback in a blind spot? Do you see me doing this sometimes, and if so, when? What impact do you see it having?"). The contrast case is the implicit supportive ask ("He's overreacting, right? He just doesn't understand I've got bigger things to worry about, right?"), where the friend is invited to be on your side and is bound to either oblige (giving you reassurance you already half-distrust) or break the implicit contract (delivering honesty you did not ask for). The chapter's broader point is that close people are hesitant to switch from supportive to honest not out of cowardice but out of confusion and care, so the burden of naming the mirror falls on the receiver who wants the honest read.
sections_html.source: Chapter 4, "See Your Blind Spots," lines 2100-2124, the section "Get a Second Opinion" together with the "Honest Mirrors Versus Supportive Mirrors" subsection. Stone and Heen distinguish the two mirror types, name the implicit-default problem, give the explicit script for inviting honest reflection, and validate supportive reflection as a legitimate but separate request. The skill installs the explicit-naming move on both sides of the conversation.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: negotiate-which-kind-you-need
description: You are about to enter (or are inside) a feedback conversation where you suspect you and the giver are on different tracks about what kind of feedback this is, and you are about to leave the misalignment unspoken and walk out frustrated. The fix is to name the kind you need explicitly and negotiate when the giver has another in mind.

sections_html.when: You can feel the kind-mismatch coming on, or it has already started. Either you are about to walk into a conversation where the giver thinks the topic is one kind of feedback and you actually need another, or you are mid-conversation and the giver is offering, say, coaching while what you need is evaluation (or appreciation, or vice versa). You are about to let the misalignment ride and absorb the wrong thing. Reach for this instead.
sections_html.move:
1. Run the three alignment questions, before or during the conversation: 1. What is my purpose in receiving (or asking for) this feedback? 2. Is that the right purpose from my point of view? 3. Is that the right purpose from the other person's point of view?
  2. Name the kind you want, out loud, in your own words. "I am looking for evaluation, where I stand against my peers." "I would like coaching, specific suggestions on how to do this more efficiently." "I really need appreciation right now; I have been working hard and want to know it is seen."
  3. Ask the giver to say what kind they think they are giving (or were planning to give). If they have not thought about it explicitly, the question itself is the alignment.
  4. If you each want different kinds, surface the difference rather than absorb the wrong one. Use the chapter's principle: "explicit disagreement is better than implicit misunderstanding." Negotiate one of three landings: shift to the kind you need now, hold this conversation for the kind they planned and schedule a separate one for the kind you need, or get a brief version of the kind you need first so you can take in the kind they planned to give.
  5. Use the take-the-bull-by-the-horns scripts when you suspect the kind without being sure: "You are offering coaching, but it would help to get a quick evaluation: am I doing all right overall?" or "You are saying this is coaching, but I am hearing it as evaluation, too. Are you saying I am falling behind?"
sections_html.signal: You and the giver can each say, in a sentence, what kind this conversation is and what kind you need, and the answers either match or are visibly negotiated. You stop trying to extract the kind you need from feedback that is not delivering it (no more reading appreciation for traces of evaluation, no more arguing the content of coaching when you are starving for "you are seen").
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If the giver and I each had to name the kind of feedback this conversation is for, would we give the same answer?" If you do not know, or if you suspect the answer is no and you have not surfaced it, the misalignment is implicit.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if both of you can name the kind, and any disagreement is now an explicit topic rather than the silent reason the conversation feels off.
sections_html.failure: Without naming the kind you need, you accept the kind on offer and try to translate. The translation does not work; appreciation does not answer where-do-I-stand, coaching does not answer am-I-seen, evaluation does not answer how-do-I-improve. You leave the conversation with an unmet need and a giver who feels rebuffed (they offered something thoughtful and you walked away unhappy). Worse, the misalignment becomes a pattern: you stop asking for feedback because "feedback" never gets you what you need, when the actual problem is that you have been asking for the wrong kind, or asking for the right one in language that does not specify it.
sections_html.example: The chapter closes its alignment section with Elsie at the batting cage. Dad has been pitching coaching all morning; Elsie has been melting down. The fix arrives when Dad stops pitching, asks "Elsie, what's up?" and the negotiation surfaces: Elsie has been practicing all week and came to the park expecting to wow him with her improved swing. What she actually needed was appreciation for the week of practice and acknowledgment of her disappointment, not more coaching on stance. Once the kind is named, the conversation becomes possible. The chapter's law-firm round-robin is the same pattern in reverse: April, Cody, and Evelyn each got a kind they did not want because none of them named the kind they did want, and Donald, meaning well, defaulted to whichever kind he found easiest in the moment.
sections_html.source: Chapter 2, "Separate Appreciation, Coaching, and Evaluation," lines 1031-1052, "Get Aligned: Know the Purpose and Discuss It." Stone and Heen offer the three purpose-questions, the periodic mid-conversation check ("I'm intending to give you coaching. Is that how you're hearing it?"), the principle "explicit disagreement is better than implicit misunderstanding," and the receiver's bull-by-the-horns scripts. The skill installs the explicit-naming move as the receiver's primary tool against cross-transactions.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: negotiate-your-internal-voice
description: You are about to receive difficult feedback, or you are in the middle of a feedback conversation and you cannot listen because your internal voice is running a defense. The chapter prescribes a specific move for working with your internal voice rather than against it, turning it from a bodyguard into a source of curiosity.

sections_html.when: You are in a feedback conversation and cannot listen. Your internal voice is too loud: it is making arguments against the feedback, defending your record, cataloguing the giver's faults, or cycling through how unfair this is. Or you are preparing for a difficult feedback conversation and want to give yourself the best chance of genuinely hearing what is said.
sections_html.move:
Do not suppress the internal voice. Give it attention. The chapter's counterintuitive instruction: your internal voice gets loud because it wants your attention. If you try to tamp it down ("think whatever you want, but keep quiet"), it doubles down. If you give it your full attention, it quiets. The first step is to tune in to what it is actually saying.

Identify the trigger pattern. When you are triggered, your internal voice does not say random things. It says specific and predictable things organized around the type of trigger that has fired. The chapter's taxonomy:

  • Truth trigger patterns: "That's wrong!" / "That's not helpful!" / "That's not me!"
  • Relationship trigger patterns: "After all I've done for you?!" / "Who are you to say?" / "You're the problem, not me."
  • Identity trigger patterns: "I screw up everything." / "I'm doomed." / "I'm not a bad person — or am I?"

Recognizing the pattern gives you traction. You know what your internal voice is protecting against and why it's talking.

Negotiate it toward curiosity. The chapter prescribes an explicit internal dialogue. Below is the model (the chapter's own dialogue is the illustration):

You: "During this conversation, you're going to be telling me this feedback is wrong."

Internal Voice: "Right. Because it will be."

You: "What might be right about what they're saying?"

Internal Voice: "Well, they might see things I don't. Their interpretation could be different but valid."

You: "So there's something to listen for."

The strategy is to acknowledge and appreciate the internal voice (these are your own thoughts; dismissing them is dismissing yourself), remind it that understanding doesn't equal agreeing, and give it an assignment: "I need you to be intensely curious about what they're saying. Help me dig in and understand. What's right about what they're saying? Why is it that they see things differently?"

Watch for hot inquiry. A specific failure mode in the listening phase: when you are upset, your "questions" may become assertions disguised as questions. "Do you actually believe that?" is not a question; it is a frustrated assault with a question mark. Sarcasm and cross-examination ("But isn't it true that...?") are the same. If you notice that your inquiry has gotten hot, stop trying to inquire and assert instead. Replace: "Do you actually think what you're saying is consistent or fair?" with: "What you're suggesting seems inconsistent with the criteria used for others in my position. That doesn't seem fair to me." Clear assertion is more honest than hot inquiry and makes listening easier afterward.
sections_html.signal: During preparation: you have had the internal negotiation, acknowledged what your internal voice is defending against, and given it a curiosity assignment. During the conversation: your questions are genuine rather than heated. You are asking what the giver means rather than cross-examining their credibility.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "if I imagine hearing the worst-case feedback, what does my internal voice start saying?" Naming the anticipated pattern before the conversation gives you advance knowledge of the bodyguard's moves.

After: ask "during the conversation, did I hear anything the giver said that I had not already decided to believe?" If yes, genuine listening happened. If no, the internal voice ran the whole encounter.
sections_html.failure: Without working with the internal voice, triggered listening collapses into the chapter's description: you miss what the giver says after the first triggering sentence because your internal voice is still arguing against it. Your fellow board member says something, you think "that's not true," and while your internal voice is building the counter-argument, the board member has moved to a second point that you now also miss. The chapter notes that this is not a matter of will: the neuroscience research shows that when we feel unfairly treated, our mirror neuron empathy response may be neurologically turned off. Skillful listening during triggered feedback is not natural; it has to be both on purpose and with a purpose.
sections_html.example: The chapter provides the internal voice dialogue as the worked illustration. The key exchange: when the internal voice insists "all the usual things will be wrong," the prompt is not to argue with it or silence it but to ask "what might be right about what they're saying?" This invites the internal voice into the curiosity project rather than pitting you against it. The voice offers: "They've been around the block a few times. Their interpretation may be different but valid." That is enough curiosity to begin genuine listening.
sections_html.source: Chapter 11, "Navigate the Conversation," lines 5110-5165 (listening's challenges: the internal voice as personal assistant, the triggered-to-bodyguard escalation, the empathy shutdown research, listening with a purpose), 5171-5264 (preparing to listen: trigger patterns mapped to internal voice patter, the negotiation dialogue, the curiosity assignment), 5287-5296 (hot inquiry: when to stop trying to inquire and assert instead).
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/catch-the-wrong-spotting — the equivalent move applied to your own thinking (not the conversation); this skill targets the conversational listening dynamic

name: notice-the-distortion-type
description: You received feedback and your story about what it means feels outsized or catastrophic. The chapter names three predictable patterns by which strong emotion distorts the stories we tell about feedback, and the diagnostic step is to name which one is running before attempting to correct it.

sources.chapter: 7. Learn How Wiring and Temperament Affect Your Story

sections_html.when: You received feedback and your story about what it means is escalating past what the feedback literally said. You notice thoughts like "I can't do anything right" or "this is how it has always been" or "this will never get better" or "everyone on the team thinks this about me." The feedback was specific and contained, but your story about it is not. Or you find yourself replaying every past failure since childhood in response to a comment about one recent meeting.
sections_html.move:
Name which of the three distortion patterns is running. The chapter's framing: "Strong feelings push us toward extreme interpretations." Naming the pattern does not fix it (the remedies are in the next chapter), but you cannot dismantle what you have not identified. The three patterns:

Google bias (the past is being distorted). Upsetting feedback triggers a search of your memory that returns only the confirming evidence: every past failure, every bad choice, every thing you regret. You feel as though your entire history is a record of incompetence or failure, even though nothing new has happened to that history. The chapter's formulation: "When you feel lousy about yourself, you are effectively Googling 'Things that are wrong with me.'" Signal of this pattern: you are thinking about past events that have no direct connection to the specific feedback you just received. The current feedback is an occasion for a search, and the search is returning results across all of your history rather than being contained to the relevant domain.

Flooding (the present is being distorted). Negative feedback on one thing overflows its container and floods into your general self-image and your read of all your relationships. "I sing off key" becomes "I can't do anything right." "My colleague raised a concern" becomes "everyone on the team hates me." Specific and partial feedback becomes total and universal. Signal of this pattern: the scope of your story has expanded beyond the domain of the feedback. The feedback was about one skill, one interaction, one project, and your story is now about who you fundamentally are or how everyone sees you.

Forever bias and snowballing (the future is being distorted). A current bad feeling is projected forward as permanent: you feel humiliated now, so you will always feel humiliated. A specific piece of negative feedback snowballs into a chain of disasters: "I had mayonnaise on my cheek during the date" becomes "I will die alone." Signal of this pattern: you are narrating a sequence of future events, each one following from the last, that did not happen and that the feedback did not predict. Or you believe your current emotional state will continue indefinitely. The chapter: "When we feel bad, we assume we will always feel bad."

Once you have named the pattern, note it plainly: "I'm Google-biasing," or "I'm flooding the scope," or "I'm snowballing." This creates enough distance between you and the story to allow the next step.
sections_html.signal: You can name the pattern in a single phrase. The story no longer feels like an accurate read of the feedback; it feels like a recognizable distortion type that your nervous system is running. The feedback is still visible underneath the distortion: you can say what the feedback actually said, separately from what your story made of it.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "is my story about the feedback larger in scope, darker in history, or more dire about the future than the feedback itself?" If yes, at least one distortion type is running. If you are unsure, restate the feedback in its original words and compare it to your current story.

After: ask "which pattern is distorting which time frame?" If you can name one of the three (past, present, or future) and label it, the diagnostic has landed. Note that two or all three patterns can run simultaneously, especially when the wiring profile involves high swing and slow recovery.
sections_html.failure: Without naming the distortion type, the distorted story continues to feel like the accurate interpretation of the feedback. The chapter is explicit: "What's so amazing about these distortions is how real they appear to us in the moment." The gap between thoughts and reality does not make itself visible; you are inside the story, not observing it. A person in the grip of the Google bias genuinely cannot remember anything they've ever done right. A person flooding cannot hold onto the fact that the feedback was about one specific behavior, not their entire character. A person snowballing feels that their catastrophic projection is calm analysis. Naming the type does not make the emotion stop, but it creates the vantage point from which it can eventually be dismantled.
sections_html.example: The chapter uses Marc's account of the Google bias: "The feedback could be small, but if I'm feeling vulnerable, it's as if I fall through the floor, plunged into the basement where all the things I've ever regretted are collected... I feel guilty about the people I've hurt and ashamed of the selfish things I've done. When I'm not in the basement I literally don't think about it. But when I'm there, it's the only reality." Marc's report shows the diagnostic signal clearly: the current feedback is small; the memory search it triggers is the entire ledger of his past. The pattern is Google bias. The chapter also offers the flooding description (singing off key becoming "I can't do anything right") and the snowballing chain ("I had mayonnaise on my cheek during the date" to "I will die alone") as two additional named examples.
sections_html.source: Chapter 7, "Learn How Wiring and Temperament Affect Your Story," lines 3460-3508 (the general introduction to how feelings distort stories, the thoughts-plus-feelings soundtrack mechanism, and the statement that there are two ways in), 3514-3535 (the Google bias), 3541-3548 (flooding: one thing becomes everything), 3554-3563 (forever bias and snowballing). Stone and Heen describe these three patterns in chapter 7 and defer the remedies explicitly to chapter 8 ("Dismantle Distortions"). The skill captures the diagnostic move, which must precede any dismantling work.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: precommit-to-riding-the-j-curve
description: You have started to implement feedback-driven change and things are getting worse, not better. The chapter names this the J curve pattern and prescribes two categories of precommitment strategies to prevent your future self from abandoning the change before you have given it a fair test.

sources.chapter: 12. Get Going (Five Ways to Take Action)

sections_html.when: You have decided to implement feedback and act on a change, but you keep finding yourself reverting to old behavior. Or you have started a change and things feel worse, not better, and you are wondering whether to abandon it. Or you are planning a change and want to increase the probability that you will follow through.
sections_html.move:
Recognize the J curve. When implementing any feedback-driven change, a common trajectory is: you start somewhere in the middle (your status quo), things get worse as you begin to change (it is awkward, you are less skilled, the discomfort is new), and then, eventually, things get better than the starting point. From the inside of the dip, you cannot tell whether you are at the bottom of the J about to rise or heading further down. This uncertainty is what causes people to abandon changes just as they are about to become beneficial. The chapter: "You are likely to get worse before you get better. And more important, you are likely to feel worse before you feel better." Knowing this is true in advance changes how you interpret the dip.

The two-decision-maker problem. Your Sunday Night self makes a commitment; your Monday Morning self faces a choice that your Sunday Night self would not have approved of. Sunday Night guy is gone when Monday Morning guy arrives, and Monday Morning guy prioritizes immediate comfort over the commitments made the night before. The question is: how can Sunday Night guy bind Monday Morning guy to the agreement? There are two strategies: increase the appeal of change (make it easier or more rewarding), or increase the cost of not changing (make reversal more expensive).

Increase the appeal of change.

  1. Make it social. Doing the change alongside someone else makes it less unpleasant, adds accountability (you might let yourself down but not your partner), and provides appreciation for your efforts. Online communities can provide the same accountability benefits without requiring in-person contact.
  1. Keep score. Tracking progress turns behavior change into a game. Miyamoto charted his jogging and swimming on the bathroom wall; Bernardus tracked the number of customers successfully entered in the new database. The chapter draws on video game design: intersperse new challenges with moments of mastery to avoid exhaustion and maintain motivation. Ask: can you make your progress measurable in some way, playful, or competitively engaging?

Increase the cost of not changing.

Tie yourself to the mast (precommit). Change the terms of the choice before you arrive at the temptation, so that Monday Morning guy faces a different cost structure than you could have predicted on Sunday Night. Schelling stopped smoking by threatening himself with a donation to an organization he found morally repugnant if he continued. Doctors with drug addictions wrote confessional letters to the medical board, sealed them, and entrusted them to a friend who would mail them if they relapsed. The stakes change the siren song of the muffin.

Commit to a specific duration. When starting the change, commit in advance to trying it for a specific period that reaches past the J curve's dip. The chapter: "Give it two weeks, thirty days, a fiscal year—whatever seems like a reasonable duration to test whether this new behavior might actually help." This is the structural form of riding out the J: you are committing to reaching the upswing before evaluating the change's value.
sections_html.signal: You have designed at least one precommitment strategy before beginning the change (social partner, score-keeping mechanism, or raised cost of reversal). You have named a duration commitment. When you hit the dip and feel like abandoning the change, you can name the J curve and remind yourself that you are in the dip, not heading indefinitely downward.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "what is the most likely moment when I will want to abandon this change?" If you can name it (it's when I get to day 5 and it still feels awkward), you can design a precommitment for that moment.

After: ask "when things got worse, did I keep going?" If yes, you may be in the upswing. If you abandoned the change, ask whether you gave it the duration you committed to.
sections_html.failure: Without the J curve framing, the dip looks like failure and gives you rational grounds to stop. The chapter notes that the dip is predictable, not evidence that the change was wrong. Bernardus's first few weeks with the database were a minor disaster; six months later he could not imagine working without it. If he had used the early performance drop as evidence to return to paper notes, he would have made a decision based on data from the worst phase of the adoption. The J curve gives you a frame to discount that data appropriately and commit to reaching the upswing.
sections_html.source: Chapter 12, "Get Going," lines 5846-5855 (the Bernardus/database setup), 5861-5870 (the two-decision-maker problem: Sunday Night and Monday Morning guy), 5872-5917 (increase appeal: make it social, keep score), 5923-5940 (increase cost: tie yourself to the mast), 5947-5972 (recognize the J curve; commit to duration; Bernardus's successful adoption).
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/try-small-experiments — the prior step; this skill handles what to do after the experiment starts and hits the J-curve dip

name: pull-not-push-reroute
description: You are receiving feedback that is poorly delivered, ill-timed, or from someone whose qualifications you doubt, and you find your attention drifting into a private catalogue of the giver's inadequacies. The internal drift feels justified but is leaving you with nothing usable.

sections_html.when: You are mid-feedback, or replaying a feedback conversation afterward, and your mind is producing a running list of why the giver is not qualified to evaluate you, what they got wrong about your situation, how badly they delivered it. The list feels accurate. It is also a sealed loop that produces no usable change. Reach for this whenever you notice that loop running.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice the inventory. The signal is a string of sentences whose subject is the giver, not the work or the relationship: "she doesn't even understand what I do," "his timing was awful," "they have no standing to say this."
  2. Stop the inventory and ask the rerouting question: "Granting everything in that list is true, what is here that I could pull?" Pull means drive your own learning, manage your resistance, find any insight that might help, ask for what you need, or stand up for how you see it.
  3. Generate at least one receiver-side action: a question to ask, a piece of the feedback to take seriously even if the rest is wrong, a clarification to seek, a boundary to name, an experiment to run.
  4. Decide what to do with the giver-inventory separately. It may be valid, but it is now a separate file (e.g., a relationship issue to handle later, a feedback-quality complaint to raise on its own).
sections_html.signal: You finish the encounter with at least one concrete next move on your side, written in the first person. The giver-inventory may still exist, but it is no longer the only output. You can answer "what did I get from this?" with something other than a critique of the giver.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask yourself: "If I had to write down what I am taking away from this feedback, what would I write?" If the answer is mostly about what is wrong with the giver or how badly they delivered it, you are inside the inventory.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer now contains at least one receiver-side action item, even if the giver-criticism still stands alongside it.
sections_html.failure: Without rerouting, you spend the encounter and the hours after it perfecting the case against the giver. The case may be airtight. It also leaves you with no learning, no clearer asks, no boundaries set, and no decisions made. The receiver controls intake, sense-making, and change, so when attention is parked on the giver's deficiencies, the leverage point sits idle. You walk away validated and unchanged, which is the receiver-side equivalent of the giver pushing harder and getting nowhere.
sections_html.example: The book opens the introduction by noting that when people describe difficult feedback, both parties slip into the same move. As givers, we notice the receiver isn't good at receiving. As receivers, we notice the giver isn't good at giving. The intro names the receiver's version of this loop directly: Tom's boss gives him a dressing-down about his organizational skills, and on the drive home Tom silently catalogues his boss's inadequacies, even pulling over to write the list down. The reroute is the move Tom does not make.
sections_html.source: Introduction "From Push to Pull," lines 367-386. Stone and Heen frame the receiver, not the giver, as the leverage point: "the receivers are in control of what they do and don't let in, how they make sense of what they're hearing, and whether they choose to change," and so "the real leverage is creating pull." The reroute is the moment-to-moment application of the book's central reframing.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: dismantle-a-distorted-story
description: You received feedback and your emotional reaction is larger than the feedback's content warrants, or your story about what the feedback means has expanded far beyond what was said. You cannot assess the feedback fairly while the distortion is running. The four-step chain restores accurate perception before you decide what to do with the feedback.

sources.chapter: 7. Learn How Wiring and Temperament Affect Your Story
sources.note: Chapter 7 establishes the two foundations: your wiring profile explains why reactions differ in intensity; the three distortion types (Google bias, flooding, forever bias) name the specific patterns that amplify feedback beyond its actual size.

Chapter 8 supplies the two active steps: separating feeling/story/feedback into three strands, and containing the story within its actual scope using the containment chart, balancing picture, and right-sizing tools.

sections_html.when: You received feedback and your reaction is noticeably stronger than the content seems to warrant. Or you realize that your story about what the feedback means has ballooned: what started as "you run late" is now "I am a bad doctor who doesn't care about patients." You cannot productively engage with what the feedback says or decide whether to act on it until you can see it at actual size. Reach for this before deciding whether to accept, reject, or act on any feedback that has triggered a strong reaction.
sections_html.move:
1. Map your wiring profile. Apply understand-your-wiring-profile. Before engaging with the content of the feedback, name your baseline (high or low), swing (narrow or wide), and sustain/recovery (fast or slow). This step is about attributing your emotional reaction correctly: some fraction of its intensity belongs to the feedback's content; some fraction belongs to your nervous system's built-in configuration. Naming the profile separates "how I experience this kind of feedback" from "how bad the feedback actually is."

Step 1 must come first because without it, steps 2-4 will be applied from inside a distorted emotional state with no recognition that the state is partly wiring-driven. A slow-recovery person who does not know they are a slow-recovery person will mistake their extended distress for evidence that the feedback is catastrophic, and the containment work of steps 3-4 will be undermined from the start.

2. Name the distortion type. Apply notice-the-distortion-type. Now that you know the reaction is partly wiring, identify which of the three distortion patterns is running: Google bias (past is being flooded with confirming failures), flooding (one specific feedback overflows into all-or-nothing present self-image), or forever bias and snowballing (current feeling projected as permanent or as the first domino in a cascade). Write down the pattern in one sentence: "I'm Google-biasing" or "I'm flooding the scope" or "I'm snowballing into the future."

Step 2 must come before steps 3-4 because the distortion type names what is happening before you try to do anything about it. You cannot pull apart strands you have not identified, and you cannot contain a story whose shape you have not named.

3. Separate feeling, story, and feedback. Apply separate-feeling-story-feedback. Ask and answer three questions in sequence, writing each answer before moving to the next: (a) What do I feel? (name the emotion and describe it physically.) (b) What is the story I am telling, and inside the story, what is the threat? (listen to it without judging it.) (c) What was the actual feedback? (the exact words, without the story added.) If the reaction connects to older material (last-straw dynamic, open wound), name that too: "this is also activating a ten-year history of being told I'm irresponsible."

Step 3 must come before step 4 because containment has to know what to contain. If the actual feedback is still merged with the story and the feeling, the containment work will contain the wrong thing. The chapter makes this explicit: "you have to be clear about what you've added, and be aware of your patterns over time for the kinds of things you tend to add."

4. Contain the story to actual size. Apply contain-the-story. Now that the strands are separated and the actual feedback is visible, restore the three containment laws the distortion violated. Use whichever of the three tools fits the distortion type identified in step 2: Feedback Containment Chart (what this IS about / what it is NOT about) for flooding and Google-bias patterns; Balancing Picture for flooding when visual proportion is the issue; Right-sizing future consequences for snowballing and forever-bias patterns. For each tool, write your actual answers. The process is not complete until you can state the actual feedback in one sentence that fits inside the left column of the containment chart.
sections_html.signal: You can write the actual feedback in one sentence. The sentence fits in the left column of a containment chart. The threat inside your story has been named and distinguished from what the feedback actually said. You can name which wiring tendencies amplified the reaction and which distortion pattern ran. The feedback now feels significantly smaller than it did when you started.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "if I had to write down exactly what the feedback said in one sentence, could I do it without the sentence expanding into my story about what it means?" If no, at least one of the four steps has not completed.

After: ask "can I name three things separately: what I feel, what my story about the feedback is, and what the feedback actually said?" If yes, step 3 has landed. Then: "does my story stay inside the domain of the feedback, or does it cover my whole past, present, and future?" If it stays inside the domain, step 4 has landed.
sections_html.failure: Without the chain, the distorted story substitutes for the feedback in every evaluation and decision that follows. You either accept the distorted story as accurate (and are devastated by a one-line comment in a patient survey) or reject the distorted story wholesale (and miss the actionable signal it contained). The chapter: "What's so amazing about these distortions is how real they appear to us in the moment... unless we are consciously looking for it, we can't see the gap when we're in it." The chain is the looking.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of feedback/understand-your-wiring-profile, feedback/notice-the-distortion-type, feedback/separate-feeling-story-feedback, feedback/contain-the-story.
  • See also feedback/change-your-vantage-point (an additional dismantling strategy that works through perspective-shift rather than scope-containment; useful when steps 3-4 are blocked because you cannot get outside the story by reasoning alone).
  • See also feedback/know-your-feedback-footprint (names your reactive behavioral patterns; parallel to step 1's wiring profile, at the behavioral level rather than the physiological level).
sections_html.source: Chain assembled from chapters 7 and 8 of Stone and Heen's "Thanks for the Feedback." Chapter 7 provides the wiring profile vocabulary (baseline, swing, sustain/recovery) and the three distortion types (Google bias, flooding, forever bias). Chapter 8 provides the strand-separation procedure and the three containment tools.

name: navigate-a-feedback-conversation
description: A feedback conversation is about to happen or is already underway, and you want to maximize what you learn from it without either shutting down or capitulating to everything the giver says. The four-step chain runs the conversation's arc (open, listen, assert, close) with an emergency rescue move for when it gets stuck.

sources.note: Chapter 11 presents the conversation as an open-body-close arc and names four body skills (listening, asserting, process moves, problem solving). The recipe follows that structure directly, with make-a-process-move and dig-for-interests-then-generate-options folded in as the rescue moves for two different stuck states.

sections_html.when: You are about to enter or are already in a feedback conversation — scheduled or spontaneous — and you want to handle it well. You want to genuinely learn something from it, say what needs to be said from your side, and end with clarity about what happens next. Reach for this as a mental framework before any high-stakes feedback conversation, or as a course-correction map during one that is going sideways.
sections_html.move:
1. Open by getting aligned. Apply align-on-conversation-purpose. Before engaging with content, clarify three things: Is this feedback, and if so what kind? Who decides? Is the outcome still open to influence? If the conversation has already started without this framing, step outside the content to establish it: "Can we take a minute to step back so I'm clear on our purposes?" This is not about substance; it is about the conversation's structure.

Step 1 must come first because without it you cannot make conscious choices about how to respond. You may argue against a decision that is already final, or accept coaching passively when you needed evaluation clarity first. The Gottman research makes the cost of skipping explicit: a harsh opening that is not corrected predicts a negative outcome 96 percent of the time.

2. Listen by negotiating your internal voice toward curiosity. Apply negotiate-your-internal-voice. Before and during the conversation, work with your internal voice rather than against it. Give it attention (it quiets when heard). Identify its trigger pattern (truth: "that's wrong!"; relationship: "after all I've done for you!"; identity: "I screw up everything."). Ask it: "What might be right about what they're saying?" and give it the assignment to find out. During the conversation, watch for hot inquiry (questions that are actually heated assertions) and replace them with clear assertions.

Step 2 must come after step 1 because knowing the type of conversation you are in (step 1) gives the internal voice negotiation in step 2 a specific target: what is there to listen for in this kind of conversation? The negotiation becomes "what might I learn about my performance in this role" rather than an undirected instruction to be curious.

3. Assert what's left out, not who's right. Apply assert-as-left-out. When you have something to say, frame it as a missing puzzle piece rather than as a replacement truth. "I disagree with that advice" rather than "that advice is wrong." "What's left out is my data / my interpretation / my feeling." For relationship-trigger assertions: name both topics on separate tracks. For identity-trigger assertions: be transparent about being overwhelmed rather than either collapsing ("it's true, I'm hopeless") or denying ("that's ridiculous, I'm not that kind of person").

Rescue move A: When the conversation is stuck on process, make a process move. Apply make-a-process-move. Step outside the conversation's content and diagnose what is stuck (both arguing without listening; two tangled topics; perspectives treated as facts; emotional flooding; deadlock). Name it out loud. Propose a next step. This is the emergency rescue that converts a stuck escalation back into a productive exchange. Use it whenever you notice the conversation is cycling rather than progressing.

Rescue move B: When the conversation is stuck on solutions, dig for interests. Apply dig-for-interests-then-generate-options. When you have reached agreement that a problem exists but are deadlocked on what to do about it, move from positions to interests. Ask what the giver is actually trying to accomplish (three buckets: helping you, helping themselves/the relationship, helping others/the system). Then generate options that address those interests. This rescue move applies specifically after the core conversation has produced understanding and reached a solution impasse.

4. Close with commitment. Apply close-with-commitment. Before parting, spend one to three sentences naming what was agreed and what each party will do next. It does not require agreeing with the feedback or promising to change. It can commit to gathering more information, returning to the conversation, or stating which parts of the feedback you have decided not to take and why. The goal is that both parties leave with the same understanding of where things stand.
sections_html.signal: After step 1: you both know what kind of conversation this is and who makes decisions. After step 2: you can name something the giver said that you did not already know or had not already decided to believe. After step 3: you said something specific about your data, interpretation, or feeling that the giver did not know before you said it. After step 4: both parties can name, in the same words, what was agreed and what comes next.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "do I know what kind of feedback this is, whether I can influence the outcome, and who decides?" If you cannot answer all three, step 1 has not been done.

After: ask "did I learn something in this conversation that I did not already know?" If yes, step 2 worked. "Did I say something the giver did not know before I said it?" If yes, step 3 worked. "Do I know what we each agreed to?" If yes, step 4 worked.
sections_html.failure: Without the chain, feedback conversations end in one of the three failure modes the chapter's version-one/two/three case illustrates. Version one: you assert that the outcome is unfair before you understand the criteria, triggering a fight about fairness. Version two: you accept passively and passive-aggressively ("I think 4 is a little low, but I suppose it's fine"), producing nothing. Version three: you ask whether the outcome can be changed before you understand the reasoning, and hear "no" before you have the information to argue for anything. The chain produces version four: you ask questions to understand, assert your view after gathering data, and close with a specific next step.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of feedback/align-on-conversation-purpose, feedback/negotiate-your-internal-voice, feedback/assert-as-left-out, feedback/make-a-process-move, feedback/dig-for-interests-then-generate-options, feedback/close-with-commitment.
  • See also feedback/spot-the-switchtrack (the diagnostic for when the conversation is stuck because it has split into two topics; make-a-process-move is the generic course-correction; spot-the-switchtrack is the specific diagnosis when the stuck pattern is a switchtrack).
sections_html.source: Chain assembled from chapter 11 of Stone and Heen's "Thanks for the Feedback." The authors structure the chapter as an explicit open-body-close arc with four body skills. The recipe follows that structure directly. The performance-rating conversation is the chapter's own synthesis example.

name: respond-to-feedback-in-a-relationship-system
description: You received feedback and both you and the giver are pointing at each other as the cause of the problem. The blame loop is stuck. The three-step chain breaks the loop by first seeing the larger system producing the problem, then introducing your own accountability in a sequence the giver can hear, then describing what the giver could do differently in terms that serve your change.

sources.chapter: 6. Identify the Relationship System (Take Three Steps Back)
sources.note: Chapter 6 provides all three steps as a sequence. The three-steps-back framework, the two-message sequencing rule, and the ask-what-would-help-you-change move are presented together as the chapter's theory-then-practice structure.

sections_html.when: You are in a feedback relationship where both parties are stuck blaming each other. The giver is pointing at what you did to cause the problem; you are pointing at what they did. The feedback loop has not produced any change because each person believes the other is the primary cause. The conversation ends in stalemate every time. Reach for this when you can clearly articulate the other person's contribution to the problem and are finding it much harder to articulate your own.
sections_html.move:
1. See the system before speaking. Apply take-three-steps-back privately — before any conversation with the giver. Work through three views of the relationship system:

  • One step back: What is the specific you-and-me combination of tendencies that produces the downward spiral? Name the cycle: your behavior A → their reaction B → your behavior A (intensified). Sandy's comfort spending + Gil's monitoring panic = each triggers the other's behavior. Name your part in the cycle.
  • Two steps back: Are there role-structure tensions that would create friction between any two people in these roles regardless of character? If so, some of the feedback is structural, not personal.
  • Three steps back: What other players, processes, or structural factors are also shaping the choices you each make?

Finally, ask "circling back to me": what am I doing or failing to do that contributes to this dynamic?

Step 1 must come first because you cannot introduce the system (step 2) or ask for what would help you change (step 3) without first having genuinely seen the system. If you skip step 1 and go straight to "let's look at the system together," it reads as an excuse because you have not yet done the work of finding your own part. The chapter is explicit: the giver will hear "relationship system" talk as deflection unless accountability comes first.

2. Take responsibility before introducing the system. Apply take-responsibility-first-then-introduce-the-system in the conversation. Deliver two messages, in order:

  • Message one (settle first): Name your specific contribution to the problem clearly and without burying it in qualifications. Wait until the giver has heard this and registered it before proceeding.
  • Message two (introduce after message one is settled): Raise your observation about the broader system using the specific you-and-me cycle, role factor, or structural factor you identified in step 1. Use plain language: "I've also noticed that when [their behavior] happens, my reaction is [your behavior], which I think makes things worse rather than better." Do not use "relationship system" language.

Step 2 must come before step 3 because ask-what-would-help-you-change is the move for when the giver cannot or will not hear message two. If you try step 3 before settling message one, it reads as an attempt to negotiate a trade (I'll take responsibility if you admit your part) rather than a genuine request. The chapter: "They'll assume you're attempting to deflect the feedback and point a finger back at them."

3. Ask what would help you change, if the giver cannot hear the system. Apply ask-what-would-help-you-change when the giver is still unable to acknowledge their contribution after step 2. Rather than insisting they admit their role, describe specifically what they could do differently that would help you change. Use the chapter's structure: (a) name your reaction, (b) own that it is out of proportion or counterproductive, (c) name what triggers it, (d) ask for the specific behavior change that would help. Gil's script: "I know I'm overreacting sometimes and I'm working on that. It would help me if you would be willing to be upfront with me about the Grape-Nuts and the mochaccino mini-vacations, and we can budget for them together."

Step 3 must come after step 2 because the ask-what-would-help framing only works if your accountability is already settled. If your own contribution is not yet on the table, a "here's what would help me change" request sounds like a condition for taking responsibility, not a genuine description of what you need.
sections_html.signal: After step 1: you can name something you are doing that contributes to the cycle, in plain language, without reference to what the other person is doing. After step 2: the giver has heard message one (their response indicates they believe you are taking responsibility) and has at least partially heard message two. After step 3 (if needed): the conversation has produced at least one specific thing the giver could do that both parties understand is genuinely in service of your change.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "can I name, without mentioning the other person, something I have done or failed to do that contributed to this problem?" If yes, step 1 has produced its key output and step 2 is ready to proceed.

After: ask "did the giver hear message one (my accountability) as genuine rather than as preamble to message two (my system observation)?" If yes, the sequencing in step 2 worked. If no, either message one was under-delivered or message two arrived too quickly.
sections_html.failure: Without the chain, the blame loop continues indefinitely. Sandy hides the Grape-Nuts; Gil finds them and confronts her; Gil clamps down harder; Sandy hides more things. Both genuinely believe the other is causing the problem. Neither sees the cycle. The feedback exchange between them ends in stalemate every time because each is bringing only message two (the system observation) without message one (settled accountability). The chapter's label for the downstream version of this failure: "fixes that fail" — solutions aimed at one component of the system that generate new problems elsewhere.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of feedback/take-three-steps-back, feedback/take-responsibility-first-then-introduce-the-system, feedback/ask-what-would-help-you-change.
  • See also feedback/spot-the-shifter-or-absorber-pattern (the diagnostic for whether the accountability conversation is stuck because you or the other party is running an absorber or shifter pattern; useful when step 2 is not landing and the cause is an accountability pattern rather than sequencing).
sections_html.source: Chain assembled from chapter 6 of Stone and Heen's "Thanks for the Feedback." The three-steps-back framework (the system view), the take-responsibility-first sequencing rule, and the ask-what-would-help-you-change technique are presented by the authors as the chapter's three-part practical arc: see the system, sequence your accountability messages, and provide a forward path that does not require the giver to see their part before anything can change.

name: separate-data-from-interpretation
description: Feedback has been delivered as a judgment ("you are not a team player," "your report was confusing," "you barked at the kids"), and you are about to either argue the judgment on its terms or accept it on its terms. You have not asked what the giver actually saw or heard underneath their conclusion.

sections_html.when: You have just received a piece of feedback that is phrased as a verdict. It tells you what kind of person you are, what kind of work you produced, what kind of impact you had. You are about to engage with the verdict directly: defending against it, conceding it, or filing it. Reach for this before you do, because the verdict is two layers deep and you are about to act on the surface layer.
sections_html.move:
1. Treat any feedback judgment as a two-step product. The chapter's claim: feedback givers arrive at their labels in two steps. They observe data (what they saw, heard, felt, smelled, remembered, read), and they interpret the data (the story they told about what it meant). The verdict you receive is the second step packaged as the whole.
  2. Ask for the data first. Some version of "what specifically did you see or hear that led you to that," or "what is an example you have in mind," or "walk me through a time when this happened." The chapter is clear that the data is not facts and figures only; it includes behaviors, statements, tone, body language, work products, even the giver's own emotional reactions ("when you didn't email me back, I was frustrated").
  3. Push past the second-order interpretation that the giver may offer in place of data. The chapter flags this directly: when Nicholas asks Adrianna for the data behind "more assertive," her first answer is "what I saw on the sales floor is that you were too laid back." That is still interpretation, not data. The skill calls for one more push: "what about my tone, my body language? Show me." The data you are after is the raw observation, not the next-level-down judgment.
  4. Then ask for the interpretation explicitly. "And what did that mean to you? What conclusion did you draw from it?" The chapter is emphatic that the goal is not to dismiss the interpretation. Both layers matter. You are after a clear picture of both, side by side.
  5. Notice your own confusion of the two. The chapter folds in the receiver's parallel mistake: even when you describe "data" to yourself you may be storing interpretations as data. Their example: "you barked at the kids" — barking is itself an interpretation; the data is the actual sound of what you said. The skill applies in both directions: when you ask the giver for data, and when you watch your own mind treat its own interpretations as facts.
sections_html.signal: You can describe, in two sentences, the data the giver observed and the interpretation they drew from it, as separate things. If a third party asked "what did your boss actually see," you could answer with the observation, not with the conclusion. If they asked "and what did your boss make of that," you could answer with the conclusion, not pretend it does not exist. The two-layer structure is visible to you and can be discussed.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to write down what specifically the giver saw or heard, separately from what they made of it, could I?" If the only answer is the verdict ("they think I'm not a team player"), the layers have not been separated.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can name the data in their words ("she heard me tell Gus I was too busy to help") and the interpretation in their words ("which she read as me prioritizing my own work over the team's"), and you can locate where you agree or disagree at each layer rather than at the verdict alone.
sections_html.failure: Without the separation, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you argue the verdict directly: "I am too a team player, here are five examples." The giver experiences this as denial of their lived observation, because the data they actually saw is still sitting underneath unrefuted. The argument heats up and neither of you is talking about the same thing. In the second, you concede the verdict because it sounds plausible or because you do not want to fight, and you end up agreeing to a conclusion whose underlying observations you have never seen. Six months later you discover the data was a single conversation taken out of context, or an event you remember differently, and the verdict has hardened into received truth in your file. Both failures share the same skipped step: the conclusion was treated as the message, when in fact the conclusion was a person's reading of a specific set of observations, both of which deserved their own discussion.
sections_html.example: The chapter walks through Nicholas and Adrianna in detail. Adrianna gives the label "be more assertive." When Nicholas asks for the data, her first move is to describe him as "too laid back" — itself an interpretation. Nicholas pushes one more level: "It's my tone? What about my tone? My body language? Show me." Only at this level does the actual observation start to surface. The chapter's broader point lands through the parenting-at-the-next-table example: when we say we "saw people parenting badly," the data was actually the way the woman looked at the baby or how the man responded when the baby wailed; "bad parenting" was the auto-story, not the observation. Paul and Monisha's conversation at the end of the chapter shows the move from the receiver's side: when Monisha says she sees "a genuine attitude problem," Paul asks "what do you mean by messaging versus attitude?" and then asks her to walk through a specific example (the ethics workshops), opening up both the data and the interpretation behind her label.
sections_html.source: Chapter 3, "First Understand," lines 1281-1341 ("Ask Where the Feedback Is Coming From," with the "They Observe Data," "They Interpret the Data," and "They Confuse Data and Interpretation (We All Do)" subsections). Stone and Heen lay out the two-step decomposition, give it the explicit Roger Schank framing about human intelligence being organized around stories rather than data, and provide the Nicholas/Adrianna script as the receiver-side move. The skill installs the unpacking move: under any judgment label sit two layers, and both must be made discussable for the feedback to be understood.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: separate-feeling-story-feedback
description: You received feedback and your reaction to it is larger than the feedback seems to warrant, or you find yourself unable to assess what the feedback actually says because your emotions and your story about the feedback are running together. The chapter prescribes a three-question move to pull apart feeling, story, and feedback into separate strands.

sections_html.when:
You have received feedback and your emotional reaction is running strong. You cannot tell whether your distressed interpretation of the feedback is accurate or distorted. You find yourself unable to say, plainly, what the feedback actually said: your sense of what it means has already overwritten your memory of what was actually said.

Also reach for this when you notice that a small piece of recent feedback has triggered a disproportionately strong reaction, and you suspect the reaction belongs partly to older, unhealed material.
sections_html.move:
Ask yourself three questions in sequence and write down the answer to each before moving to the next.

What do I feel? Name the emotion as precisely as possible: anxiety, shame, anger, sadness, surprise, humiliation, embarrassment. Then describe how it feels physically, not metaphorically. Seth's example: "I feel a jolt of adrenaline that is by now very familiar to me. It's what I imagine an electric shock feels like. And then I often feel sick to my stomach and slightly faint." The physical description anchors the feeling without amplifying it and keeps it from merging with the story.

What's the story I'm telling, and inside the story, what's the threat? Listen to your story about what the feedback means without yet evaluating whether it's true or reasonable. The chapter: "Pay special attention to the threat." Common threats include: a bad thing that might happen as a result (I will lose this job), how others now see you (everyone knows I'm incompetent), or how you must now see yourself (I have always been a mess). Seth's account: "I thought, 'I knew it!' and then my thoughts snowballed: 'This job is the best opportunity I'll ever have and I've messed it up. I mess up everything.' So there are a few threats in there: that my boss disapproves of me, that I will lose my job, that I won't be able to live with myself."

What's the actual feedback? Peel back the story and ask only: what was said? What were the exact words? Resist embellishing or inferring. Seth's feedbeck was his boss's single comment about "everyone" being disorganized, including Seth. Everything else in Seth's story (assumptions about the boss's attitude, fears about losing his job, self-loathing) was Seth's addition.

Check for shadowboxing with the past. Once the three strands are visible, ask: is the current threat connected to a longer story? Sometimes a small piece of feedback is the last straw in a series of accumulated comments, or it aggravates a wound that never fully healed. The chapter's example: a neighbor's lawn comment triggers a disproportionate reaction because it is the latest chapter in a lifelong story about social obliviousness. A colleague's suggestion to speak more authoritatively detonates old material from childhood bullying and a breakup. Recognizing that the current feedback has activated older material does not make the older material less real. But it separates what belongs to this feedback from what belongs to older fights.

Assess alignment. Once the strands are separated, ask whether your story is reasonably aligned with the actual feedback, or whether and how it is distorted. The goal is not to dismiss the emotion or the story, but to see how each element is affecting the others, and to distinguish what was added from what was said.
sections_html.signal: You can write three separate answers, in distinct sentences, for each of the three questions. The answer to "what was the actual feedback" is shorter and more contained than the answer to "what's the story." The threat is named explicitly rather than hovering vaguely. You can identify at least one thing your story added that the feedback did not say.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "if I had to write down exactly what was said in one sentence, what would I write?" If you cannot do this without the sentence ballooning into your story about what it must mean, the strands are merged and need separating.

After: ask "what did the feedback actually say, separately from what I told myself it must mean?" If you can answer in plain words without the story re-entering, the strands have been separated.
sections_html.failure: When feeling, story, and feedback are merged, the distorted story substitutes for the feedback in your memory and your evaluation. You process your story as if it were the content of the feedback, and your learning is based on what you told yourself rather than what was said. The chapter: "Our mind takes what was said and immediately tells a story." The longer the story runs unchecked, the more difficult it is to find the actual feedback underneath it. Shadowboxing with the past compounds this: the older material fills in the story's threat content with material the current feedback did not generate and cannot address.
sections_html.example: Seth's boss made one comment, with humor, in a meeting. Seth's merged response: nausea, shame, despair, certainty that his boss has disapproved of him all along, conviction that he would lose his job and fail at everything, decision to cancel a flight to a meaningful family celebration. After separating the strands: one offhand comment (actual feedback), fear of disapproval and global incompetence (story), a strong jolt of adrenaline and nausea (feeling). In retrospect Seth cannot understand how he got from the comment to canceling the flight. That gap is the distance between actual feedback and merged story.
sections_html.source: Chapter 8, "Dismantle Distortions," lines 3695-3718 (the three-question move, the strand-separation technique, Seth's analysis of his own strands), 3724-3741 (shadowboxing with the past: last straw dynamics, open wounds, and the framing that such reactions are not wrong but rather belong to a longer story the current feedback is not equipped to address). Stone and Heen call this the second distortion-dismantling strategy, "Separate the Strands: Feeling / Story / Feedback."
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/notice-the-distortion-type — the prior step; names which distortion type is running before this skill separates the strands
  • feedback/contain-the-story — the next step once the strands are separated; uses the actual feedback to restore scope limits

name: separate-impact-from-intent
description: You have received feedback about how you came across, and it does not match what you were trying to do. You are about to either reject the feedback ("but I had good intentions, the impact must not have been real or must not be my fault") or accept it and abandon your sense of your own intentions ("I guess I really am that bad"). The chapter says hold the layers separately.

sections_html.when: Feedback has just landed about your impact. They felt bossed around. They felt disrespected. They thought you were difficult, contemptuous, controlling, dismissive, self-obsessed. The feedback does not square with your sense of what you were trying to do. You were trying to help, to be efficient, to support, to give space. Reach for this before you collapse the gap by discarding either the feedback or your sense of your intentions.
sections_html.move:
1. Hold both layers as separate facts. The chapter's structural claim: you have privileged access to your intentions (arrow 2 on the Gap Map) and almost no direct access to your behavior (arrow 3) or your impact (arrow 4). Others have it the other way around. They cannot read your intentions; they can only read the impact, and they often guess the intentions from the impact. Treat your intentions as your data, and treat their feedback as data about your impact. Neither datum cancels the other.
  2. Notice the leaks that produce the gap. The chapter spends most of the first half on the anatomy of why this gap exists: your face is visible to everyone and not to you, your tone is processed by your STS only when others speak (turning off when you speak), and even big patterns of your behavior live in your blind spot (the cellphone-pacing parent who works hard to minimize phone time and is still mimed by his five-year-old). The leaks mean others' feedback is your only way to access the part of yourself you cannot observe.
  3. Notice the three amplifiers that widen the gap. The chapter names three systematic asymmetries between your story and theirs. - Emotional math: you subtract certain emotions from your self-description ("I wasn't really angry, the situation was tense") while others count them double. The angry exit you don't remember as anger is the heart of how the room remembers you. - Situation versus character: you attribute your missteps to the situation; others attribute the same actions to your character. You took the last piece of cake because no one else wanted it; they read it as selfishness. - Impact versus intent proper: you judge yourself by intentions; they judge you by impacts. You assume good intentions produce good impacts; they reason backward from impact to intent and conclude bad impact means careless or bad intent. All three converge to make your self-story systematically more flattering than the story being told about you, and to make their feedback systematically more about character than yours feels.
  4. Use the chapter's verbal move. When the gap is on the table, do not collapse it: surface it. The script: "I was working hard to be patient [arrow 2, my intentions]. And it sounds like that's not the impact I'm having [arrow 4]. That's upsetting. Let's figure out why." Both layers are named. Both are now discussable. Neither has been discarded.
  5. Decide whether to change behavior or feeling. The chapter's "from the inside out" move. If the gap is wide and the feedback consistent, surface-level behavior change will keep leaking through your face and tone, because your underlying state will keep transmitting. The deeper choice is between (a) talking explicitly about the underlying state ("I get frustrated when X happens; here is what's behind it; let's problem-solve") and (b) working to change the underlying state itself (developing actual empathy, getting to know people better as people, finding what they are doing well). Either is a fuller response than "I'll act more X." Pretending will not work because your face will not pretend along with you.
sections_html.signal: You can describe, in two sentences, your intent and their impact as separate, both real, neither cancelled by the other. You can name at least one of the three amplifiers (emotional math, situation/character, impact/intent) operating in this specific case. You have moved from "the feedback is wrong because my intentions were good" or "I guess my intentions were bad after all" to "my intentions were X, the impact was Y, those are different facts, what now?"
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "When I think about this feedback, am I treating it as a verdict on what kind of person I am, or as data about what others experienced?" If the answer is "verdict," the layers are still collapsed.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can complete two sentences in the giver's words and your own. "What I was trying to do was ___" (your intentions, your access). "What landed was ___" (their impact, their access, in their words). The truthfulness of each sentence does not depend on the other.
sections_html.failure: Without separation, two failures alternate. In the first, you hold onto your intentions as a defense and discard the impact data: "I had good intentions, therefore the negative impact you describe must not have happened, must not have been mine, or must say more about your sensitivity than my behavior." The chapter's case is Annabelle, whose response to "you're difficult" is essentially "I have positive intentions and therefore positive impacts." The feedback gets dismissed; the leak stays open; the next 360 surfaces the same theme. In the second failure, you swallow the impact and discard the intentions: "I tried to be respectful and they say I'm not, so my self-perception must be a lie."
