This is not actually correction; it is identity collapse, and it produces either over-correction (performing respect while still feeling contempt) or paralysis (afraid to act because every action might be a leak you cannot see). The chapter's broader point in "from the inside out" is that surface-only correction without inner work does not close the gap; the leaks resume as soon as you stop policing yourself, because the underlying feeling is the source.
sections_html.example: The chapter's anchoring case is Annabelle. Her thoughts and feelings about her team (impatience, contempt, the underlying belief that they should figure out questions on their own) are real, accruing over years of high-standards experience and positive reinforcement for being quietly resourceful. Her intentions, formed downstream of those feelings, are different: she wants colleagues to feel respected and appreciated. Her behavior is downstream of both, and the chapter is precise that her face, tone, and body language are leaking the feelings, not enacting the intentions. Her colleagues are reading the leak accurately. Annabelle, hearing the feedback, registers her intentions ("I am trying so hard to act respectful") and rejects the impact data ("they must be wrong, or playing politics"). The chapter walks through what would be different if she held the layers separately: she could name the gap, problem-solve the underlying frustration with the team rather than performing a respect she does not feel, and either explicitly discuss the feelings ("I get frustrated under pressure, please point out my reaction in the moment") or work to change them. Sasha and the conference-room outburst are smaller cases of the same separation: in each, the receiver's intent is real, the impact is real, and collapsing one onto the other forecloses the conversation.
sections_html.source: Chapter 4, "See Your Blind Spots," lines 1814-1880 (Behavioral Blind Spots, with leaky face, leaky tone, leaky patterns), 1919-1970 (the three amplifiers: Emotional Math, Situation versus Character, Impact versus Intent), and 2149-2162 ("Focus on Change from the Inside Out"). Stone and Heen lay out the structural asymmetry between intent-access and impact-access, name the three amplifiers that systematically widen the gap, and prescribe the verbal move that surfaces both layers without collapsing them. The skill installs the chapter's central diagnostic-and-response move for impact feedback that does not match self-perceived intent.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: set-the-boundary-clearly
description: You have decided that a boundary is needed and you know which type. Now you need to have the conversation. The chapter prescribes a sequence of moves for turning away feedback gracefully while keeping the relationship intact.

sources.chapter: 10. How Good Do I Have to Be? (Draw Boundaries When Enough Is Enough)

sections_html.when: You have run the diagnostic, identified that a boundary is needed, and named the type. Now you need to say it out loud to the person who is giving you unwanted feedback. You want to be clear without being cruel, firm without being dismissive, and honest about your feelings without starting a fight.
sections_html.move:
Be transparent: actually tell them. The biggest mistake in setting boundaries is assuming the other person knows what is going on with you. They may not know you are overwhelmed, struggling, or that their coaching is making things worse. Dave needed a hearing aid and took six months to tell his coworkers. His coworkers, watching him ignore their concerns, concluded he was dismissing them. All he needed to say was: "I got checked. I need a hearing aid. I'm going to get one. It's a hard adjustment. I'll do better if I'm not pestered." The transparency does not fix the underlying issue but it fixes the feedback dynamic around it.

Be firm and appreciative. These are not opposites. PJ, about to lecture, is consistently made more anxious when her department head whispers "don't be nervous" just before she begins. Her conversation with the department head: she names the good intention (I know you're trying to help), she names the impact (it makes me more nervous, not less), she holds to her point when the department head pushes back, and she redirects to something that would actually help (tell me how you cope with anxiety when you speak, but on days when I am not lecturing). She does not let the relationship warmth or the good intention invalidate her experience of the impact.

Redirect unhelpful coaching. Before establishing a hard limit, consider whether there is a version of the giver's interest that you could welcome. The chapter: "You might experiment with loaning your giver a corner of your acre, and tell them what you'd love to see there." Hunyee to her mother: "I have so much to learn from you. When you visit, would you teach me to make your amazing dumplings?" This meets the giver's need to be useful and valued while redirecting away from the feedback that is not helping. It also lays a foundation for firmer limits if the redirect is ignored.

Use "and" not "but." When you reject feedback while affirming the relationship, the temptation is to say "but." "I love seeing you, but if you're going to come to my house you need to stop criticizing everything." "But" signals a contradiction: the love is real, but it is conditional on them stopping. "And" holds both truths as simultaneously true. "I love seeing you and your comments about my weight are incredibly upsetting and I need you to keep them to yourself." Neither feeling cancels the other. The chapter: "Human emotions don't necessarily cancel each other out." The And Stance lets you share the complexity of your feelings without hiding behind a simple bottom-line message.

Raul's use of ands with his parents: "I've been afraid to talk to you about this and it's important to me to be honest." "I've decided to major in music and I know this makes you worry." "I'm also very fearful of the struggle I may face and I need to try." "I know this is hard for you and I hope you will still be supportive of me." Each sentence holds two real truths simultaneously.

Be specific about three things. The chapter names three specifics:

  1. The Request: What exactly are you asking? A particular topic off-limits (my weight, my new spouse), a behavior off-limits (looking disapprovingly when I step outside for a cigarette), a context off-limits (no coaching right before I lecture)? If the person needs examples of what you're talking about, give them, with their impact on you.
  1. The Time Frame: How long is the boundary likely to be in place? Is this a "not right now, I need time to adjust" or an indefinite limit? Let them know if and how they can check in with you about it without violating it.
  1. Their Assent: Ask explicitly whether they can honor the request. When they say yes, their identity and reputation are enlisted in living up to the commitment. The chapter: "Don't assume that they understand you or agree."

Describe consequences as warnings. Let them know what happens if they cannot or will not honor the boundary. Your goal is a warning, not a threat: you are giving them information about legitimate consequences so they can make an informed choice. The chapter's example: "If you can't leave this issue behind for now, I'll make sure to visit Marv at times when I know you won't be here." You cannot control whether they honor the boundary; you can control your response if they don't.
sections_html.signal: The giver knows specifically what you are asking, how long, and what happens if they do not honor it. They have said yes. You did not hide the complexity of your feelings behind a simple bottom-line message; you used "and" to hold the relationship warmth and the limit at the same time.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "have I actually told them what I need, out loud, clearly, including the time frame and the consequences?" If the conversation has only happened in your head, the boundary has not been set.

After: ask "did I hear them say they would honor the request?" If you did not ask for their assent, you have delivered the message but not yet made it a commitment between you.
sections_html.failure: Without transparency, the giver continues in good faith because they do not know their feedback is damaging. Without firmness, the giver overrides the boundary at the first sign of pushback because they sense you can be persuaded. Without the And Stance, the setting of the boundary feels like an accusation to the giver and triggers all their own truth, relationship, and identity reactions. Without specificity, the request is too vague to honor ("back off" or "stop being so critical" leaves the giver unable to know when they are in compliance). Without consequences, there is no information about what changes if the boundary is not honored.
sections_html.example: Hunyee to her mother: "Mom, I love you and I know you want the best for me. And your comments about my weight and housekeeping and clothing are incredibly upsetting to me. If you're going to stay with me, I need you to keep them to yourself. Is that a request you can honor?" This script uses the And Stance (I love you AND this must stop), is specific about the request (weight, housekeeping, clothing, not a vague "back off"), includes an implicit consequence (if you're going to stay with me), and asks for assent. The chapter contrasts this with the weaker version: "I need you to back off" or "Quit being so critical," which set up a fight about whether the label is accurate rather than a clear request about specific behavior.
sections_html.source: Chapter 10, "How Good Do I Have to Be?," lines 4755-4776 (be transparent: Dave's case), 4781-4799 (be firm and appreciative: PJ's case), 4804-4813 (redirect unhelpful coaching: Hunyee/dumplings), 4819-4838 (use "and": the "but" problem, Raul's ands with his parents, the And Stance), 4844-4863 (be specific about request, time frame, assent; Hunyee's specific script; hierarchy note with Brodie/Jake), 4869-4881 (describe consequences as warnings, not threats; the smoking/visiting example).
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/diagnose-and-name-the-boundary — the prior step; determines whether a boundary is needed and which type
  • feedback/mitigate-when-not-changing — the companion skill for what to do after refusing feedback: your duty to reduce the impact of your decision not to change

name: settle-evaluation-first
description: You are trying to take in coaching or appreciation while a where-do-I-stand question is unanswered, and the coaching is not landing because the loud unresolved evaluation is drowning it out. You are about to attribute the failure to the coaching's content (or to your own inability to absorb it) when the actual issue is sequencing.

sections_html.when: You are receiving (or trying to receive) coaching or appreciation while an evaluation question is open and unsettled. The open question may be loud (you just got a worse rating than you expected, or a vague comment that might be evaluative) or silent (no evaluation has been offered at all and you are mining everything else for clues). Either way, you can feel that you are not actually absorbing the coaching, even if you are nodding along. Reach for this before you try harder to absorb it.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause and name the open evaluation question. "Did I meet expectations or fall short?" "Am I a stopgap or part of the long-term plan?" "Am I still wanted here?" Whatever is taking the airtime in your head while the giver is talking.
  2. Treat the evaluation question as prerequisite, not parallel. The chapter's image: when a professor hands back a graded paper, the student turns to the last page first to see the grade; only after that can they read the margin notes. The order is not a flaw of attention, it is structural.
  3. If the evaluation is loud and unfavorable (you got the lower rating, or the bad news), do not try to absorb the coaching in the same conversation. Acknowledge the evaluation, sit with it, and schedule the coaching for later — the chapter recommends days, probably longer.
  4. If the evaluation is silent (none has been offered and you are guessing), ask for it directly before continuing with coaching or appreciation. The "evaluative floor" needs to be there before the rest can stand on it. Use a script like: "Before we get into how I can improve, I need a quick read on where I stand overall. Am I broadly on track?"
  5. Once the evaluation question is answered enough to free attention, return to the coaching or appreciation and absorb it deliberately.
sections_html.signal: You can name the evaluation question that was occupying you, and you have either answered it (asked and gotten an answer, or confirmed it is settled enough) or deferred the coaching until the evaluation is settled. The internal noise (what about the times I bailed you out, what's wrong with me, what does this mean for my compensation) has dropped enough that you can actually hear what the giver is saying about how to improve, or take in their thanks without parsing it for hidden bad news.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "While they are coaching me (or thanking me), what is actually running in my head?" If the answer is an unanswered where-do-I-stand question rather than the content of the feedback, evaluation is not yet settled.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if the answer is now the content of the coaching or the appreciation itself, with the evaluation question either answered or visibly parked for a separate conversation.
sections_html.failure: Without sequencing, two failure modes alternate. First, in a loud evaluation: you sit through the coaching nodding while internally relitigating the rating, then leave with neither the rating accepted nor the coaching absorbed, and you misremember the meeting as having been about the rating only. Second, in a silent evaluation: you mine the coaching and the appreciation for hidden evaluative content (why is the boss giving me so much coaching on this — am I in trouble? why was I singled out for thanks in one email but not the next?), so the coaching cannot land as coaching and the appreciation cannot land as appreciation. Both kinds get rerouted to the slot of the unanswered question, and the actual content of both is lost. The receiver concludes feedback is opaque or political when the real issue is that the evaluative floor was never set.
sections_html.example: The chapter's "Evaluation Shortfalls" section makes the silent-evaluation case directly: in a workplace where evaluation is absent, the receiver puts an ear to the ground and reads coaching and appreciation as veiled signals about standing. The "Separate Evaluation" section makes the loud-evaluation case: walking into a performance review hoping to learn how to improve, getting a "meets" instead of the "exceeds" you expected, and then being unable to hear any of the coaching that follows because internal voices have taken over. The graded-paper image at line 1067 is the chapter's compact statement of the principle: students cannot read the margin notes until they have seen the grade.
sections_html.source: Chapter 2, "Separate Appreciation, Coaching, and Evaluation," lines 907-918 ("Evaluation Shortfalls" with the "evaluative floor") and 1058-1069 ("Separate Evaluation from Coaching and Appreciation" with the bugle-blast metaphor and the graded-paper analogy). Stone and Heen treat evaluation as structurally loud, and the receiver's task as putting evaluation first in time, either by getting the answer or by deferring the rest of the conversation. The skill installs the sequencing as a receiver-side move when the evaluation question is open and the rest of the feedback is failing to land.

name: shift-to-honest-mirror
description: You have vented to a friend or colleague about feedback you received, and they have been supportive. Now you want to see if there is something to learn, but you are not sure how to make that shift or how to structure the conversation so that you hear both the valid and invalid parts of the feedback.

sources.chapter: 12. Get Going (Five Ways to Take Action)

sections_html.when: You have already talked to someone about feedback that upset you, and they have supported you. Now the sting has faded and you want to check whether you have missed something worth learning. Or you are about to vent to a friend and want to get more than validation from the conversation.
sections_html.move:
Explicitly request the shift. Tell the person you already vented to (or are about to vent to) that you want them to function as an honest mirror rather than only a supportive one. Name the difference out loud: "I've been venting and you've been supportive and I appreciate it. But I'd like you to help me sift the feedback for what I might learn. I want you to be honest with me, not just reassuring." Amy made this shift with Hank: "I can usually see where people are coming from, but with Ivan there's something going on that I don't get. I need you to help me with that."

The shift is a request, not a command. Your friend may not have seen enough of the situation to offer a useful honest read. But they are almost always positioned to see more than they have offered while in the supportive mode.

Use the two-column technique. Take a piece of paper and draw two columns: what's wrong with the feedback, and what might be right or valid or reasonable. Write down the concerns about the feedback (Ivan's delivery was unprofessional; his comment about being late was in front of eighteen people) in the first column. This acknowledges the legitimate problems without dismissing them. Then focus on the second column: what might be right? The chapter's formulation: "Writing down and discussing what's wrong frees Amy to see what might be right."

Ground rules for the two-column exercise: The two sides do not net each other out. You are not trying to reach a verdict on who was right. You are digging to learn. Each column can have true things in it simultaneously; the point is to separate the delivery problems from the content, and the content problems from the content that might be valuable.

Hank's column for Amy: In "what's wrong," Amy's concerns about Ivan's public scolding and his dismissal of her prior knowledge about signatures. In "what might be right," a pattern Hank had noticed: Amy's habit of needing to have the last word, evidenced by the text exchange that ran during the conference call and her insistence at the end of the phone call with Hank that she really was only two minutes late.
sections_html.signal: You have heard something from your honest-mirror partner that you would not have heard in the purely supportive mode. The second column has at least one item in it that you consider worth examining, even if it is uncomfortable.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "am I getting validation and comfort, or am I also getting a perspective on what might be true in the feedback?" If only the first, the mirror is supportive only.

After: ask "is there anything in the second column that I believe might be worth acting on?" If yes, the honest-mirror mode has produced something useful.
sections_html.failure: Without the shift, venting to a supportive mirror ends in relief and validation but no learning. The chapter: "Too often we stop there. We ask our friends to be supportive mirrors so that we can get recentered and feel better. But we miss the opportunity to also ask them to help us sift the feedback itself for anything we might learn." Amy ends her conversation with Hank having gotten reassurance but not the observation that her need-to-be-right pattern was contributing to the Ivan conflict. The honest-mirror version of the same conversation gave her that.
sections_html.source: Chapter 12, "Get Going," lines 6274-6310 (Amy and Hank: triangulating for comfort vs. coaching, Amy's explicit request for the honest-mirror shift, Hank's hunch about Amy's pattern), 6316-6325 (the two-column technique: what's wrong, what might be right, the Amy napkin example).
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/name-which-mirror-you-need — the chapter-6 skill for naming which kind of mirror you are asking for; this skill extends it to the explicit shift request and the two-column technique

name: signpost-and-track-each-topic
description: You have realized a feedback conversation contains two tangled topics (the original feedback and a relationship concern that hooked you). You are about to either keep going while tangled, hoping it sorts itself out, or pull onto your own topic without saying so. The chapter says make the split explicit, propose a sequence, and put both of you on the same topic at the same time.

sections_html.when: You have just spotted that a feedback exchange has split into two topics: the giver's original feedback and a relationship concern of yours (delivery, credibility, trust, appreciation, autonomy, or acceptance). You are about to do one of two things, both bad. Option one: keep going on what looks like one topic, telling yourself you can resolve both as you go. Option two: switch onto your own topic without naming the move, and hope the giver follows. The chapter's prescription is neither.
sections_html.move:
1. Step outside the conversation and name the split out loud. The chapter is explicit that this is unnatural and that the unnaturalness is part of why it works: it interrupts the reactive pattern by being hyper-explicit about what is happening. Use your own words, but name two things: that there are two topics, and that both deserve conversation.
  2. Use the chapter's signposting template, in your own phrasing: "I see two related but separate topics for us to discuss. They are both important. Let's discuss each fully but separately, finishing one before swinging back to the other."
  3. State each topic concretely. The giver's topic in their words ("you'd like me to spend less time with Howard and more with the other children"). Your topic in your words ("the way I'm getting feedback this year, including the fact that this is the first piece of feedback I've heard"). Both topics named, neither minimized.
  4. Decide the sequence using the chapter's two-factor rule: - Default: edge to the original feedback. That is what the giver came to discuss; all things being equal, start with their topic. - Override: if your relationship trigger reaction is loud enough that you will not actually take in their topic, name the trigger and propose your topic first ("I want to engage what you're saying about Howard, and I notice I'm too triggered by the appreciation question to hear it well; can we talk about the second one first, briefly, so I can come back to the first?"). The override exists because the chapter notes that under sufficient activation, you will not actually hear the giver's topic anyway, so processing yours first is in service of getting back to theirs, not avoiding it.
  5. Hold the discipline of one track at a time. The point of signposting is that both of you are on the same topic at the same time. When you finish one, swing back to the other deliberately. The signposting does not work if you signpost and then immediately re-tangle.
sections_html.signal: The conversation now has a visible structure both of you can name: there are two topics, you are talking about one, you have agreed to come back to the other. The giver knows what you have agreed to discuss now and what you have agreed to discuss next. You are no longer covertly steering the conversation toward your topic; you are openly proposing the order.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "Does the giver know that there is a second topic on my side, and does the giver know which topic we are discussing right now?" If the answer to either is no, the conversation is still tangled, regardless of how the words are landing.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can answer yes to both: the giver knows there is a second topic, and both of you know which one is on the table at this moment.
sections_html.failure: Without signposting, two failures alternate. In the first, you stay tangled: both of you continue on parallel tracks, each hearing the other's words through your own topic's filter, each accumulating evidence that the other is missing the point. The conversation does not resolve because resolution requires being on the same topic at the same time, and you are not. In the second failure, you switch unilaterally. You quietly drop the giver's topic and pull onto yours without naming the move. The giver feels unheard on their topic and unfooted on yours, because you arrived without warning, and the conversation either escalates or collapses. The chapter notes that signposting is unnatural; that is the cost of the move. The cost of skipping it is that the conversation defeats the feedback in both directions.
sections_html.example: The chapter's worked case is Ella, a teacher's assistant who has spent eight months designing activities, collecting supplies, and devoting extra time to a class of children with disabilities. Her supervising teacher's first piece of feedback all year is "you're spending too much time focused on Howard. There are nine other children in this class." Ella's switchtrack is silent: she leaves the room with her objections trapped in her head ("after eight months, the first piece of feedback I get is that I'm caring too much?"). The chapter walks through what would be different if she signposted instead. After calming down, Ella returns and says (paraphrased): "Let's talk about Howard and how I'm spending my time. That's important. This is also the first time I've gotten feedback. So after we talk about Howard, I want us to come back to the question of how I get feedback and what you notice in my work with the kids that is positive." Both topics named, sequence proposed, original feedback given the edge, second topic preserved for after. The chapter also offers Louie's hypothetical signposting move at the end of the chapter as the same template applied to the rose scene: "Okay, I forgot you don't like roses. You should remind me again why. And then I have to say that I'm feeling a little underappreciated for my effort. We should talk about both."
sections_html.source: Chapter 5, "Don't Switchtrack: Disentangle What from Who," lines 2622-2648 ("Give Each Topic Its Own Track" and "Signposting"). Stone and Heen offer signposting as the operational response to a spotted switchtrack: name both topics out loud, propose a sequence, and discuss each fully and separately. The chapter explicitly notes that signposting is not a natural conversational move because it requires stepping outside the conversation; that absence of flow is what gives it its corrective power. The decision rule for sequencing (default to the giver's topic, override only when your trigger is loud enough to prevent hearing) is the second half of the move and is what turns the script into a procedure.

name: sort-the-kind
description: You are about to receive, ask for, or react to "feedback" as one undifferentiated category, when in fact what is on the table is one of three distinct kinds (appreciation, coaching, or evaluation), each with a different purpose and a different receiver-side need.

sections_html.when: You are inside a feedback exchange (or about to start one), and you notice yourself thinking, asking for, or rejecting "feedback" as a single thing. You are about to ask for "more feedback," complain that you "do not get any feedback," or tell yourself this conversation is "feedback" without naming what kind. Reach for this before you proceed.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause and ask the categorizing question: which of the three kinds is in play right now? - Appreciation: communicates "I see you, I know how hard you have been working, you matter." It is fundamentally about relationship and connection, not improvement. - Coaching: aimed at helping the receiver learn, grow, or change. Two flavors. The first responds to the receiver's need to build capability for a new challenge. The second is prompted by the giver's own feeling of imbalance or hurt in the relationship, where "you should change" is really "this situation should change for me." - Evaluation: tells you where you stand. It is an assessment, ranking, or rating, always implicitly or explicitly comparative (against others, against a standard, against expectations).
  2. Sort the three by what each is for. Appreciation motivates and connects. Coaching accelerates learning, or addresses a relational imbalance. Evaluation aligns expectations and informs decisions.
  3. If the feedback in front of you is more than one kind at once (it usually is, especially because every coaching message carries some evaluation), name the mix and which kind dominates.
  4. Now decide what to do with what is in front of you, with the kind explicitly named. The needs and the response differ by kind, so the same move does not cover all three.
sections_html.signal: You can label, in one sentence, the kind (or the mix) of feedback you are giving, getting, or seeking. You can answer "what kind?" without falling back on "feedback." The follow-up move you choose (ask for clarification, accept the thanks, push back on the rating, request coaching) is selected for that kind, not for the generic category.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to write down which of the three kinds this is, could I?" If the honest answer is "I do not know, it just feels like feedback," you have not yet sorted.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can label the kind in one sentence and, if more than one kind is present, name which kind is loudest or most urgent for you right now.
sections_html.failure: Without the sort, three failure modes alternate. You ask for "feedback" generically and receive one kind when you needed another, then walk away dismayed without knowing why. You react to incoming feedback as if all three kinds had the same weight, so a piece of evaluation that should have settled an anxiety gets treated as coaching to argue with, or a piece of coaching that was meant to help you grow gets treated as evaluation that wounds. You complain about a "feedback shortfall" that is really a kind-shortfall: you are getting plenty of one and starving for another, and the lump-category word hides the real gap.
sections_html.example: The chapter opens with the post-merger scenario where a colleague says "you do not get feedback?" but the boss has been publicly thanking you in meetings. The friend hears that as feedback (appreciation); the receiver is starving for coaching about a brand-new role (Pacific Rim) and later for evaluation (am I a stopgap or a successor?). The same word, "feedback," covers three different things; each conversation only resolves once the kind is named.
sections_html.source: Chapter 2, "Separate Appreciation, Coaching, and Evaluation," lines 780-905. Stone and Heen open the chapter by distinguishing three kinds of feedback that the everyday word collapses, give each its own definition (appreciation as relational signaling, coaching as growth or relational rebalancing, evaluation as comparative assessment), and lay out the giver's purpose for each in a table at lines 879-905. The skill installs the categorize-the-kind move as the first step before any other receiver-side work in this part of the book.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: sort-toward-coaching
description: Feedback has arrived and your identity has been triggered. The chapter's first practice for cultivating a growth identity is to sort the feedback into the coaching bin rather than the evaluation bin, which reduces the identity reaction and makes the feedback learnable.

sources.chapter: 9. Cultivate a Growth Identity (Sort Toward Coaching)

sections_html.when: You received feedback and your identity has been hooked. You are asking yourself "am I enough?" rather than "what can I learn?" The feedback may have been offered as coaching, but you heard it as evaluation. Or the feedback genuinely contained both evaluation and coaching, and you got stuck on the evaluation part. Or you are simply unsure whether there is coaching in the feedback at all.
sections_html.move:
Run the sorting exercise. Take the feedback and imagine hearing it, first as evaluation, then as coaching. The two interpretations produce different experiences and different questions:

  • If heard as evaluation: what does this say about who I am? What label does it assign? Am I competent, attractive, responsible, a good parent? Identity is triggered; the question is about a verdict.
  • If heard as coaching: what is there to learn? What could I do differently, understand more fully, or work on next? Identity is not triggered; the question is about a next action.

The chapter's case: Elsbeth is told to "amp up the energy level" during a presentation break. As evaluation, she hears: "Am I boring everyone? Am I not up to an audience this senior?" Her attention goes to defending her self-image. As coaching: "I should have another cup of coffee and make this next segment more interactive." Her attention goes to the second half of the presentation. Same words, opposite effects on learning.

Default to coaching. The chapter prescribes a default: when you are in the presence of feedback that could reasonably be heard either way, hear it as coaching. This is not self-deception; coaching IS the charitable and often accurate interpretation of what the giver intends. Even evaluation feedback usually contains an implicit coaching message.

Find the coaching in evaluation. When feedback is explicitly evaluative and the evaluation is unflattering, there may still be coaching at the core. The chapter's key diagnostic: ask what the giver actually wants. Margaret hears Lisa saying "you were a bad mother." Lisa wants three things: to be understood, to receive acknowledgment that her mother's choices contributed to her pain, and to have a better current relationship. The coaching is at the core; the evaluation is the surface packaging. Margaret can reach past the surface packaging by asking: what is she trying to help me learn or understand?

Notice your oversort patterns. After running the sorting exercise several times with feedback from your past, the chapter predicts three discoveries: you can usually hear most feedback either way with some effort; identity reaction diminishes when you hear coaching; and you have identifiable tendencies toward oversort. If you oversort toward evaluation once out of ten times, that is one identity crisis that did not need to happen. If you oversort eight out of ten times, you have a consistent pattern that is blocking learning.
sections_html.signal: After receiving feedback, you can articulate what action you would take based on hearing it as coaching. The identity question ("am I good enough?") has moved to the background. Elsbeth's signal: she has a coffee, thinks about how to make the next segment more interactive, and delivers the second half. She did not need to resolve whether she is the kind of person senior audiences find engaging.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "what am I currently asking myself about this feedback?" If the question is about your worth, competence, or whether you are a certain kind of person, you are in the evaluation bin.

After: ask "what is the action this feedback is suggesting, if I hear it as coaching?" If you can answer this in one sentence, the coaching frame has been found.
sections_html.failure: Without sorting toward coaching, identity-relevant feedback consistently triggers a defensive reaction, and the content gets lost in the reaction. The chapter's label for this: "we snatch defensiveness from the jaws of learning." The receiver spends the energy defending their self-image that they would otherwise spend engaging with the content. In the Lisa/Margaret case, Margaret's identity reaction (I am a good mother; this feedback says I am a bad mother) prevents her from hearing what Lisa actually wants, which is acknowledgment and connection. The feedback fails for both of them. The chapter notes that "placing the conversation in a coaching frame" is important not because it reduces Margaret's pain (though it might), but because it accurately reflects what Lisa intends.
sections_html.example: The chapter offers a self-administered exercise: take a real piece of feedback you received recently and run it through both interpretations. "Your friend asked why you let your children stay up so late." As evaluation: you are an overpermissive parent. As coaching: this is a conversation about what your friend has noticed and what they're concerned about, something worth considering alongside your own observations. The exercise surface two things: the evaluation interpretation made you defensive; the coaching interpretation opened a question you could actually engage. Notice which one you defaulted to.
sections_html.source: Chapter 9, "Cultivate a Growth Identity," lines 4332-4373 (sort toward coaching: the Elsbeth case, the evaluation-vs-coaching sorting exercise, the oversort pattern observation), 4378-4395 (when coaching and evaluation get tangled: the Lisa/Margaret case, the diagnostic of what the giver wants, Margaret's better response). Stone and Heen name this "Practice #1" of the three growth-identity practices.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/adopt-growth-mindset-for-feedback — the conceptual foundation; this skill is the behavioral application of the growth mindset to a specific feedback moment
  • feedback/unpack-judgment-from-evaluation — the companion practice for feedback that is genuinely evaluative and cannot be sorted as coaching

name: spot-the-label
description: Someone has just told you to "be more X" or "stop being so Y" or "you are too Z," and the meaning is already crystallizing in your head before any clarification has happened. The label arrived as a few vague words, but you have automatically attached your own high-definition movie to it and are about to act on or reject your interpretation rather than theirs.

sections_html.when: The feedback you are receiving consists of an evaluative shorthand: "be more proactive," "you need boundaries," "be more assertive," "don't be so selfish," "act your age," "be more affectionate." The shorthand sounds like it has content. You can already picture what to do (or what to push back on). Reach for this before that picture solidifies into a plan or a rejection.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice the label. The chapter's image is the supermarket shelf where items are stocked under generic names: SOUP, COLA. A feedback label is the same shape: a topic word with no nutritional content. The chapter says spotting labels is easy once you are listening for them, like counting how many times someone says "and" in a conversation. The hard part is remembering to listen.
  2. Resist filling in your own meaning. The chapter is explicit that this is the second step and the harder one. The label triggers your high-definition movie of what the giver "obviously" means, and once that movie is playing you have nothing to ask, nothing to be curious about, no reason to inquire. Catch the auto-fill in the act.
  3. Run a quick multiplicity check. Force yourself to generate two or three plausible alternative readings of the label. The chapter's "be more affectionate" example offers four (initiate sex more often / hold hands in public / pitch in around the house / be more playful and cuddly), all of which fit the label and are different in what they ask of you.
  4. Ask. Some version of "tell me more about what you mean by [label]" or "can you describe what that would look like" or "show me." The point is not to put the giver on the spot, it is to make the label hand off its content. The chapter's "Spot the Label" section names the work as figuring out what is under the label; without the question, the label stays empty.
  5. Hold the label as a placeholder, not a meal. The label is useful as a topic header you can return to later. It is not the meaning. The chapter's line: "the label is not the meal."
sections_html.signal: You can say, in one sentence, what the giver actually means by the label — sourced from what they told you, not from your auto-fill. If asked "what does be-more-X mean to your boss," you would answer with their words and examples, not with your guess. If you decide to act on the feedback or reject it, you are acting on or rejecting their meaning, not the version your own mind supplied.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to write down right now what they mean by [the label], could I distinguish my interpretation from theirs?" If the answer is "of course, they mean what they said," you have not yet spotted the label.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you can name your interpretation and theirs as distinct, you can articulate at least one plausible alternative reading the label would also fit, and you have asked enough questions to know which reading is the giver's.
sections_html.failure: Without spotting, two failure modes alternate. In the first, you "take" the feedback and act on your interpretation of the label. The giver later reacts with surprise or alarm at what you did. The chapter's case is Nicholas, told by his manager Adrianna to "be more assertive," who interprets that as "push the customer to close the deal today, before they leave the floor," and ends up threatening a customer. Adrianna had meant "show some energy and engagement, let your personality shine through" — almost the opposite. Nicholas was earnest and Adrianna was clear, and the label still hand-delivered the wrong instruction. In the second failure mode, you reject the feedback because your interpretation of the label is unappealing or wrong-headed. The chapter's case is Holly, who hears "you are too enmeshed" and "you need boundaries" and interprets that as "care less," which she rightly refuses; what Irwin actually meant was "watch the specific pattern of giving cash to clients who need real help, because it costs you their trust." Holly rejected an interpretation Irwin never intended.
sections_html.example: The chapter offers the multiple-choice exercise on "be more affectionate" (lines 1231-1241) directly. A spouse says it; the receiver auto-fills "she wants me to initiate sex more often." The chapter offers (a) hold hands in public, (b) pitch in more around the house, (c) be more playful and cuddly, (d) tell me you love me at least once a decade. The chapter's punchline: "the correct answer? You won't know until you talk about it, and you won't talk about it if you assume you already know." That is the move in two sentences. In Paul and Monisha's conversation at the end of the chapter, Paul models the move: when Monisha says mid-level managers feel "disempowered and out of the loop," Paul does not nod; he asks "what does it mean that they're feeling disempowered and out of the loop?"
sections_html.source: Chapter 3, "First Understand," lines 1164-1218 ("Feedback Arrives With Generic Labels" and "Giver and Receiver Interpret the Label Differently") and 1224-1241 ("Play 'Spot the Label'"). Stone and Heen name the soup-can label image, the high-definition movie that lives in the giver's head but does not travel with the words, and the explicit two-step move (notice the label, resist filling in). The skill installs the surface-form pattern recognition that opens the door to the deeper understanding moves later in the chapter.
sections_html.related_skills: (none yet)

name: spot-the-shifter-or-absorber-pattern
description: A feedback conversation about accountability is stuck, or the receiver finds themselves consistently either deflecting all blame or accepting all of it. The chapter names two dysfunctional patterns, shifter and absorber, that distort accountability and block learning, and describes what each looks like so it can be spotted from the inside.

sources.chapter: 6. Identify the Relationship System (Take Three Steps Back)

sections_html.when:
You are in a feedback conversation about something that went wrong, and the accountability discussion feels off in one of two ways. Either the conversation ends very quickly because you accepted all the fault and the other party has nothing more to say, and you feel a private resentment building later. Or you find yourself explaining at length how each factor that contributed was outside your control, and the other person keeps insisting you have not addressed the core problem.

Also reach for this when you notice the pattern across multiple conversations: you consistently feel responsible for things that go wrong around you, or you consistently notice that others are to blame before you notice your own contribution.
sections_html.move:
Identify which pattern is running. Two profiles:

Absorber pattern. When things go wrong, your first move is to accept responsibility, often quickly and completely. Signals: the feedback conversation ends fast because you agreed; you carry responsibility for outcomes that clearly required other people's actions too; you find yourself privately resentful that others are not stepping up; you are prone to staying in situations longer than seems warranted because you believe changing yourself will fix the dynamic. The absorber's learning obstruction: cutting the conversation short by accepting all responsibility prevents you from exploring what actually caused the problem, which means the next occurrence is no more tractable than this one. The chapter's specific caution: absorbers can remain in abusive relationships longer than is safe, because the feedback they receive ("you provoked me") accurately describes their contribution while omitting the harm being done to them.

Shifter pattern. When things go wrong, your first move is to identify external causes and other people's contributions. Signals: feedback bounces off because you can always identify a reason why the outcome was not yours to control; you feel victimized, powerless, or assaulted by other people's incompetence or bad intentions; you feel angry or depressed rather than curious when things fail; you explain each failure with a unique external cause (the VCs are fools, the markets are impossible, the team didn't deliver, the brief was wrong). The shifter's learning obstruction: a victim stance makes feedback impenetrable. Because the causes are all external, there is nothing to learn and nothing to change, and the same failures recur.

Interrupt the pattern. Once you have named the pattern you are in, apply the systems view from take-three-steps-back. For absorbers: the instruction is not to stop taking responsibility but to insist on looking at the full system, and to hold space for the other people and structural factors that contributed. Accepting all responsibility before exploring the system is not more accountable; it is less accurate and prevents durable learning. For shifters: the instruction is not to find someone else to blame but to locate your own contribution in the cycle. The chapter is explicit: seeing your own contribution to your circumstances makes you stronger, not weaker, because it means there are things you have the power to change.
sections_html.signal: You can name, in one sentence, which pattern was running. You can identify the specific thing you accepted too quickly (absorber) or the specific factor you attributed to others that you could have influenced (shifter). The accountability conversation does not end with you holding all the blame or none of it.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "when the conversation about this failure ended, did it end because the other person ran out of things to say, or because you ran out of explanations?" The first pattern is absorber; the second is shifter. Many people have a default, and situations of emotional stress or fatigue tend to intensify it.

After: ask "can I name both something I am accountable for and something others or the structure contributed to?" Both halves should be answerable. If only one is, the pattern is still running.
sections_html.failure: Without naming the pattern, absorbers continue to accept blame fully and quickly in ways that feel virtuous but obstruct both learning and fair distribution of accountability. Over time resentment builds and the absorber's capacity for change is exhausted, while others never look at their part. Shifters continue to reinterpret every feedback signal as evidence of others' inadequacy, remaining stuck and increasingly isolated. Both patterns are self-reinforcing: absorbers attract more blame because they accept it; shifters attract more unsolicited feedback because nothing sticks.
sections_html.example: The chapter describes Benny, who accepts Alice's feedback that he must bring projects in on time and on budget, and then fixes the problem on his own by inflating timelines and budgets. Benny absorbed the feedback without asking about the board's behavior, Vince's communication failures, or Alice's incomplete briefings. The solution works briefly and then generates new problems, because the system was never examined. This is the absorber version. The chapter does not pair it with a named shifter case, but the structural description is clear: a person who received feedback on the same project failures and explained them by board changes, Vince's silence, and Alice's missed hand-offs, without asking what they themselves might have done differently, would be exhibiting the shifter pattern.
sections_html.source: Chapter 6, "Identify the Relationship System," lines 3063-3099. Stone and Heen introduce the shifter and absorber profiles as "two common feedback profiles that are particularly challenging to deal with on the topic of accountability," presented as the sixth benefit of systems thinking (it helps correct the tendency to shift or absorb). The skill installs the diagnostic for recognizing which profile is running in the receiver at the moment of a feedback conversation.
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/take-three-steps-back — the systems framework that provides the remedy once the absorber or shifter pattern has been named

name: spot-the-switchtrack
description: A feedback exchange is escalating rather than resolving, you and the giver appear to be discussing the same subject (the roses, the sales numbers, the bath time, the crosswalk), and you are each using the other's words back at them while heading in opposite directions. The chapter says the conversation has quietly split into two topics and the first move is to see the split.

sections_html.when:
A feedback conversation is going sideways. The heat is rising rather than dropping, you and the giver seem to be repeating yourselves, and you have the sense of being misheard at every turn. On the surface you are still using the same vocabulary (the gift, the timing, the report, the kid bath time), but the encounter feels not like one disagreement but like two people insisting past each other. Reach for this before the next round.

Also reach for this when you notice the after-the-fact version: you have just walked away from a feedback exchange and find yourself venting to a third party about the giver's character, motives, delivery, or fairness, while never quite addressing what the feedback itself said. That is silent switchtracking, and it confirms the split was real.
sections_html.move:
1. Pause and ask the diagnostic question: are we using the same words to talk about two different topics? The chapter calls the artifact a switchtrack, by analogy to a railroad switch: the conversation looks like one track but has quietly become two, and you and the giver are now heading in different directions on parallel tracks.
  2. Locate the giver's topic. What was the original feedback, in their words? It is rarely the topic you have been arguing on. Try to state it in one sentence: the daughter's "you treat me like an untrustworthy child," the boss's "you missed the sales numbers," the wife's "you didn't do what you promised," the pedestrian's "you're in the crosswalk."
  3. Locate your topic. What is the second topic that has hooked you and pulled the conversation sideways? The chapter catalogues six common families of relationship trigger that produce these second topics. Check each: - Skill or judgment: the how, when, or where of the delivery (in front of others, at the wrong time, with the wrong tone). The feeling is "you have no business raising this this way." - Credibility: the giver lacks the expertise, background, experience, or values to speak on this. The feeling is "you have no standing to say this." - Trust / motives: the giver's intentions are suspect (jealous, threatened, controlling, building a case, checking a box). The feeling is "I cannot take this from someone who is doing this for that reason." - Appreciation: your efforts and successes feel unseen; this feedback feels unbalanced or arrives in the place where a thank-you should have been. The feeling is "after all I have done, this is what you bring me?" - Autonomy: the giver is encroaching on a decision, behavior, or domain that is yours. The feeling is "you don't get to tell me what to do here." - Acceptance: the giver is asking you to be a different person, not adjust a behavior. The feeling is "you don't accept who I am."
  4. Name your second topic in one sentence using one of those six handles. The wife who reacts to "your tone" with "I'm not the dog" has switchtracked from the broken bath-time promise (giver's topic) to autonomy and delivery (her topics). Louie has switchtracked from "I don't like red roses" (Kim's topic) to "you don't appreciate me" (his appreciation topic).
  5. Confirm the split is real by checking if both topics could be true. The chapter is explicit that the second topic may be at least as important as the first, and that's part of the trap: the second topic is real, you are right that it deserves attention, AND you have stopped engaging with the first topic. Both being true is the diagnostic. (The next step, signposting, is its own skill.)
sections_html.signal: You can write two sentences, one labelled "their topic" and one labelled "my topic," in different words, on different subjects, both true, both deserving conversation. You are no longer unsure why the exchange is escalating. The split is now visible to you, even if it is not yet visible to the giver.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I had to summarize what we are arguing about in one sentence, would the giver agree that's the topic?" If the answer is "no, they'd say we're arguing about something else" or "I'm not sure, we keep talking past each other," you are in a switchtrack and have not yet seen it.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if you can write the giver's topic in their words and your topic in your words, distinctly, and identify the moment the conversation forked.
sections_html.failure: Without spotting the switchtrack, the conversation becomes the loop the chapter names: two people giving feedback, no one receiving it. Each utterance gets heard through the other person's topic-filter, so the daughter's "don't you trust me" becomes evidence of ingratitude when the mother is on the gratitude track, and the wife's "you did precisely none of the things you promised" becomes evidence of bad tone when the husband is on the autonomy track. Each person hears their own topic confirmed by everything the other says, and the actual feedback (in both directions) goes nowhere. The silent version is worse: the receiver never voices the second topic at all, smiles through the conversation, and then triangulates afterward to friends or colleagues, short-circuiting learning in every direction. The chapter's framing: switchtracking does not just slow feedback; it defeats it.
sections_html.example: The chapter opens with Louie and Kim. Kim presents red roses she has told Louie before that she does not like, and gives him simple feedback: I don't like red roses. Louie's reaction is not "tell me more about that" but "let me critique how you just told me that." On the surface they are both talking about red roses, but they are not. Kim's topic is that Louie does not listen to her (a credibility-of-effort, autonomy-of-her-stated-preferences, and acceptance trigger underneath, but on the surface a coaching message). Louie's topic is that Kim does not appreciate his effort (an appreciation trigger). Same words, different topics. The chapter's spotting practice continues with four additional vignettes, each modelling one of the six relationship-trigger types: the daughter and mother (appreciation), the boss and salesperson (skill/judgment of timing), the husband and wife at the recital (skill/judgment plus autonomy of tone), and the pedestrian and driver (autonomy of vehicle vs. autonomy of body in crosswalk). Each scene shows the same structural artifact: same surface words, two distinct topics, both real, both being missed.
sections_html.source: Chapter 5, "Don't Switchtrack: Disentangle What from Who," lines 2293-2330 (the dynamic named, with Louie/Kim and the silent-switchtrack note), 2336-2509 (the two families of relationship triggers and their six subcategories that produce the second topics), 2587-2616 (the four spotting-practice vignettes that train the diagnostic). Stone and Heen treat the switchtrack as the chapter's central artifact: a feedback exchange that has quietly split into two topics, neither of which is being received because each party is hearing the other's words through their own topic's filter. The skill installs the chapter's diagnostic move and the typology that names the second topic.

name: take-responsibility-first-then-introduce-the-system
description: The receiver wants to name the larger system contributing to the problem but every time they try, the giver hears it as excuse-making. The chapter prescribes a sequencing rule: settle your own accountability first, then raise your observations about the broader system.

sources.chapter: 6. Identify the Relationship System (Take Three Steps Back)

sections_html.when: You have genuinely seen your contribution to the problem, and you have also seen real structural or relational factors that contributed, factors you believe the giver has not acknowledged or addressed. When you try to name those other factors, the giver becomes more frustrated rather than less, and says something like "you're making excuses" or "you're deflecting." The conversation is stuck.
sections_html.move:
The chapter names two messages that need to be delivered, and a sequencing rule about them.

Message one: I take responsibility for my part. State your contribution clearly and specifically, without burying it in qualifications or presenting it as smaller than you believe it is. The giver needs to hear this first, on its own terms. Do not rush past it.

Message two: We are both contributing to this. This is your observation about the broader system, the intersections, roles, or structural factors that are also part of the cause.

The sequencing rule. Deliver message one fully before attempting message two. The chapter's reason: the two messages are logically consistent, but to the person giving you feedback, they can sound contradictory. If you present them simultaneously, the giver almost always hears message two as a retraction of message one. A "relationship system" observation that arrives before your accountability is settled registers as: "I'm not really taking responsibility; I'm just describing the situation in a way that spreads the blame around."

Gauge readiness before introducing message two. After message one is settled, check whether the giver is in a state to hear a systems observation. This is a conversational judgment. If the giver is still visibly angry, still asserting that the problem is entirely yours, or has not acknowledged hearing message one, message two will not land productively. It may need to wait for a second conversation.

Avoid the vocabulary of "systems." The chapter says explicitly: avoid phrases like "relationship system" altogether. That vocabulary signals you have been reading a business book and are about to explain why nothing is your fault. The content of message two is the same; the framing is: "I've noticed that when X happens, my reaction to it tends to be Y, and I wonder if that's part of what sets this up. What do you think?" That framing keeps the receiver posture intact while opening the system question.
sections_html.signal: The giver has heard message one and confirmed it (verbally or in posture). The conversation is no longer stuck on "who is really at fault." When you raise message two, the giver engages with it rather than returning to message one. The conversation now includes both what you are accountable for and what the shared system might be contributing.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "have I said, out loud, in this conversation, what specifically I did or failed to do that contributed to this problem, without qualifying it by immediately pivoting to what the other person did?" If the answer is no, message one has not been delivered yet.

After: ask "does the giver now know what I believe I am responsible for?" If yes, message two is now appropriate to attempt.
sections_html.failure: Without the sequencing rule, the receiver tries to introduce the systems view as a simultaneous or even preemptive reframe, and the giver reads the whole move as a sophisticated deflection. The conversation shifts from a productive accountability discussion to a meta-argument about whether the receiver is being honest or just clever. The systems observation, which may be accurate and useful, becomes radioactive because it arrived before accountability was settled. The giver's trust in the receiver's honesty drops, and subsequent conversations become harder. The chapter's framing: "They'll assume you're attempting to deflect the feedback and point a finger back at them. They won't be interested in your fancy ideas about systems."
sections_html.example: The chapter does not provide a single named worked example for this sequencing move, but the Gil-and-Sandy thread from earlier in the chapter provides the logic. If Gil receives Sandy's feedback about his obsessive monitoring and responds with "You're right, I panic when surprised by spending, and I know I'm overreacting, and here's what would help me," he has owned his contribution (absorber-corrected) before describing the system need. That sequence is far more likely to be heard than if he began with "The real issue is you hide things from me."
sections_html.source: Chapter 6, "Identify the Relationship System," lines 3125-3149, the section titled "Talking About Systems" and specifically "Take Responsibility for Your Part." Stone and Heen frame the sequencing rule as the practical prescription for how to raise a systems observation without having it heard as blame-shifting. The skill captures the sequencing principle and the vocabulary caution.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/take-three-steps-back — generates the systems observations this skill then sequences into the conversation
  • feedback/ask-what-would-help-you-change — the complementary move when the giver is not ready to hear message two at all

name: take-three-steps-back
description: Feedback feels unfair or only partly accurate, and the receiver's instinct is to argue about who is really at fault. The chapter says the problem is rarely in one person; it lives in the relationship system between them, and seeing that system requires stepping back to three vantage points.

sources.chapter: 6. Identify the Relationship System (Take Three Steps Back)

sections_html.when:
Feedback has arrived and something about it feels unfair or off. Your first thought is "but you are the one who..." or "if you had just..." You can articulate the other person's contribution to the problem more easily than your own. Or you have received the same feedback from this person many times and it never resolves, because every conversation ends in competing accounts of whose fault it is.

Also reach for this when you are the giver and the conversation keeps ending in stalemate, both parties genuinely believing they see the real cause.
sections_html.move:
The three vantage points are applied in order from close to wide.

One step back: You + Me intersections. Ask what the combination of your tendencies and theirs is producing. The framing is not "what is wrong with you" but "what is the specific you-and-me configuration that creates the friction?" Observe the cycle: your reaction to their behavior produces a behavior in you that reinforces their behavior. Sandy copes with financial stress by spending; Gil copes by monitoring. Each one's coping intensifies the other's coping, producing a downward spiral. Neither is wrong in isolation; together they generate the problem. Locate your own part in the cycle before naming theirs.

Two steps back: Role clashes. Add the layer of the roles each person occupies. Ask whether the conflict would exist between strangers cast in these roles, regardless of personality. Two people at opposite ends of a rope doing their jobs are in a tug-of-war before they have said a word to each other. Common role tensions arise from structural design (compliance and trading, sales and legal, surgeons and anesthesiologists), from role confusion (three people who each believe they own task A, and no one who believes they own tasks B through D), and from role change (yesterday we were peers; today you are two levels above me). For each, ask: how much of the friction is role structure, and how much is the person? You cannot always answer, but asking changes the conversation.

Three steps back: The big picture. Add other players, processes, policies, the physical environment, timing and decision structures, and the coping strategies people have developed to work around all of the above. In the refinery injury example, the worker's behavior is one factor. The maintenance records, the overtime policy, the performance evaluation incentive, the shift-change communication protocol, and the training budget are also factors. In the Benny/Alice/Vince/board chain, Benny's late delivery is the presenting problem; the board's habit of changing requirements, Alice's failure to pass those changes on, and the absence of any honest accounting of how changes affect timelines are the structural contributors. Ask: what other players are shaping the choices I and the other person are making? What processes or structures are reinforcing the pattern?

Circling back to yourself. After all three steps, ask: what am I doing, or failing to do, that is contributing to this dynamic? This step returns the frame to something you can act on without waiting for the other person to change.

Using the system to create change. Once the contours of the system are visible, look for structural changes that do not require anyone to change their personality. Shift roles, revise processes, change the environment. The chapter notes that Me + Everybody patterns (feedback that recurs across relationships) are among the most tractable: when one person changes, multiple systems improve simultaneously.
sections_html.signal: You can draw the cycle: my behavior (A) produces their reaction (B), which reinforces my behavior (A). You can name one role factor that would produce friction between any two people in these roles, regardless of their characters. You can name at least one other player, process, or structure that is constraining the choices both of you are making. The feedback no longer feels like an accusation aimed only at you, nor like something you can dismiss by pointing at the other person. It is a system problem with multiple levers.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "whose fault is this?" If you answered without hesitation, you have not yet seen the system. A felt sense of clear, one-directional blame is the exact signal that stepping back is needed.

After: ask "what would need to change for this to get better?" If the answer is only "they would need to change," the system view has not yet landed. The skill has landed when you can name something you can change, something they could change, and at least one structural thing that could change, and all three feel genuine.
sections_html.failure: Without the system view, feedback conversations about recurring problems cycle through two competing accounts of who is really at fault. Each party genuinely sees the other's contribution and genuinely does not see their own. The cycle ends in stalemate, mutual resentment, and a coping strategy (Sandy hides the Grape-Nuts) that introduces a new set of problems. The chapter's label for the downstream version of this failure: "fixes that fail." When feedback is aimed at one component of the system and the other components are left unchanged, solutions produce new problems (Benny doubles his budget estimates; the board uses the extra time to pile on more requirements). The system generates the same outcome through a different path.
sections_html.example: The chapter opens with a snoring husband and a noise-sensitive wife. From inside the problem, each person sees only the other's contribution. The systems view at one step back shows an intersection: his snoring plus her sensitivity together produce the sleeplessness. Neither is "bad"; together they create the problem. The chapter's framing: "It takes the two of you being the way you are to create the problem. That's how systems work." At two steps back: if they also have a role dynamic (one partner has medical needs, one has a history of minimizing medical concerns), that adds to the tally. At three steps back: the white noise machine he bought is a coping strategy that failed because it was imposed rather than agreed on; that history now colors her refusal to try solutions he proposes.
sections_html.source: Chapter 6, "Identify the Relationship System," lines 2801-2814 (the systems insight, two core observations about why each person sees only the other's contribution), 2820-2866 (one step back: intersections, the Sandy/Gil downward spiral, and the father-in-law demonstration of stepping outside the system), 2872-2895 (two steps back: role clashes and accidental adversaries), 2901-2966 (three steps back: the big picture table with five factors), 2976-3011 (the Kenzie classroom example worked through all three steps plus broader structural factors), 3017-3057 (the three benefits framed as accuracy, reduced judgment, enhanced accountability), 3167-3188 (Me+Everybody patterns and using the system to support change). Stone and Heen treat the three-step framework as the chapter's organizing spine, with each step a wider view of the same system.
