name: try-small-experiments
description: You have received feedback that you are unsure about, or you are stuck between the status quo and a change that seems too large and final to risk. The chapter prescribes treating the feedback as an experiment rather than a decision, which breaks the paralysis.

sources.chapter: 12. Get Going (Five Ways to Take Action)

sections_html.when: You have received feedback and your analysis of pros and cons has not produced a conclusion. The status quo has home field advantage: you are going along reasonably well, the feedback asks you to do something unfamiliar, and you cannot see in advance whether the change would help. Or you are stuck because the change feels all-and-always: if I try this, I'm committing to it permanently and admitting I was wrong all along.
sections_html.move:
Reframe from decision to experiment. You are not choosing whether to change permanently. You are testing whether the change might help. This distinction removes the home field advantage of the status quo, because the question is no longer "should I change my whole approach?" but "is it worth finding out what this might do?" The chapter: "Experiment. Not because you know that it's right or you know it will help. But because it's possible it will help."

Try it on (the mental experiment). Before committing to any real-world change, run the experiment in your head. Harpreet received a student evaluation calling him "arrogant and condescending" and rejected it entirely. His department head suggested: "Try it on. Assume the student is on to something. Not because you already know it fits, but because it's a good way of finding out. If it doesn't, no worries. Take it off." Harpreet sat with the possibility and began to see what the student might have meant. He did not conclude the feedback was true, but found a perspective he could act on. The mental try-on is low-risk: if the feedback doesn't fit after a genuine attempt, you take it off. If it does, even in some small way, you have something to work on.

Try it out (the real-world experiment). Run the feedback as a time-limited behavioral test. Emily received five years of coaching to cut her twenty-minute introduction. She heard it as an all-and-always verdict and resisted. When she reframed it as a one-night experiment, she tried it. The result: some parts of the intro were dispensable, some were not, and five minutes with a handout at the end turned out to be better than twenty minutes with nothing. She would not have known this from analysis alone.

Reframe from all-and-always to some-and-sometimes. When the feedback asks you to "change your workshops entirely for the rest of time," that is the all-and-always frame, and the answer will always be no. When the question is "try it for one evening and see what you think," the answer becomes accessible. The chapter: "If the question is 'should I go to yoga for the rest of my life?' the answer will always be no. If it's 'should I try yoga for one morning and see what I think?' the costs drop dramatically."
sections_html.signal: You have defined a specific, time-limited experiment: what you will try, under what conditions, for how long, before deciding whether to continue. You have not committed to permanent change; you have committed to finding out. Or, in the mental version, you have spent genuine time trying to inhabit the feedback rather than dismissing it.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "am I stuck because I cannot commit to permanent change, or because I genuinely cannot see any upside to the change?" If the first, the all-and-always reframe applies. If the second, the try-it-on mental experiment is the first step.

After: ask "did I actually try it, or did I just think about trying it?" The experiment produces data only if it is actually run.
sections_html.failure: Without the experiment reframe, feedback that you are not sure about produces no action. The chapter's observation: "In any contest between change and the status quo, the status quo has home field advantage. All things being equal, we won't change." Analysis of pros and cons, in the absence of real experience, rarely produces a conclusion about things that are genuinely uncertain. Gawande hired a surgical coach not because he knew he needed one, but because the downside was small and the upside, though unclear, was worth exploring. His complication rates went down. He could not have concluded this from analysis. Emily's five-year resistance to a one-night experiment is the cost of treating an experiment as a decision.
sections_html.source: Chapter 12, "Get Going," lines 5748-5806 (the decision vs. experiment reframe: Emily's case, the yoga case, the status-quo home-field advantage), 5776-5791 (try it on: Harpreet's "try it on" experiment with the arrogant-and-condescending evaluation), 5797-5821 (try it out: the yoga case, Gawande and his surgical coach), 5827-5838 (it's not all-and-always: Emily's one-night test and what she learned).
sections_html.related_skills: • feedback/precommit-to-riding-the-j-curve — what to do after the experiment starts and you feel worse before better

name: understand-your-wiring-profile
description: You received feedback and your emotional reaction is stronger than the content seems to warrant, or you notice that feedback that would unsettle others bounces off you entirely. The chapter explains that these differences are built in and names three variables that describe your profile.

sources.chapter: 7. Learn How Wiring and Temperament Affect Your Story

sections_html.when:
You received feedback and your emotional reaction is dramatically larger or smaller than the feedback's content seems to warrant. A colleague who received the same feedback appears barely affected, or is devastated while you are unfazed. You find yourself judging your reaction as too sensitive, too brittle, or too dismissive. You believe your distressed interpretation of the feedback is the accurate one.

Also reach for this before entering a high-stakes feedback conversation, as preparation for what your nervous system is likely to do.
sections_html.move:
Name your profile on three dimensions. The point is not to diagnose yourself medically but to describe how your nervous system tends to respond so you can stop attributing your reaction entirely to the content of the feedback.

Baseline: Your default level of contentment or well-being, the level you return to over time after both positive and negative events. People with higher baselines get a stronger positive lift from good feedback and find negative feedback less emotionally sticky. People with lower baselines get a smaller boost from positive feedback and are hit harder by negative feedback. Ask: "Across my life, would people describe me as typically upbeat or typically more subdued?" The chapter's example: Alita's positive patient ratings give her only a brief lift; the critical comments land hard and stick.

Swing: How far you move emotionally from your baseline when feedback arrives, whether the input is positive or negative. High-swing people experience a strong physiological jolt even from comparatively minor inputs; low-swing people remain in a narrower emotional band. Ask: "When I receive unexpected negative feedback, do I tend to have an immediate, intense emotional reaction, or do I stay relatively even?" The chapter's example: Eliza is frantic from the same client message that Jeron reads as merely "a bit more work."

Sustain and recovery: How long you ride a positive boost and how quickly you return to baseline after being brought low. These operate independently. A fast recoverer from negative feedback is back to baseline quickly; a slow recoverer is still processing weeks later. A high-sustainer of positive feedback rides the boost for hours; a low-sustainer loses it in minutes. Ask: "After difficult feedback, how many days before I stop replaying the conversation? After a strong compliment, how long before the glow fades?" The chapter notes that recovery time differs by up to 3,000 percent across individuals.

What to do with the profile. Once you have named your profile, you have two uses for it.

First, calibrate how much of your current emotional reaction belongs to the feedback's content and how much is your nervous system doing what it was built to do. Alita's devastation at "sometimes runs late" is real, but a significant fraction of it is Alita's baseline-and-swing profile operating as designed, not evidence that the feedback is as catastrophic as it feels.

Second, manage your sustain deliberately. The chapter notes that you can extend positive sustain by recalling positive feedback during a low, deliberately replaying genuine praise to counterbalance a strong negative swing. This works because the same circuits that fire during the original positive experience fire during vivid recall.

The liberation framing. The chapter is explicit that wiring is not destiny and is not blame-worthy. It is also not fixed: meditation, exercise, and deliberate practice can raise baseline over time. What matters immediately is that labeling a strong reaction as "this is how I'm built" is more accurate than labeling it as "this is how terrible the feedback must be." Your reaction is not evidence about the feedback's content.
sections_html.signal: You can name your approximate profile (e.g., "moderate baseline, high swing, slow recovery"). When you notice a strong emotional reaction, you can say "part of this is my wiring" in the same sentence as "let me also take the feedback seriously." The two clauses are no longer contradictory.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "am I treating my current emotional state as the accurate read of what the feedback means?" If yes, and the reaction feels extremely strong, the wiring profile is likely shaping the story more than the content warrants.

After: ask "can I separate 'what they actually said' from 'how I happen to experience that kind of message'?" If yes, the wiring profile has been named and does not dominate the interpretation.
sections_html.failure: Without the wiring profile, people whose nervous systems respond strongly to negative feedback consistently over-weight the feedback's severity, while people whose nervous systems respond weakly consistently under-weight it. Alita interprets her devastation as evidence that the feedback is catastrophic; it is actually evidence that her sustain-recovery profile makes criticism sticky. Krista interprets her equanimity as evidence that the feedback is not important; it is actually evidence that her fast-recovery profile makes criticism slippery. Both are making the same error: using their emotional reaction as an accurate thermometer of the feedback's content rather than as a report on their nervous system. The chapter's framing: Alita's reactions are "not due to a lack of courage or surplus of self-pity" and Krista's are not evidence of genuine imperviousness. "Your reactions are not due to a lack of courage."
sections_html.example: Krista and Alita receive structurally similar feedback in analogous professional situations. Krista: her patients honk in support; the negatives she misses. Alita: her patients give glowing reviews; the negative comments about wait times render the entire survey toxic and sit unopened for two months. Same category of feedback, radically different emotional response. The chapter uses both cases to illustrate that the difference is not in the quality of the feedback or the character of the receiver; it is in their wiring profiles.
sections_html.source: Chapter 7, "Learn How Wiring and Temperament Affect Your Story," lines 3283-3292 (the liberation framing: wiring is real and non-judgmental), 3298-3417 (the three variables with Krista and Alita as worked cases throughout, the "bad is stronger than good" negativity bias, the four sustain/recovery combinations), 3434-3451 (wiring is not fixed; the 50-40-10 formula establishing the 40% interpretive zone). Stone and Heen use this chapter to build the conceptual vocabulary (baseline, swing, sustain/recovery) that underlies the distortion-dismantling work of the next chapter.

name: unpack-judgment-from-evaluation
description: You received evaluation feedback that cannot be sorted as coaching, and your identity has been triggered. The chapter's second practice is to break the evaluation into its three constituent parts (assessment, consequences, judgment) so you can identify which part is actually triggering you and handle each appropriately.

sources.chapter: 9. Cultivate a Growth Identity (Sort Toward Coaching)

sections_html.when: You received feedback that is genuinely evaluative: a grade, a rejection, a ranking, a termination. You cannot sort this as coaching because the evaluation is explicit and the stakes are real. Your identity has been triggered, but you are not sure whether it is the rating itself, the consequences, or what the evaluator seems to think of you that is hitting hardest.
sections_html.move:
Break the evaluation into three separate components and handle each on its own terms.

Assessment: where do you stand? The assessment is the data. At the track meet you ran the mile in five minutes, nineteen seconds, placing you fourth in the forty-to-forty-five age group. It is a number, a rank, a placement. The assessment is usually the least identity-loaded of the three components; it is a measurement. Ask: "Is this assessment accurate?" If you have concerns about the measurement itself (the methodology, the criteria, the data used), raise them. If the assessment seems accurate, accept it.

Consequences: what happens as a result? Consequences are the real-world outcomes that follow from the assessment. You qualify for regionals; you do not qualify for nationals. You did not get the promotion. Consequences can be certain or speculative, immediate or longer-term. Ask: "Are the consequences clear? Are they proportionate to the assessment?" Consequences deserve attention because they are often the locus of the most legitimate concern. Distinguish between consequences that are certain (you did not get the raise; there is a dollar amount) and consequences that are speculative (your spouse will leave you because of the raise). The chapter's caution: in the grip of upsetting feedback, we tend to process speculative consequences as if they were certain. Right-size the speculative ones (this belongs to contain-the-story).

Judgment: what story does the giver tell about the assessment and its consequences? Judgment is the interpretation. Your coach is disappointed by your time and thinks you should have done better. Your neighbor disapproves of your "home environment." Judgment is one person's reading of what the assessment means. It is not the assessment itself. Ask: "Whose judgment is this? Do they have information I don't have? Is there something to learn from their interpretation? Or is it one reading among multiple possible readings?"

Locate which component is triggering your identity. In most evaluations it is the judgment that hooks identity. The assessment (fourth place) is acceptable; the consequences (regionals, not nationals) are manageable; the coach's disappointment is what cuts. Once you have located the trigger, you can respond to it specifically. You may want to discuss why the coach's judgment differs from yours. Or you may decide that their judgment is one interpretation you can weigh and set aside. Either way, you are no longer responding to the whole evaluation as if it were a single undifferentiated blow.

The chapter: "Accurate assessment is valuable and the consequences are important to understand. Others' judgments? You may find certain judgments illuminating; other judgments you'll rightly dismiss. It's one person's interpretation, and you've got your own interpretation, thank you very much."
sections_html.signal: You can write three separate sentences: what was the assessment, what are the certain consequences, and what is the evaluator's judgment. The trigger is in one of those sentences, not spread across all three. You know which one.
sections_html.probe:
Before: ask "when I think about this evaluation, what exactly is hurting?" If the answer is "all of it" or "I'm not sure," the three components are merged and need unpacking.

After: ask "is the assessment fair? Are the consequences clear? Whose judgment am I reacting to, and what would it mean if I treated that judgment as one interpretation rather than a verdict?" If you can answer each question separately, the unpacking has worked.
sections_html.failure: Without unpacking, the whole evaluation lands as a single undifferentiated identity challenge. The assessment, the consequences, and the judgment arrive together and are processed together. The result is that you either accept or reject the whole package, which means you lose the parts worth accepting and preserve the parts worth contesting in the same move. A receiver who cannot separate judgment from assessment may reject an accurate assessment to protect against an unfair judgment. Or may accept a harsh judgment because the assessment is accurate. Unpacking allows the three components to be handled appropriately rather than as a single all-or-nothing verdict.
sections_html.example: The chapter's track meet case: the assessment (fifth minute, nineteen seconds, fourth place) is objective. The consequences (regionals yes, nationals no) are specified by the rules. The coach's judgment (disappointed, thinks you should have done better) is one interpretation among possible others: you are delighted by the time because it beats your morning expectation. The identity trigger is the judgment, not the assessment or the consequences. Locating this allows you to respond specifically: why does the coach have a different judgment about your time? Is there something to learn from that discrepancy?
sections_html.source: Chapter 9, "Cultivate a Growth Identity," lines 4401-4419. Stone and Heen name this "Practice #2," "Unpack Judgment from the Evaluation Suitcase." The track meet example is the worked illustration.
sections_html.related_skills:
• feedback/sort-toward-coaching — the prior practice; sort-toward-coaching applies when coaching might be hiding inside the evaluation; this skill applies when the evaluation is genuine and needs to be handled on its own terms
  • feedback/give-yourself-a-second-score — the follow-on practice; once the first score is understood at actual size, the second score is about how you handle it

name: use-your-reaction-as-blind-spot-alert
description: You have just received feedback that does not match how you see yourself, and your mind has started building a case against the giver. You are working out their agenda, their hidden motives, their projection, their personality issue, the office politics they must be playing. You are about to use the case-against-the-giver to file the feedback as wrong.

sections_html.when: The feedback feels off. You can hear it, you can repeat it, and you can already tell the giver is wrong about you. As you sit with it, your mind is not idle. It is constructing an explanation for why this person, specifically, would say something so off base. They must have an agenda. They must be reading their own stuff into you. They must have a personality quirk that warps how they read others. Reach for this skill the moment you notice that the explanation you are constructing is about them, not about the feedback.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice the thought form. The chapter names the specific cognitive moves that signal you are in the trap. "What was their agenda?" "What's wrong with them?" "Why are they really saying this?" "Are they projecting?" Annabelle's version, when she received the second 360, was to wonder whether her subordinates were playing politics or shadowboxing absent parents. The chapter is unsparing here: this is not unusual reasoning under triggered conditions. It is the predictable shape the mind takes when a piece of feedback contradicts the self.
  2. Treat that thought as the alarm, not the analysis. The chapter's structural claim is that looking harder at yourself does not help, because what you will see when you look harder is that the feedback is wrong and the giver must have an agenda. The verdict of "they are off" is the receiver-side terminus of the very same Gap Map gap the chapter is trying to surface. So when the agenda thought arrives, do not act on it. Use it. Make the next thought a deliberate one: "I wonder if this feedback is sitting in my blind spot."
  3. Hold both possibilities open. The skill is not "the feedback must be right." It is "I cannot tell yet, and the feeling that it must be wrong is not, by itself, evidence." Leave the question open long enough to do the next moves the chapter prescribes (look for patterns, ask a friend who will be an honest mirror, ask the giver "how am I getting in my own way"). The agenda story might be true. It usually is not the whole story.
  4. Notice the symmetry. The chapter points out that your reaction to wrong feedback runs the same Gap Map in reverse: you upset, you assume intent, you conclude something is wrong with them. Knowing the loop is bidirectional makes it easier to hold the agenda thought as a hypothesis instead of a finding.
sections_html.signal: You can name, in one sentence, the specific pathologizing thought your mind just produced about the giver ("I'm wondering whether her review is really about her own boss"). And you can name, in a second sentence, the question you have substituted for it ("which means I should treat this as possibly sitting in my blind spot, and check"). The case-against-the-giver is on hold. The next move you make is one of the chapter's checking moves, not the dismissal.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "When I think about why this feedback is wrong, am I describing the feedback's content, or am I describing the giver?" If the answer is the giver (their agenda, their character, their issue), the alarm is sounding.

After applying, ask the same. The skill landed if you have moved from "they're off" to "I cannot yet tell, and here is the move I am making to find out." A good test: you can identify the specific blind-spot-checking action you are now taking, and you can articulate that the agenda story might still turn out to be true, but it is no longer your conclusion.
sections_html.failure: Without this skill, the receiver mistakes the most predictable possible response (the agenda story) for a careful judgment about the giver. The chapter is explicit that "looking harder" produces the same dismissal verdict, because the dismissal is the asymmetric output of a system that gives you full access to your intentions and almost no access to your behavior. The giver becomes the problem. The conversation closes. The feedback that was actually trying to surface a leaky face, a leaky tone, or a pattern your friends and exes have all noticed gets filed alongside the dismissed others. Six months or three years later, the same feedback arrives again from a different giver, and the receiver is shocked at the consistency, because each giver was processed individually as having their own peculiar agenda. Annabelle's second 360, three years after the first with the same theme, is the chapter's worked case of exactly this failure.
sections_html.example: The chapter's case is Annabelle, who receives a second 360 in three years naming her as difficult, impatient, and disrespectful. Her mind goes immediately to the candidate explanations the chapter names: maybe her subordinates are playing politics, maybe they enjoy taking anonymous shots at the boss, maybe it is projection, maybe they are working through unrelated developmental issues with an authority figure. The chapter then says: Annabelle is right that something else is going on, but it is not any of those things. It is that her actual feelings (impatience and contempt under pressure) are leaking through her face and tone, and her colleagues are reading the leak accurately. The agenda story was the predictable response, but it was not the truth of the situation. The skill installs the move Annabelle did not make: when the agenda thought arrives, treat it as the alarm that the feedback may be exactly where the chapter says people are blindest, and run the checking moves before drawing a conclusion.
sections_html.source: Chapter 4, "See Your Blind Spots," lines 2003-2012, the section "Use Your Reaction as a Blind-Spot Alert." Stone and Heen open the broader "What Helps Us See Our Blind Spots?" section by ruling out the obvious move (look harder), explain why looking harder produces the wrong-feedback verdict, and prescribe the inversion: when you notice the agenda or personality-disorder thought, make sure your next thought is the blind-spot question. The skill installs the cognitive trigger and the substitution.
